The neocortex is responsible for all of our rational and analytical thought and language. The middle two sections make up our limbic brains,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 與黃金圈完美相關。我們最新的大腦，我們的智人大腦，對應於「什麼」層次。新皮質負責我們所有的理性和分析性思考和語言。中間的兩個部分組成了我們的邊緣腦，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: and our limbic brains are responsible for all of our feelings, It's also responsible for all human behavior, all decision-making, and it has no capacity for language. In other words, when we communicate from the outside in, yes, people can understand vast amounts of complicated information
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 我們的邊緣腦負責我們所有的感覺，它也負責所有人類的行為，所有的決策，但它沒有語言能力。換句話說，當我們從外向內交流時，是的，人們可以理解大量複雜的訊息

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: It just doesn't drive behavior. When we can communicate from the inside out, we're talking directly to the part of the brain that controls behavior, and then we allow people to rationalize it with the tangible things we say and do. This is where gut decisions come from.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 它只是不會驅動行為。當我們能夠由內而外地溝通時，我們就直接與大腦中控制行為的部分交談，然後我們讓人們用我們所說和所做的有形的事情來合理化它。這就是直覺決定的來源。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: Sometimes you can give somebody all the facts and figures, and they say, "I know what all the facts and details say, but it just doesn't feel right." Why would we use that verb, it doesn't "feel" right? Because the part of the brain that controls decision-making doesn't control language.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 有時，你可以向某人提供所有事實和數據，他們會說：「我知道所有事實和細節的意思，但就是感覺不對。」為什麼我們要使用那個動詞，它「感覺」不對？因為大腦中控制決策的部分並不控制語言。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: The best we can muster up is, "I don't know.  It just doesn't feel right." Or sometimes you say you're leading with your heart or soul. I hate to break it to you, those aren't other body parts controlling your behavior. It's all happening here in your limbic brain,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 我們最多只能說：「我不知道。只是感覺不對。」或者有時你說你是用心或靈魂來領導的。我不想告訴你，那些不是控制你行為的其他身體部位。這一切都發生在你的大腦邊緣

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: the part of the brain that controls decision-making and not language. But if you don't know why you do what you do, and people respond to why you do what you do, then how will you ever get people to vote for you, or buy something from you, or, more importantly, be loyal
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 大腦中控制決策而不是語言的部分。但是，如果你不知道你為什麼做你所做的事情，而人們對你為什麼做你所做的事情做出回應，那麼你如何讓人們投票給你，或者從你那裡購買東西，或者更重要的是，保持忠誠

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: and want to be a part of what it is that you do. The goal is not just to sell to people who need what you have; the goal is to sell to people who believe what you believe. The goal is not just to hire people who need a job; it's to hire people who believe what you believe.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 並希望成為您所做工作的一部分。我們的目標不只是把你所擁有的產品賣給需要的人；目標是把產品賣給那些和你有相同信念的人。我們的目標不僅是僱用需要工作的人；就是僱用那些相信你所相信的人。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: I always say that, you know, if you hire people just because they can do a job, they'll work for your money, but if they believe what you believe, they'll work for you with blood and sweat and tears. Nowhere else is there a better example than with the Wright brothers.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 我總是說，你知道，如果你僱用員工只是因為他們能勝任工作，他們就會為你的錢而工作，但如果他們相信你所相信的，他們就會用血、汗和淚水為你工作。沒有比萊特兄弟更好的例子了。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: Most people don't know about Samuel Pierpont Langley. And back in the early 20th century, the pursuit of powered man flight was like the dot com of the day. Everybody was trying it. And Samuel Pierpont Langley had, what we assume, to be the recipe for success. Even now, you ask people,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 大多數人不知道塞繆爾·皮爾龐特·蘭利。早在 20 世紀初，對載人動力飛行的追求就像當時的網路一樣。每個人都在嘗試。我們認為，塞繆爾·皮爾龐特·蘭利是成功的秘訣。即使現在，你問人們，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: "Why did your product or why did your company fail?" and people always give you the same permutation of the same three things: under-capitalized, the wrong people, bad market conditions. It's always the same three things, so let's explore that. Samuel Pierpont Langley
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 「為什麼你的產品或你的公司失敗了？」人們總是給你同樣的三件事的相同排列：資本不足、錯誤的人、糟糕的市場狀況。這三件事總是相同的，所以讓我們來探討一下。塞繆爾·皮爾龐特·蘭利

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: was given 50,000 dollars by the War Department to figure out this flying machine. Money was no problem. He held a seat at Harvard and worked at the Smithsonian and was extremely well-connected; he knew all the big minds of the day. He hired the best minds money could find
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 美國陸軍部撥款 5 萬美元來研發這種飛行器。錢沒問題。他曾在哈佛大學任職，並在史密森學會工作，人脈非常廣。他了解當時所有的大人物。他聘請了金錢所能找到的最優秀的人才

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: and the market conditions were fantastic. The New York Times followed him around everywhere, and everyone was rooting for Langley. Then how come we've never heard of Samuel Pierpont Langley? A few hundred miles away in Dayton, Ohio, Orville and Wilbur Wright,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 市場狀況非常好。 《紐約時報》到處跟蹤他，每個人都支持蘭利。那我們為什麼從未聽過塞繆爾·皮爾龐特·蘭利呢？幾百英里外的俄亥俄州代頓，奧維爾·賴特和威爾伯·賴特，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: they had none of what we consider to be the recipe for success. They had no money; they paid for their dream with the proceeds from their bicycle shop. Not a single person on the Wright brothers' team had a college education, not even Orville or Wilbur.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他們沒有我們認為的成功秘訣。他們沒有錢；他們用自行車店的收益來實現自己的夢想。萊特兄弟團隊中沒有一個人受過大學教育，甚至連奧維爾和威爾伯也沒有。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: And The New York Times followed them around nowhere. The difference was, Orville and Wilbur were driven by a cause, by a purpose, by a belief. They believed that if they could figure out this flying machine, it'll change the course of the world. Samuel Pierpont Langley was different.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 《紐約時報》也無處追蹤他們。不同之處在於，奧維爾和威爾伯是被一個事業、一個目的、一個信念所驅動。他們相信，如果他們能研發出這種飛行器，它將改變世界的進程。塞繆爾·皮爾龐特·蘭利則不同。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: He wanted to be rich, and he wanted to be famous. He was in pursuit of the result. He was in pursuit of the riches. And lo and behold, look what happened. The people who believed in the Wright brothers' dream worked with them with blood and sweat and tears. The others just worked for the paycheck.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他想要變得富有，他想要出名。他追求的是結果。他追求的是財富。你瞧，看看發生了什麼事。相信萊特兄弟夢想的人們與他們一起付出了血汗和淚水。其他人只是為了薪水而工作。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: They tell stories of how every time the Wright brothers went out, they would have to take five sets of parts, because that's how many times they would crash before supper. And, eventually, on December 17th, 1903, the Wright brothers took flight, and no one was there to even experience it.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他們講述了萊特兄弟每次外出時都必須帶上五套零件的故事，因為這就是他們晚餐前墜毀的次數。最終，1903 年 12 月 17 日，萊特兄弟成功起飛，但沒有人親眼目睹。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: We found out about it a few days later. And further proof that Langley was motivated by the wrong thing: the day the Wright brothers took flight, He could have said, "That's an amazing discovery, guys, and I will improve upon your technology," but he didn't.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 幾天後我們才知道這件事。進一步證明蘭利的動機是錯的：萊特兄弟起飛的那天，他本可以說，「這是一個驚人的發現，夥計們，我會改進你們的技術，」但他沒有。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: He wasn't first, he didn't get rich, he didn't get famous, so he quit. People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it. If you talk about what you believe, you will attract those who believe what you believe. But why is it important to attract those who believe what you believe?
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他不是第一，他沒有致富，他沒有出名，所以他退出了。人們不買你做的事，而是買你做的事。他們購買你這樣做的理由。如果你談論你的信仰，你就會吸引那些相信你信仰的人。但為什麼吸引那些相信你所信仰的人很重要呢？

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: Something called the law of diffusion of innovation, if you don't know the law, you know the terminology. The first 2.5% of our population are our innovators. The next 13.5% of our population are our early adopters. The next 34% are your early majority, your late majority and your laggards.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 有一種叫做創新擴散定律的東西，如果你不知道這個定律，你就知道這個名詞。我們人口中的前 2.5% 是我們的創新者。接下來 13.5% 的人口是我們的早期採用者。接下來的 34% 是早期多數、晚期多數和落後者。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: The only reason these people buy touch-tone phones is because you can't buy rotary phones anymore. We all sit at various places at various times on this scale, but what the law of diffusion of innovation tells us is that if you want mass-market success or mass-market acceptance of an idea,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 這些人購買按鍵式電話的唯一原因是因為你不能再買旋轉式電話了。在這種規模上，我們在不同的時間坐在不同的地方，但創新擴散定律告訴我們，如果你想要大眾市場的成功或大眾市場接受一個想法，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: you cannot have it until you achieve this tipping point between 15 and 18 percent market penetration, and then the system tips. I love asking businesses, "What's your conversion on new business?" They love to tell you, "It's about 10 percent," proudly. Well, you can trip over 10% of the customers.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 直到你達到 15% 到 18% 的市場滲透率這個臨界點，然後系統才會提示你，你才能擁有它。我喜歡問企業：「你們的新業務轉換率是多少？」他們喜歡自豪地告訴你，「大約是百分之十」。嗯，你可能會絆倒超過 10% 的顧客。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: We all have about 10% who just "get it." That's how we describe them, right? That's like that gut feeling, "Oh, they just get it." The problem is: How do you find the ones that get it before doing business versus the ones who don't get it? So it's this here, this little gap that you have to close,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 我們都有大約 10% 的人只是「明白了」。我們就是這樣描述它們的，對嗎？這就像直覺，「哦，他們明白了。」問題是：如何找到在開展業務之前就獲得了它的人和沒有獲得它的人？所以這就是這裡，你必須彌補的這個小差距，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: as Jeffrey Moore calls it, "Crossing the Chasm" -- because, you see, the early majority will not try something until someone else has tried it first. And these guys, the innovators and the early adopters, they're comfortable making those gut decisions.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 正如傑弗裡摩爾所說，「跨越鴻溝」——因為，你看，早期的大多數人在其他人先嘗試過之前不會嘗試某件事。這些人，創新者和早期採用者，他們很樂意做出這些直覺決定。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: They're more comfortable making those intuitive decisions that are driven by what they believe about the world and not just what product is available. These are the people who stood in line for six hours to buy an iPhone when they first came out,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他們更願意根據自己對世界的看法而做出直觀的決定，而不僅僅是可用的產品。這些人是在 iPhone 剛上市時排隊六小時購買的人，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: when you could have bought one off the shelf the next week. These are the people who spent 40,000 dollars on flat-screen TVs when they first came out, even though the technology was substandard. And, by the way, they didn't do it because the technology was so great; they did it for themselves.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 下週你就可以買一個現成的。這些人在平板電視剛問世時就花了 4 萬美元購買，儘管技術並不合格。順便說一句，他們這樣做並不是因為技術太先進了；而是因為技術太先進了。他們是為自己做的。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: It's because they wanted to be first. People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it and what you do simply proves what you believe. In fact, people will do the things that prove what they believe. The reason that person bought the iPhone in the first six hours, stood in line for six hours,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 這是因為他們想成為第一名。人們不買你做的事，而是買你做的事。他們相信你這樣做的原因，而你所做的只是證明了你的信念。事實上，人們會做一些事情來證明他們所相信的事情。之所以有人在前六小時內買了iPhone，排了六個小時的隊，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: was because of what they believed about the world, and how they wanted everybody to see them: they were first. People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it. So let me give you a famous example, a famous failure and a famous success of the law of diffusion of innovation.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 是因為他們對世界的信念，以及他們希望每個人如何看待他們：他們是第一。人們不買你做的事，而是買你做的事。他們購買你這樣做的理由。那麼，讓我給你們一個著名的例子，創新擴散定律的一個著名的失敗和一個著名的成功。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: First, the famous failure. It's a commercial example. As we said before, the recipe for success is money and the right people and the right market conditions. You should have success then. From the time TiVo came out about eight or nine years ago to this current day,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 首先是著名的失敗。這是一個商業範例。正如我們之前所說，成功的秘訣是金錢、合適的人才和合適的市場條件。那你應該會成功。從大約八、九年前TiVo問世到現在，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: they are the single highest-quality product on the market, hands down, there is no dispute. They were extremely well-funded. Market conditions were fantastic. I mean, we use TiVo as verb. I TiVo stuff on my piece-of-junk Time Warner DVR all the time. But TiVo's a commercial failure.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 它們是市場上品質最高的產品，毫無爭議。他們的資金非常充足。市場狀況非常好。我的意思是，我們使用 TiVo 作為動詞。我一直在我的垃圾時代華納 DVR 上播放 TiVo 內容。但 TiVo 在商業上是失敗的。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: They've never made money. And when they went IPO, their stock was at about 30 or 40 dollars and then plummeted, and it's never traded above 10. In fact, I don't think it's even traded above six, except for a couple of little spikes. Because you see, when TiVo launched their product,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他們從來沒有賺過錢。當他們進行 IPO 時，他們的股票價格約為 30 或 40 美元，然後暴跌，並且其交易價格從未超過 10 美元。事實上，我認為除了幾次小高峰外，其交易價格甚至沒有超過 6 美元。因為你看，當 TiVo 推出他們的產品時，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: they told us all what they had. They said, "We have a product that pauses live TV, skips commercials, rewinds live TV and memorizes your viewing habits without you even asking." And the cynical majority said, "We don't believe you. We don't need it. We don't like it. You're scaring us."
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他們告訴了我們他們所擁有的一切。他們說：“我們有一款產品可以暫停直播電視、跳過廣告、倒帶直播電視並記住您的觀看習慣，甚至無需您詢問。”憤世嫉俗的大多數人說：“我們不相信你。我們不需要它。我們不喜歡它。你嚇到我們了。”

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: What if they had said, "If you're the kind of person who likes to have total control over every aspect of your life, boy, do we have a product for you. It pauses live TV, skips commercials, memorizes your viewing habits, etc., etc." People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 如果他們說：「如果你是那種喜歡完全控制生活各個方面的人，孩子，我們有一款適合你的產品嗎？它可以暫停電視直播、跳過廣告、記住你的觀看習慣等等。」人們不買你做的事，而是買你做的事。他們購買你為什麼這樣做，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: and what you do simply serves as the proof of what you believe. Now let me give you a successful example of the law of diffusion of innovation. In the summer of 1963, 250,000 people showed up on the mall in Washington to hear Dr. King speak. They sent out no invitations,
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 而你所做的只是證明你所相信的。現在讓我給大家舉一個創新擴散定律的成功例子。 1963 年夏天，25 萬人出現在華盛頓的購物中心聆聽金恩博士的演講。他們沒有發出邀請，

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: and there was no website to check the date. How do you do that? Well, Dr. King wasn't the only man in America who was a great orator. He wasn't the only man in America who suffered in a pre-civil rights America. In fact, some of his ideas were bad. But he had a gift.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 而且沒有網站可以查日期。你是怎麼做到的？嗯，金恩博士並不是美國唯一一位偉大的演說家。他並不是唯一一個在民權運動前的美國遭受苦難的人。事實上，他的一些想法是不好的。但他有天賦。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: He didn't go around telling people what needed to change in America. He went around and told people what he believed. "I believe, I believe, I believe," he told people. And people who believed what he believed took his cause, and they made it their own, and they told people.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 他沒有到處告訴人們美國需要改變什麼。他四處走動，告訴人們他的信仰。 「我相信，我相信，我相信，」他告訴人們。那些相信他所信仰的人接受了他的事業，並將其變成自己的事業，並告訴人們。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: And some of those people created structures to get the word out to even more people. And lo and behold, 250,000 people showed up on the right day at the right time to hear him speak. How many of them showed up for him? They showed up for themselves. It's what they believed about America
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 其中一些人創建了一些結構來向更多人傳播訊息。你瞧，有 25 萬人在正確的日子、正確的時間出現聆聽他的演講。有多少人為他出現？他們為自己而出現。這就是他們對美國的信念

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: that got them to travel in a bus for eight hours to stand in the sun in Washington in the middle of August. It's what they believed, and it wasn't about black versus white: 25% of the audience was white. Dr. King believed that there are two types of laws in this world:
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 這讓他們在八月中旬乘坐巴士八個小時來到華盛頓站在陽光下。這就是他們所相信的，而且這與黑人與白人無關：25% 的觀眾是白人。金恩博士認為，這個世界上有兩種法律：

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: those that are made by a higher authority and those that are made by men. And not until all the laws that are made by men are consistent with the laws made by the higher authority will we live in a just world. It just so happened that the Civil Rights Movement
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 那些是由更高權威製造的，那些是由人製造的。只有當人類制定的所有法律都與更高權威制定的法律一致時，我們才會生活在一個公正的世界。碰巧民權運動

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: was the perfect thing to help him bring his cause to life. We followed, not for him, but for ourselves. By the way, he gave the "I have a dream" speech, not the "I have a plan" speech. Listen to politicians now, with their comprehensive 12-point plans. They're not inspiring anybody.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 是幫助他實現自己事業的完美選擇。我們跟隨，不是為了他，而是為了我們自己。順便說一句，他發表的是「我有一個夢想」的演講，而不是「我有一個計劃」的演講。現在就聽聽政客們的意見，看看他們 12 點的全面計畫。他們沒有激勵任何人。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: Because there are leaders and there are those who lead. Leaders hold a position of power or authority, but those who lead inspire us. Whether they're individuals or organizations, we follow those who lead, not because we have to, but because we want to.
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 因為有領導者，也有領導者。領導者擁有權力或權威，但領導者會激勵我們。無論他們是個人還是組織，我們都會跟隨那些領導者，不是因為我們必須這樣做，而是因為我們願意這樣做。

amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.en: We follow those who lead, not for them, but for ourselves. And it's those who start with "why" that have the ability to inspire those around them or find others who inspire them. Thank you very much. Notifications alt+T
amy_cuddy_your_body_language_may_shape_w.zh: 我們追隨那些領導者，不是為了他們，而是為了自己。那些從「為什麼」開始的人有能力激勵周圍的人或找到其他激勵他們的人。非常感謝。通知 alt+T

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So I want to start by offering you a free no-tech life hack, and all it requires of you is this: that you change your posture for two minutes. But before I give it away, I want to ask you to right now do a little audit of your body and what you're doing with your body.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 因此，我想首先為您提供一個免費的無技術生活竅門，它所要求的只是：改變姿勢兩分鐘。但在我透露之前，我想請您現在對您的身體以及您對身體所做的事情進行一些檢查。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So how many of you are sort of making yourselves smaller? Maybe you're hunching, crossing your legs, maybe wrapping your ankles. Sometimes we hold onto our arms like this. Sometimes we spread out. (Laughter) So I want you to pay attention to what you're doing right now.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 那麼，你們當中有多少人在某種程度上讓自己變得更小呢？也許你彎腰駝背，交叉雙腿，也許包裹腳踝。有時我們會這樣握住手臂。有時我們會分散開來。 （笑聲）所以我希望你注意你現在正在做的事情。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: We're going to come back to that in a few minutes, and I'm hoping that if you learn to tweak this a little bit, it could significantly change the way your life unfolds. So, we're really fascinated with body language, and we're particularly interested in other people's body language.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我們將在幾分鐘後回到這個問題，我希望如果你學會稍微調整一下，它可以顯著改變你的生活展開方式。所以，我們真的對肢體語言著迷，對其他人的肢體語言特別感興趣。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: You know, we're interested in, like, you know — (Laughter) — an awkward interaction, or a smile, or a contemptuous glance, or maybe a very awkward wink, or maybe even something like a handshake. Narrator: Here they are arriving at Number 10.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你知道，我們感興趣的是，例如，你知道——（笑聲）——尷尬的互動，或者一個微笑，或者一個輕蔑的眼神，或者一個非常尷尬的眨眼，或者甚至是握手之類的東西。旁白：他們來到了10號。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: This lucky policeman gets to shake hands with the President of the United States. Here comes the Prime Minister -- No. (Laughter) (Applause) (Laughter) (Applause) Amy Cuddy: So a handshake, or the lack of a handshake, can have us talking for weeks and weeks and weeks.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 這位幸運的警察能夠與美國總統握手。總理來了——不。 （笑聲）（掌聲）（笑聲）（掌聲） Amy Cuddy：所以一次握手，或是不握手，都會讓我們聊上幾週、幾週、幾週。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Even the BBC and The New York Times. So obviously when we think about nonverbal behavior, or body language -- but we call it nonverbals as social scientists -- it's language, so we think about communication. When we think about communication, we think about interactions.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 甚至 BBC 和紐約時報。顯然，當我們考慮非語言行為或肢體語言時——但我們作為社會科學家稱之為非語言——它是語言，所以我們考慮的是溝通。當我們考慮溝通時，我們會考慮互動。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So what is your body language communicating to me? What's mine communicating to you? And there's a lot of reason to believe that this is a valid way to look at this. So social scientists have spent a lot of time looking at the effects of our body language,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 那麼你的肢體語言在傳達什麼訊息給我呢？我在跟你傳達什麼訊息？有很多理由相信這是看待這個問題的有效方法。所以社會科學家花了很多時間研究我們肢體語言的影響，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: or other people's body language, on judgments. And we make sweeping judgments and inferences from body language. And those judgments can predict really meaningful life outcomes For example, Nalini Ambady, a researcher at Tufts University, shows that when people watch 30-second soundless clips
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 或是其他人的肢體語言，來進行判斷。我們根據肢體語言做出全面的判斷和推論。這些判斷可以預測真正有意義的生活結果 例如，塔夫茨大學的研究員 Nalini Ambady 表明，當人們觀看 30 秒的無聲剪輯時

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: of real physician-patient interactions, their judgments of the physician's niceness predict whether or not that physician will be sued. So it doesn't have to do so much with whether or not that physician was incompetent, but do we like that person and how they interacted?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 在真實的醫病互動中，他們對醫生友善的判斷可以預測該醫生是否會被起訴。因此，這與該醫生是否無能沒有太大關係，但我們是否喜歡那個人以及他們如何互動？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Even more dramatic, Alex Todorov at Princeton has shown us that judgments of political candidates' faces in just one second predict 70 percent of U.S. Senate and gubernatorial race outcomes, and even, let's go digital, emoticons used well in online negotiations
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 更戲劇性的是，普林斯頓大學的亞歷克斯·托多羅夫（Alex Todorov）向我們展示了，僅僅一秒鐘對政治候選人面孔的判斷就可以預測美國參議院和州長競選結果的 70%，甚至，讓我們走向數字化，在線談判中廣泛使用的表情符號

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: can lead you to claim more value from that negotiation. If you use them poorly, bad idea. Right? So when we think of nonverbals, we think of how we judge others, how they judge us and what the outcomes are. We tend to forget, though, the other audience
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 可以引導您從談判中獲得更多價值。如果你使用不當，那是個壞主意。正確的？因此，當我們想到非語言時，我們會想到我們如何評價他人，他們如何評價我們以及結果是什麼。然而，我們往往會忘記其他觀眾

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: that's influenced by our nonverbals, and that's ourselves. We are also influenced by our nonverbals, our thoughts and our feelings and our physiology. So what nonverbals am I talking about? I'm a social psychologist. I study prejudice, and I teach at a competitive business school,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 這是受到我們的非語言行為的影響，那就是我們自己。我們也受到我們的非語言、我們的思想、我們的感受和我們的生理的影響。那我在談論什麼非語言呢？我是一名社會心理學家。我研究偏見，在一所競爭激烈的商學院任教，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: so it was inevitable that I would become interested in power dynamics. I became especially interested in nonverbal expressions of power and dominance. And what are nonverbal expressions of power and dominance? Well, this is what they are. So in the animal kingdom, they are about expanding.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以我不可避免地會對權力動力學產生興趣。我對權力和統治力的非語言表達特別感興趣。什麼是權力和統治力的非語言表達？嗯，這就是他們的本質。所以在動物王國裡，它們正在擴張。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So you make yourself big, you stretch out, you take up space, you're basically opening up. It's about opening up. And this is true across the animal kingdom. It's not just limited to primates. And humans do the same thing. (Laughter) So they do this both when they have power sort of chronically,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以你讓自己變得更大，你伸展，你佔據空間，你基本上是在開放。這是關於開放。在整個動物王國都是如此。它不僅限於靈長類動物。人類也做同樣的事情。 （笑聲）所以當他們長期擁有權力時他們都會這樣做，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and also when they're feeling powerful in the moment. And this one is especially interesting because it really shows us how universal and old these expressions of power are. This expression, which is known as pride, Jessica Tracy has studied. She shows that people who are born with sight
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 當他們此刻感覺強大時也是如此。這一點特別有趣，因為它確實向我們展示了這些權力表達方式是多麼普遍和古老。傑西卡·特蕾西研究過這種被稱為驕傲的表達方式。她表明，生來就有視力的人

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and people who are congenitally blind do this when they win at a physical competition. So when they cross the finish line and they've won, it doesn't matter if they've never seen anyone do it. So the arms up in the V, the chin is slightly lifted. What do we do when we feel powerless?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 先天失明的人在體育比賽中獲勝時也會這樣做。因此，當他們衝過終點線並獲勝時，即使他們從未見過有人這樣做也沒關係。所以雙臂向上呈V字形，下巴微微抬起。當我們感到無能為力時，我們該怎麼辦？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: We do exactly the opposite. We close up. We wrap ourselves up. We make ourselves small. We don't want to bump into the person next to us. So again, both animals and humans do the same thing. And this is what happens when you put together high and low power.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我們的做法恰恰相反。我們關閉。我們把自己包裹起來。我们让自己变得渺小。我们不想撞到旁边的人。同样，动物和人类都做同样的事情。這就是當你將高功率和低功率放在一起時會發生的情況。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So what we tend to do when it comes to power is that we complement the other's nonverbals. So if someone is being really powerful with us, we tend to make ourselves smaller. We don't mirror them. We do the opposite of them. So I'm watching this behavior in the classroom, and what do I notice?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 因此，當涉及到權力時，我們傾向於做的就是補充對方的非語言能力。因此，如果有人對我們非常強大，我們會傾向於讓自己變得更渺小。我們不鏡像他們。我們做的與他們相反。那麼我在教室裡觀察這種行為，我注意到了什麼？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: I notice that MBA students really exhibit the full range of power nonverbals. So you have people who are like caricatures of alphas, really coming into the room, they get right into the middle of the room before class even starts, like they really want to occupy space.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我注意到 MBA 學生確實展現了全方位的非語言能力。所以有些人就像漫畫中的阿爾法，真正進入房間，他們甚至在上課之前就進入房間的中間，就像他們真的想佔據空間一樣。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: When they sit down, they're sort of spread out. They raise their hands like this. You have other people who are virtually collapsing when they come in. As soon they come in, you see it. You see it on their faces and their bodies, and they sit in their chair and they make themselves tiny,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 當他們坐下時，他們會分散開來。他們像這樣舉起手來。還有一些人進來時幾乎崩潰了。他們一進來，你就看到了。你可以在他們的臉上和身體上看到這一點，他們坐在椅子上，讓自己變得很小，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and they go like this when they raise their hand. I notice a couple of things about this. One, you're not going to be surprised. It seems to be related to gender. So women are much more likely to do this kind of thing than men. Women feel chronically less powerful than men, so this is not surprising.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 當他們舉手時，他們就會這樣走。我注意到有關於此的一些事情。第一，你不會感到驚訝。這似乎與性別有關。所以女性比男性更容易做這種事。女性長期感覺不如男性強大，所以這並不奇怪。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: But the other thing I noticed is that it also seemed to be related to the extent to which the students were participating, and how well they were participating. And this is really important in the MBA classroom, because participation counts for half the grade.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 但我注意到的另一件事是，這似乎也與學生參與的程度以及參與的程度有關。這在 MBA 課堂上非常重要，因為參與度佔成績的一半。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So business schools have been struggling with this gender grade gap. You get these equally qualified women and men coming in and then you get these differences in grades, and it seems to be partly attributable to participation. So I started to wonder, you know, okay,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 因此，商學院一直在努力解決這種性別成績差距。你讓這些同等資格的女性和男性進來，然後你會發現成績上的差異，這似乎部分歸因於參與。所以我開始想知道，你知道，好吧，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: so you have these people coming in like this, and they're participating. Is it possible that we could get people to fake it and would it lead them to participate more? So my main collaborator Dana Carney, who's at Berkeley, and I really wanted to know, can you fake it till you make it?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以你讓這些人這樣進來，他們正在參與。我們是否有可能讓人們偽造它並會導致他們更多地參與？所以我的主要合作者達納·卡尼（Dana Carney）在伯克利大學，我真的很想知道，你能假裝它直到你成功嗎？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and actually experience a behavioral outcome that makes you seem more powerful? So we know that our nonverbals govern how other people think and feel about us. There's a lot of evidence. But our question really was, do our nonverbals govern how we think and feel about ourselves?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 並實際體驗到讓你看起來更強大的行為結果？所以我們知道，我們的非語言行為決定著其他人對我們的看法和感受。有很多證據。但我們真正的問題是，我們的非語言行為是否決定了我們對自己的思考與感受？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: There's some evidence that they do. So, for example, we smile when we feel happy, but also, when we're forced to smile by holding a pen in our teeth like this, it makes us feel happy. So it goes both ways. When it comes to power, it also goes both ways. So when you feel powerful,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 有一些證據表明他們確實這樣做。所以，舉例來說，當我們感到快樂時，我們會微笑，而且，當我們像這樣用牙齒叼著一支筆被迫微笑時，也會讓我們感到快樂。所以這是雙向的。當談到權力時，它也是雙向的。所以當你覺得自己很強大的時候，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: you're more likely to do this, but it's also possible that when you pretend to be powerful, you are more likely to actually feel powerful. So the second question really was, you know, so we know that our minds change our bodies, but is it also true that our bodies change our minds?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你更有可能這樣做，但也有可能當你假裝強大時，你更有可能真正感到強大。所以第二個問題確實是，你知道，所以我們知道我們的思想改變我們的身體，但是我們的身體也改變我們的思想嗎？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: And when I say minds, in the case of the powerful, what am I talking about? So I'm talking about thoughts and feelings and the sort of physiological things that make up our thoughts and feelings, and in my case, that's hormones. I look at hormones.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 當我說思想時，就強者而言，我在說什麼？所以我談論的是想法和感受，以及構成我們的想法和感受的生理因素，就我而言，這就是荷爾蒙。我看荷尔蒙。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So what do the minds of the powerful versus the powerless look like? So powerful people tend to be, not surprisingly, more assertive and more confident, more optimistic. They actually feel they're going to win even at games of chance. They also tend to be able to think more abstractly.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 那麼，強者與弱者的想法是什麼樣的呢？毫不奇怪，如此強大的人往往會更有自信、更有自信、更樂觀。他們實際上覺得即使在機會遊戲中他們也會獲勝。他們也往往能夠進行更抽象的思考。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So there are a lot of differences. They take more risks. There are a lot of differences between powerful and powerless people. Physiologically, there also are differences on two key hormones: testosterone, which is the dominance hormone, and cortisol, which is the stress hormone.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以有很多差異。他們承擔更多的風險。有權力的人和無權力的人之間有很多區別。從生理角度來看，兩種關鍵荷爾蒙也存在差異：睪固酮（主導荷爾蒙）和皮質醇（壓力荷爾蒙）。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So what we find is that high-power alpha males in primate hierarchies have high testosterone and low cortisol, and powerful and effective leaders also have high testosterone and low cortisol. So what does that mean? When you think about power, people tended to think only about testosterone,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以我們發現，靈長類等級中的高權力雄性領袖具有高睪固酮和低皮質醇，而強大而有效的領導者也具有高睪固酮和低皮質醇。那麼這是什麼意思呢？當你想到權力時，人們往往只想到睪固酮，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: because that was about dominance. But really, power is also about how you react to stress. So do you want the high-power leader that's dominant, high on testosterone, but really stress reactive? Probably not, right? You want the person who's powerful and assertive and dominant,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 因為那是關於統治地位的。但實際上，力量還在於你如何面對壓力。那麼，你想要一個高權力的領導者嗎？他具有主導地位，睪固酮水平高，但對壓力非常敏感？可能不會，對吧？你想要的是一個有力量、有自信、有統治力的人，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: but not very stress reactive, the person who's laid back. So we know that in primate hierarchies, if an alpha needs to take over, if an individual needs to take over an alpha role sort of suddenly, within a few days, that individual's testosterone has gone up
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 但對壓力不太敏感，是個悠閒的人。所以我們知道，在靈長類等級制度中，如果一個阿爾法需要接管，如果一個個體需要突然接管阿爾法角色，幾天之內，該個體的睪固酮就會上升

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: significantly and his cortisol has dropped significantly. So we have this evidence, both that the body can shape the mind, at least at the facial level, and also that role changes can shape the mind. So what happens, okay, you take a role change, what happens if you do that at a really minimal level,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 顯著，他的皮質醇也顯著下降。所以我們有證據表明，身體可以塑造心靈，至少在臉部層面，角色的變化也可以塑造心靈。那麼會發生什麼，好吧，你改變角色，如果你在最低限度上這樣做，會發生什麼，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: "For two minutes," you say, "I want you to stand like this, and it's going to make you feel more powerful." So this is what we did. We decided to bring people into the lab and run a little experiment, and these people adopted, for two minutes, either high-power poses or low-power poses,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 「兩分鐘，」你說，「我希望你像這樣站著，這會讓你感覺更有力量。」這就是我們所做的。我們決定帶人們進入實驗室並進行一個小實驗，這些人在兩分鐘內採用了高功率姿勢或低功率姿勢，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and I'm just going to show you five of the poses, although they took on only two. This one has been dubbed the "Wonder Woman" by the media. Here are a couple more. So you can be standing or you can be sitting. And here are the low-power poses. So you're folding up, you're making yourself small.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我將向您展示其中五個姿勢，儘管它們只呈現了兩個。這位被媒體稱為「神力女超人」的人。這裡還有一些。所以你可以站著，也可以坐著。這是低功率姿勢。所以你折起來，你讓自己變小。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: This one is very low-power. When you're touching your neck, you're really protecting yourself. So this is what happens. They come in, they spit into a vial, for two minutes, we say, "You need to do this or this." They don't look at pictures of the poses.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 這個功率非常低。當你觸摸自己的脖子時，你實際上是在保護自己。所以這就是發生的事情。他們進來，對著小瓶子吐口水，持續兩分鐘，我們說，「你需要這樣做或這樣做。」他們不看姿勢照片。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: We don't want to prime them with a concept of power. We want them to be feeling power. So two minutes they do this. We then ask them, "How powerful do you feel?" on a series of items, and then we give them an opportunity to gamble, and then we take another saliva sample.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我們不想讓他們灌輸權力概念。我們希望他們感受到力量。他們這樣做了兩分鐘。然後我們問他們：「你覺得自己有多強大？」在一系列項目上，然後我們給他們一個賭博的機會，然後我們又採集了另一個唾液樣本。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: That's it. That's the whole experiment. So this is what we find. Risk tolerance, which is the gambling, we find that when you are in the high-power pose condition, 86 percent of you will gamble. When you're in the low-power pose condition,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 就是這樣。這就是整個實驗。這就是我們發現的。風險承受能力，也就是賭博，我們發現當你處於高權力姿勢的時候，86%的人會賭博。當你處於低功率姿勢狀態時，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: only 60 percent, and that's a whopping significant difference. Here's what we find on testosterone. From their baseline when they come in, high-power people experience about a 20-percent increase, and low-power people experience about a 10-percent decrease.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 只有 60%，這是一個巨大的顯著差異。這是我們在睪酮方面的發現。從他們進來時的基線來看，高權力的人經歷了大約 20% 的增長，而低權力的人經歷了大約 10% 的下降。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So again, two minutes, and you get these changes. Here's what you get on cortisol. High-power people experience about a 25-percent decrease, and the low-power people experience about a 15-percent increase. So two minutes lead to these hormonal changes that configure your brain
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 再說一遍，兩分鐘，你就會得到這些變化。這就是你從皮質醇中得到的結果。高權力的人經歷了大約 25% 的減少，而低權力的人經歷了大約 15% 的增加。因此，兩分鐘就會導致這些荷爾蒙變化，從而配置您的大腦

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: to basically be either assertive, confident and comfortable, or really stress-reactive, and feeling sort of shut down. And we've all had the feeling, right? So it seems that our nonverbals do govern how we think and feel about ourselves, so it's not just others, but it's also ourselves.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 基本上不是自信、自信、自在，就是對壓力非常敏感，感覺有點被封閉。我們都有過這種感覺，對吧？所以看來我們的非語言確實控制著我們對自己的思考和感受，所以這不只是別人的，也是我們自己的。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Also, our bodies change our minds. But the next question, of course, is, can power posing for a few minutes really change your life in meaningful ways? This is in the lab, it's this little task, it's just a couple of minutes. Where can you actually apply this? Which we cared about, of course.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 此外，我們的身體會改變我們的想法。但下一個問題當然是，幾分鐘的強力姿勢真的能以有意義的方式改變你的生活嗎？這是在實驗室裡，這是一個小任務，只需幾分鐘。你可以在哪裡實際應用這個？當然，我們關心這一點。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: And so we think where you want to use this is evaluative situations, Where are you being evaluated, either by your friends? For teenagers, it's at the lunchroom table. For some people it's speaking at a school board meeting. It might be giving a pitch or giving a talk like this
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以我們認為你想在評估情境中使用它，你在哪裡被評估，或是由你的朋友評估？對於青少年來說，它是在午餐桌上。對某些人來說，這是在學校董事會會議上的演講。可能是進行推介或進行這樣的演講

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: or doing a job interview. We decided that the one that most people could relate to because most people had been through, was the job interview. So we published these findings, and the media are all over it, and they say, Okay, so this is what you do when you go in for the job interview, right?
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 或進行工作面試。我們決定，大多數人都可以理解的面試就是工作面試，因為大多數人都經歷過。所以我們發表了這些發現，媒體都在關注它，他們說，好吧，這就是你參加工作面試時要做的，對吧？

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: You know, so we were of course horrified, and said, Oh my God, no, that's not what we meant at all. For numerous reasons, no, don't do that. Again, this is not about you talking to other people. It's you talking to yourself. What do you do before you go into a job interview? You do this.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你知道，所以我們當然感到震驚，並說，天哪，不，我們根本不是這個意思。由於多種原因，不，不要這樣做。再說一遍，這不是你與其他人交談的問題。這是你在自言自語。在參加工作面試前你會做什麼？你這樣做。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: You're sitting down. You're looking at your iPhone -- or your Android, not trying to leave anyone out. You're looking at your notes, you're hunching up, making yourself small, when really what you should be doing maybe is this, So that's what we want to test. Okay? So we bring people into a lab,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你坐下了。您正在查看您的 iPhone 或 Android，而不是試圖將任何人排除在外。你正在看你的筆記，你彎腰駝背，讓自己變得渺小，而你真正應該做的也許是這個，所以這就是我們想要測試的。好的？所以我們把人們帶進實驗室，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and they do either high- or low-power poses again, they go through a very stressful job interview. It's five minutes long. They are being recorded. They're being judged also, and the judges are trained to give no nonverbal feedback, so they look like this. Imagine this is the person interviewing you.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 他們再次擺出高權力或低權力的姿勢，他們經歷了一次非常緊張的工作面試。有五分鐘長。他們正在被記錄。他們也在接受評判，評審們接受過不提供非語言回饋的培訓，所以他們看起來像這樣。想像一下這是面試你的人。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So for five minutes, nothing, and this is worse than being heckled. People hate this. It's what Marianne LaFrance calls "standing in social quicksand." So this really spikes your cortisol. So this is the job interview we put them through, because we really wanted to see what happened.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 五分鐘之內，什麼也沒有，比被質問更糟糕。人們討厭這個。這就是瑪麗安·拉弗朗斯所說的「站在社會流沙中」。所以這確實會刺激你的皮質醇。這就是我們對他們的面試，因為我們真的想看看發生了什麼。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: We then have these coders look at these tapes, four of them. They're blind to the hypothesis. They're blind to the conditions. They have no idea who's been posing in what pose, and they end up looking at these sets of tapes, and they say, "We want to hire these people," all the high-power posers.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 然後我們讓這些編碼員查看這些磁帶，共四盤。他們對這個假設視而不見。他們對條件視而不見。他們不知道誰擺出了什麼姿勢，最後他們看了這些錄像帶，然後說，“我們想僱用這些人”，所有這些人都是高權力的擺姿勢者。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: "We don't want to hire these people. We also evaluate these people much more positively overall." But what's driving it? It's not about the content of the speech. It's about the presence that they're bringing to the speech. Because we rate them on all these variables
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 「我們不想僱用這些人。我們對這些人的整體評價也更加積極。」但是是什麼推動了它呢？這與演講的內容無關。這是關於他們在演講中的表現。因為我們根據所有這些變數對它們進行評級

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: related to competence, like, how well-structured is the speech? How good is it? What are their qualifications? No effect on those things. This is what's affected. These kinds of things. People are bringing their true selves, basically. They're bringing themselves.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 與能力相關，例如演講的結構如何？有多好？他們有什麼資格？對那些東西沒有影響。這就是受到影響的。諸如此類的事情。基本上，人們正在展現真實的自己。他們自己帶來。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: They bring their ideas, but as themselves, with no, you know, residue over them. So this is what's driving the effect, or mediating the effect. So when I tell people about this, that our bodies change our minds and our minds can change our behavior,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 他們帶來了自己的想法，但就像他們自己一樣，你知道，沒有任何殘留物。所以這就是驅動效應或調節效果的因素。所以當我告訴人們這一點時，我們的身體可以改變我們的思想，我們的思想可以改變我們的行為，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and our behavior can change our outcomes, they say to me, "It feels fake." Right? So I said, fake it till you make it. I don't want to get there and then still feel like a fraud. I don't want to feel like an impostor. I don't want to get there only to feel like I'm not supposed to be here.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我們的行為可以改變我們的結果，他們對我說，「這感覺很假。」正確的？所以我說，假裝它直到你成功為止。我不想到了那裡之後仍然感覺自己像個騙子。我不想感覺自己像個騙子。我不想到了那裡只是覺得我不應該在這裡。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: And that really resonated with me, because I want to tell you a little story about being an impostor and feeling like I'm not supposed to be here. When I was 19, I was in a really bad car accident. I was thrown out of a car, rolled several times. I was thrown from the car.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 這確實引起了我的共鳴，因為我想告訴你一個關於作為冒名頂替者的小故事，並且感覺我不應該在這裡。當我19歲的時候，我遭遇了一場嚴重的車禍。我被從車上扔了出來，滾了好幾圈。我被從車上丟了出來。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: And I woke up in a head injury rehab ward, and I had been withdrawn from college, and I learned that my IQ had dropped by two standard deviations, which was very traumatic. I knew my IQ because I had identified with being smart, and I had been called gifted as a child.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我在頭部受傷復健病房醒來，我已經從大學退學，我得知我的智商下降了兩個標準差，這是非常痛苦的。我知道自己的智商，因為我認為自己很聰明，小時候就被稱為天才。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So I'm taken out of college, I keep trying to go back. They say, "You're not going to finish college. Just, you know, there are other things for you to do, but that's not going to work out for you." So I really struggled with this, and I have to say,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以我被大學退學了，我一直在努力回去。他們說：「你不會完成大學學業。只是，你知道，你還有其他事情要做，但這對你來說是行不通的。」所以我真的很掙扎，我不得不說，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: having your identity taken from you, your core identity, and for me it was being smart, having that taken from you, there's nothing that leaves you feeling more powerless than that. So I felt entirely powerless. I worked and worked, and I got lucky, and worked, and got lucky, and worked.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你的身份被剝奪了，你的核心身份被剝奪了，對我來說這是明智的，被剝奪了你的身份，沒有什麼比這更讓你感到無能為力了。所以我感到完全無力。我工作又工作，我很幸運，我工作，我很幸運，我工作。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Eventually I graduated from college. It took me four years longer than my peers, and I convinced someone, my angel advisor, Susan Fiske, to take me on, and so I ended up at Princeton, and I was like, I am not supposed to be here. I am an impostor. And the night before my first-year talk,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 最終我大學畢業了。我比同齡人多花了四年時間，然後我說服了一個人，我的天使顧問蘇珊·菲斯克（Susan Fiske）來接受我，所以我最終來到了普林斯頓大學，我當時想，我不應該在這裡。我是個騙子。在我第一年演講的前一天晚上，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and the first-year talk at Princeton is a 20-minute talk to 20 people. I was so afraid of being found out the next day that I called her and said, "I'm quitting." She was like, "You are not quitting, because I took a gamble on you, and you're staying.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 普林斯頓第一年的演講是對 20 個人進行 20 分鐘的演講。我很害怕第二天被發現，所以我打電話給她說：「我要辭職了。」她說，「你不會放棄，因為我在你身上下了賭注，而你要留下來。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: You're going to stay, and this is what you're going to do. You are going to fake it. You're going to do every talk that you ever get asked to do. You're just going to do it and do it and do it, even if you're terrified and just paralyzed and having an out-of-body experience,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你會留下來，這就是你要做的。你會偽造它。你將完成要求你做的每一次演講。你只是要做，做，做，即使你很害怕，只是癱瘓，有一種靈魂出竅的體驗，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: until you have this moment where you say, 'Oh my gosh, I'm doing it. So that's what I did. Five years in grad school, a few years, you know, I'm at Northwestern, I moved to Harvard, I'm at Harvard, I'm not really thinking about it anymore, but for a long time I had been thinking,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 直到你有這一刻說，『天哪，我正在這麼做。這就是我所做的。在研究所讀了五年，幾年，你知道，我在西北大學，我搬到了哈佛，我在哈佛，我不再真正考慮它了，但很長一段時間我一直在想，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: "Not supposed to be here." So at the end of my first year at Harvard, a student who had not talked in class the entire semester, who I had said, "Look, you've gotta participate or else you're going to fail," came into my office. I really didn't know her at all.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 「不應該在這裡。」因此，在我在哈佛的第一年結束時，一個整個學期都沒有在課堂上說話的學生走進我的辦公室，我對他說：「聽著，你必須參與，否則你就會失敗。」我真的根本不認識她。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: She came in totally defeated, and she said, "I'm not supposed to be here." And that was the moment for me. Because two things happened. One was that I realized, oh my gosh, I don't feel like that anymore. I don't feel that anymore, but she does, and I get that feeling.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 她完全失敗了，她說：「我不應該在這裡。」那就是我的時刻。因為發生了兩件事。一是我意識到，天哪，我不再有那樣的感覺了。我不再有這種感覺了，但她有，而且我有這種感覺。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: And the second was, she is supposed to be here! So I was like, "Yes, you are! You are supposed to be here! And tomorrow you're going to fake it, you're going to make yourself powerful, and, you know -- And you're going to go into the classroom, and you are going to give the best comment ever."
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 第二個是，她應該在這裡！所以我說，“是的，你是！你應該在這裡！明天你要假裝它，你要讓自己變得強大，而且，你知道——你要走進教室，你要給出有史以來最好的評論。”

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: You know? And she gave the best comment ever, and people turned around and were like, oh my God, I didn't even notice her sitting there. (Laughter) She comes back to me months later, and I realized that she had not just faked it till she made it, she had actually faked it till she became it.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 你知道？她給出了有史以來最好的評論，人們轉過身來，說，天哪，我什至沒有註意到她坐在那裡。 （笑聲） 幾個月後她回到我身邊，我意識到她不僅僅是在成功之前假裝它，而是在她真正成功之前一直假裝它。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: So she had changed. And so I want to say to you, don't fake it till you make it. Fake it till you become it. Do it enough until you actually become it and internalize. The last thing I'm going to leave you with is this. Tiny tweaks can lead to big changes. So, this is two minutes.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 所以她變了。所以我想對你說，在你成功之前不要假裝它。假裝它直到你成為它。做得足夠多，直到你真正成為它並內化為止。我要留給你的最後一件事就是這個。微小的調整可能會帶來巨大的改變。所以，這是兩分鐘。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Two minutes, two minutes, two minutes. Before you go into the next stressful evaluative situation, for two minutes, try doing this, in the elevator, in a bathroom stall, at your desk behind closed doors. That's what you want to do. Configure your brain to cope the best in that situation.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 兩分鐘，兩分鐘，兩分鐘。在你進入下一個有壓力的評估情境之前，花兩分鐘時間，在電梯裡、在浴室隔間裡、在緊閉門後的辦公桌前嘗試這樣做。這就是你想做的事。配置你的大腦以在這種情況下最好地應對。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Get your testosterone up. Get your cortisol down. Don't leave that situation feeling like, oh, I didn't show them who I am. Leave that situation feeling like, I really feel like I got to say who I am and show who I am. So I want to ask you first, you know, both to try power posing,
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 讓你的睪丸激素升高。讓你的皮質醇下降。不要讓這種情況感覺，哦，我沒有向他們展示我是誰。讓這種情況感覺就像，我真的覺得我必須說出我是誰並展示我是誰。所以我想先問你，你知道，都嘗試擺姿勢，

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: and also I want to ask you to share the science, because this is simple. I don't have ego involved in this. (Laughter) Give it away. Share it with people, because the people who can use it the most are the ones with no resources and no technology and no status and no power.
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 我也想請您分享科學原理，因為這很簡單。我沒有自我參與其中。 （笑聲） 把它送給別人。與人分享，因為最能利用它的人是那些沒有資源、沒有技術、沒有地位、沒有權力的人。

susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.en: Give it to them because they can do it in private. They need their bodies, privacy and two minutes, and it can significantly change the outcomes of their life. Notifications alt+T
susan_cain_the_power_of_introverts.zh: 把它交給他們，因為他們可以私下做。他們需要自己的身體、隱私和兩分鐘，這可以顯著改變他們的生活結果。通知 alt+T

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: When I was nine years old, I went off to summer camp for the first time. And my mother packed me a suitcase full of books, which to me seemed like a perfectly natural thing to do. Because in my family, reading was the primary group activity. And this might sound antisocial to you,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 當我九歲的時候，我第一次去參加夏令營。我的母親給我裝滿了書，這對我來說似乎是一件很自然的事。因為在我的家裡，閱讀是主要的團體活動。這對你來說可能聽起來很反社會，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: but for us it was really just a different way of being social. You have the animal warmth of your family sitting right next to you, but you are also free to go roaming around the adventureland inside your own mind. And I had this idea that camp was going to be just like this, but better.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 但對我們來說，這其實只是一種不同的社交方式。 You have the animal warmth of your family sitting right next to you, but you are also free to go roaming around the adventureland inside your own mind.我的想法是，營地會像這樣，但會更好。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: I had a vision of 10 girls sitting in a cabin cozily reading books in their matching nightgowns. Camp was more like a keg party without any alcohol. And on the very first day, our counselor gathered us all together and she taught us a cheer that she said we would be doing
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我想像 10 個女孩穿著配套的睡衣坐在小屋裡舒適地閱讀。營地更像是一場沒有任何酒精的小桶派對。第一天，我們的輔導員把我們大家聚集在一起，她教我們歡呼，她說我們會這樣做

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: every day for the rest of the summer to instill camp spirit. And it went like this: that's the way we spell rowdie. Rowdie, rowdie, let's get rowdie." So I couldn't figure out for the life of me why we were supposed to be so rowdy, or why we had to spell this word incorrectly.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 在夏天剩下的時間裡，每天都在灌輸營地精神。事情是這樣的：這就是我們拼寫“rowdie”的方式。 Rowdie，rowdie，讓我們來Rowdie。 「所以我一生都無法弄清楚為什麼我們應該如此吵鬧，或者為什麼我們必須錯誤地拼寫這個詞。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: But I recited a cheer. I recited a cheer along with everybody else. And I just waited for the time that I could go off and read my books. But the first time that I took my book out of my suitcase, the coolest girl in the bunk came up to me and she asked me, "Why are you being so mellow?" --
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 但我卻歡呼一聲。我和大家一起歡呼。我只是等待我可以出去讀書的時間。但當我第一次從手提箱裡拿出書時，舖位上最酷的女孩走過來問我：“你為什麼這麼溫柔？” --

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: mellow, of course, being the exact opposite of R-O-W-D-I-E. And then the second time I tried it, the counselor came up to me with a concerned expression on her face and she repeated the point about camp spirit and said we should all work very hard to be outgoing. And so I put my books away,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 當然，柔和，與 R-O-W-D-I-E 完全相反。然後當我第二次嘗試時，輔導員臉上帶著擔憂的表情走到我面前，她重複了有關營地精神的觀點，並說我們都應該努力變得外向。於是我把書收起來，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: back in their suitcase, and I put them under my bed, and there they stayed for the rest of the summer. And I felt kind of guilty about this. I felt as if the books needed me somehow, and they were calling out to me and I was forsaking them. But I did forsake them and I didn't open that suitcase again
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 回到他們的手提箱裡，我把它們放在我的床下，他們在那裡度過了整個夏天。我對此感到有點內疚。我覺得這些書似乎需要我，它們在呼喚我，而我卻拋棄了它們。但我確實拋棄了他們，也沒有再打開那個手提箱

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: until I was back home with my family at the end of the summer. Now, I tell you this story about summer camp. I could have told you 50 others just like it -- all the times that I got the message that somehow my quiet and introverted style of being was not necessarily the right way to go,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 直到暑假結束時我和家人一起回到家。現在，我要跟大家講一個關於夏令營的故事。我本可以告訴你另外 50 個類似的人——每次我都收到這樣的訊息：不知何故，我安靜和內向的生活方式不一定是正確的方式，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: that I should be trying to pass as more of an extrovert. And I always sensed deep down that this was wrong and that introverts were pretty excellent just as they were. But for years I denied this intuition, and so I became a Wall Street lawyer, of all things,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我應該努力表現得更像一個外向的人。我內心深處總覺得這是錯的，內向的人本來就很優秀。但多年來我否認這種直覺，所以我成為了華爾街律師，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: instead of the writer that I had always longed to be -- partly because I needed to prove to myself that I could be bold and assertive too. And I was always going off to crowded bars when I really would have preferred to just have a nice dinner with friends.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 而不是我一直渴望成為的作家——部分原因是我需要向自己證明我也可以大膽而自信。當我真的更想和朋友一起吃一頓豐盛的晚餐時，我總是去擁擠的酒吧。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And I made these self-negating choices so reflexively, that I wasn't even aware that I was making them. Now this is what many introverts do, and it's our loss for sure, but it is also our colleagues' loss and our communities' loss. And at the risk of sounding grandiose, it is the world's loss.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我本能地做出了這些自我否定的選擇，以至於我甚至沒有意識到我正在做出這些選擇。現在，這就是許多內向者所做的事情，這肯定是我們的損失，但也是我們同事和社區的損失。冒著聽起來有些誇張的風險，這是世界的損失。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Because when it comes to creativity and to leadership, we need introverts doing what they do best. A third to a half of the population are introverts -- a third to a half. So that's one out of every two or three people you know. So even if you're an extrovert yourself,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 因為當談到創造力和領導力時，我們需要內向的人做他們最擅長的事情。三分之一到一半的人口是內向的人──三分之一到一半。所以這是你認識的每兩三個人就有一個。所以即使你本身就是一個外向的人

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: I'm talking about your coworkers and your spouses and your children and the person sitting next to you right now -- all of them subject to this bias that is pretty deep and real in our society. We all internalize it from a very early age without even having a language for what we're doing.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我說的是你的同事、你的配偶、你的孩子以及現在坐在你旁邊的人——他們所有人都受到我們社會中根深蒂固、真實存在的偏見的影響。我們從很小的時候就將其內化，甚至沒有語言來表達我們正在做的事情。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Now, to see the bias clearly, you need to understand what introversion is. It's different from being shy. Shyness is about fear of social judgment. Introversion is more about, how do you respond to stimulation, including social stimulation. So extroverts really crave large amounts of stimulation,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 現在，要清楚地看到這種偏見，您需要了解什麼是內向。這與害羞不同。害羞是對社會評判的恐懼。內向更多的是關於你如何應對刺激，包括社交刺激。所以外向的人確實渴望大量的刺激，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: whereas introverts feel at their most alive and their most switched-on and their most capable when they're in quieter, more low-key environments. Not all the time -- these things aren't absolute -- but a lot of the time. So the key then to maximizing our talents is for us all to put ourselves
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 而內向的人在更安靜、更低調的環境中會感到最有活力、最活躍、最有能力。並非所有時候——這些事情都不是絕對的——但很多時候。因此，最大限度地發揮我們的才能的關鍵是我們所有人都將自己置於

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: in the zone of stimulation that is right for us. But now here's where the bias comes in. Our most important institutions, our schools and our workplaces, they are designed mostly for extroverts and for extroverts' need for lots of stimulation. And also we have this belief system right now
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 在適合我們的刺激區域。但現在偏見就出現了。我們最重要的機構、我們的學校和我們的工作場所，它們主要是為外向的人以及外向的人對大量刺激的需求而設計的。我們現在也有這個信仰體系

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: that I call the new groupthink, which holds that all creativity and all productivity comes from a very oddly gregarious place. So if you picture the typical classroom nowadays: When I was going to school, we sat in rows. We sat in rows of desks like this,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我稱之為新群體思維，它認為所有創造力和所有生產力都來自一個非常奇怪的群居環境。所以，如果你想像現在典型的教室：當我去學校時，我們坐成一排。我們像這樣坐在一排桌子上，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: and we did most of our work pretty autonomously. But nowadays, your typical classroom has pods of desks -- four or five or six or seven kids all facing each other. And kids are working in countless group assignments. Even in subjects like math and creative writing,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我們相當自主地完成了大部分工作。但現在，典型的教室裡有很多桌子──四個、五個、六、七個孩子麵對面。孩子們正在完成無數的小組作業。即使在數學和創意寫作等學科中，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: which you think would depend on solo flights of thought, kids are now expected to act as committee members. And for the kids who prefer to go off by themselves or just to work alone, those kids are seen as outliers often or, worse, as problem cases. And the vast majority of teachers
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 你認為這取決於個人的思想飛翔，但現在孩子們被期望充當委員會成員。對於那些喜歡自己出去或獨自工作的孩子來說，這些孩子經常被視為異常值，或者更糟的是，被視為問題案例。還有絕大多數老師

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: reports believing that the ideal student is an extrovert as opposed to an introvert, even though introverts actually get better grades and are more knowledgeable, according to research. Okay, same thing is true in our workplaces. Now, most of us work in open plan offices,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 報告認為，理想的學生是外向的人，而不是內向的人，儘管研究表明，內向的人實際上成績更好，知識更豐富。好吧，在我們的工作場所也是如此。現在，我們大多數人在開放式辦公室工作，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: where we are subject to the constant noise and gaze of our coworkers. And when it comes to leadership, introverts are routinely passed over for leadership positions, even though introverts tend to be very careful, much less likely to take outsize risks --
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 在那裡，我們經常受到同事的噪音和目光的影響。當談到領導力時，內向的人通常會被忽視擔任領導職位，儘管內向的人往往非常小心，更不可能冒太大的風險——

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: which is something we might all favor nowadays. And interesting research by Adam Grant at the Wharton School has found that introverted leaders often deliver better outcomes than extroverts do, because when they are managing proactive employees,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 這是我們現在可能都喜歡的東西。華頓商學院的亞當·格蘭特 (Adam Grant) 進行的一項有趣的研究發現，內向的領導者通常會比外向的領導者取得更好的成果，因為當他們管理積極主動的員工時，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: they're much more likely to let those employees run with their ideas, whereas an extrovert can, quite unwittingly, get so excited about things that they're putting their own stamp on things, and other people's ideas might not as easily then bubble up to the surface.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 他們更有可能讓這些員工貫徹他們的想法，而外向的人可能會在不知不覺中對事情感到如此興奮，以至於他們在事情上打上自己的印記，而其他人的想法可能不會那麼容易浮出水面。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Now in fact, some of our transformative leaders in history have been introverts. I'll give you some examples. Eleanor Roosevelt, Rosa Parks, Gandhi -- all these people described themselves as quiet and soft-spoken and even shy. And they all took the spotlight,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 事實上，我們歷史上的一些變革型領導人都是內向的。我舉一些例子給你聽。羅斯福、羅莎·帕克斯、甘地——所有這些人都形容自己安靜、說話輕聲細語，甚至害羞。他們都成為了眾人矚目的焦點，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: even though every bone in their bodies was telling them not to. And this turns out to have a special power all its own, because people could feel that these leaders were at the helm not because they enjoyed directing others and not out of the pleasure of being looked at;
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 儘管他們身體裡的每一根骨頭都告訴他們不要這樣做。事實證明，這本身就具有一種特殊的力量，因為人們可以感覺到這些領導人掌舵，並不是因為他們喜歡指導別人，也不是因為被人注視的樂趣；

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: they were there because they had no choice, because they were driven to do what they thought was right. Now I think at this point it's important for me to say that I actually love extroverts. I always like to say some of my best friends are extroverts, including my beloved husband.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 他們在那裡是因為他們別無選擇，因為他們被迫去做他們認為正確的事情。現在我認為在這一點上我很重要的是要說我實際上喜歡外向的人。我總是喜歡說我最好的一些朋友都是外向的人，包括我深愛的丈夫。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And we all fall at different points, of course, along the introvert/extrovert spectrum. Even Carl Jung, the psychologist who first popularized these terms, said that there's no such thing as a pure introvert or a pure extrovert. He said that such a man would be in a lunatic asylum,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 當然，我們都處於內向/外向譜系的不同點。就連最先普及這些術語的心理學家卡爾榮格也表示，不存在純粹內向或純粹外向的人。他說這樣的人會進瘋人院，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: if he existed at all. And some people fall smack in the middle of the introvert/extrovert spectrum, and we call these people ambiverts. And I often think that they have the best of all worlds. But many of us do recognize ourselves as one type or the other.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 如果他真的存在的話。有些人正好處於內向/外向譜系的中間，我們稱這些人為中間性格。我常常認為他們擁有世界上最好的。但我們中的許多人確實認識到自己屬於其中一種類型。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And what I'm saying is that culturally, we need a much better balance. We need more of a yin and yang between these two types. This is especially important when it comes to creativity and to productivity, because when psychologists look at the lives of the most creative people,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我想說的是，在文化上，我們需要更好的平衡。在這兩種類型之間我們需要更多的陰陽。當涉及創造力和生產力時，這一點尤其重要，因為當心理學家觀察到最具創造力的人的生活時，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: are people who are very good at exchanging ideas and advancing ideas, but who also have a serious streak of introversion in them. And this is because solitude is a crucial ingredient often to creativity. he took long walks alone in the woods and emphatically turned down dinner-party invitations.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 這些人非常善於交流想法和推進想法，但也有嚴重的內向傾向。這是因為孤獨往往是創造力的關鍵因素。他獨自在樹林裡散步，並斷然拒絕了晚宴的邀約。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Theodor Geisel, better known as Dr. Seuss, he dreamed up many of his amazing creations in a lonely bell tower office that he had in the back of his house in La Jolla, California. And he was actually afraid to meet the young children who read his books
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 西奧多·蓋塞爾（Theodor Geisel），更廣為人知的名字是蘇斯博士（Dr. Seuss），他在加利福尼亞州拉霍亞的房子後面一間孤獨的鐘樓辦公室裡夢想著他的許多令人驚嘆的創作。他其實害怕見到讀他書的小孩子

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: for fear that they were expecting him this kind of jolly Santa Claus-like figure and would be disappointed with his more reserved persona. Steve Wozniak invented the first Apple computer sitting alone in his cubicle in Hewlett-Packard where he was working at the time.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 因為擔心他們期待他是這種快樂的聖誕老人般的人物，而會對他更內向的性格感到失望。史蒂夫·沃茲尼亞克發明了第一台蘋果電腦，他獨自坐在惠普公司的小隔間裡，當時他就在那裡工作。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And he says that he never would have become such an expert in the first place had he not been too introverted to leave the house when he was growing up. Now, of course, this does not mean that we should all stop collaborating --
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 他說，如果他在成長過程中不是太內向，不願離開家，他一開始就不會成為這樣的專家。當然，現在這並不意味著我們都應該停止合作——

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: and case in point, is Steve Wozniak famously coming together with Steve Jobs to start Apple Computer -- but it does mean that solitude matters and that for some people it is the air that they breathe. And in fact, we have known for centuries about the transcendent power of solitude.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 舉個例子，著名的史蒂夫·沃茲尼亞克（Steve Wozniak）與史蒂夫·喬布斯（Steve Jobs）一起創辦了蘋果電腦（Apple Computer）——但這確實意味著孤獨很重要，而對於某些人來說，孤獨就是他們呼吸的空氣。事實上，幾個世紀以來我們就知道孤獨的超凡力量。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: It's only recently that we've strangely begun to forget it. If you look at most of the world's major religions, you will find seekers -- Moses, Jesus, Buddha, Muhammad -- seekers who are going off by themselves alone to the wilderness, where they then have profound epiphanies and revelations
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 直到最近我們才奇怪地開始忘記它。如果你看看世界上大多數主要宗教，你會發現尋求者——摩西、耶穌、佛陀、穆罕默德——這些尋求者獨自前往荒野，在那裡他們獲得了深刻的頓悟和啟示。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: that they then bring back to the rest of the community. So, no wilderness, no revelations. This is no surprise, though, if you look at the insights of contemporary psychology. It turns out that we can't even be in a group of people without instinctively mirroring, mimicking their opinions.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 然後他們將其帶回社區其他人。所以，沒有荒野，就沒有啟示。不過，如果你看看當代心理學的見解，這並不奇怪。事實證明，我們甚至無法在一群人中不本能地反映、模仿他們的觀點。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Even about seemingly personal and visceral things you will start aping the beliefs of the people around you without even realizing that that's what you're doing. And groups famously follow the opinions of the most dominant or charismatic person in the room, even though there's zero correlation
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 即使對於看似個人和發自內心的事情，你也會開始模仿周圍人的信仰，甚至沒有意識到這就是你正在做的事情。眾所周知，團體會遵循房間裡最具統治力或最有魅力的人的意見，儘管相關性為零

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: between being the best talker and having the best ideas -- You might be following the person with the best ideas, but you might not. And do you really want to leave it up to chance? Much better for everybody to go off by themselves, generate their own ideas
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 在成為最好的談話者和擁有最好的想法之間——你可能會追隨擁有最好想法的人，但你可能不會。你真的想聽天由命嗎？每個人都可以自己出發，產生自己的想法，會更好

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: freed from the distortions of group dynamics, and then come together as a team to talk them through in a well-managed environment and take it from there. Now if all this is true, then why are we getting it so wrong? Why are we setting up our schools this way, and our workplaces?
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 擺脫群體動態的扭曲，然後作為一個團隊聚集在一起，在管理良好的環境中討論這些問題，並從那裡開始。如果這一切都是真的，那為什麼我們會犯下這麼大的錯誤呢？我們為什麼要這樣建立我們的學校和工作場所？

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And why are we making these introverts feel so guilty about wanting to just go off by themselves some of the time? One answer lies deep in our cultural history. Western societies, and in particular the U.S., have always favored the man of action over the "man" of contemplation.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 為什麼我們會讓這些內向的人因為有時想要獨自離開而感到如此內疚？一個答案深藏在我們的文化歷史中。西方社會，特別是美國，總是青睞行動者而不是沉思者。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: But in America's early days, we lived in what historians call a culture of character, where we still, at that point, valued people for their inner selves and their moral rectitude. And if you look at the self-help books from this era, they all had titles with things like
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 但在美國早期，我們生活在歷史學家所說的品格文化中，那時我們仍然重視人們的內在自我和道德正直。如果你看看這個時代的自助書籍，它們的標題都是這樣的：

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: "Character, the Grandest Thing in the World." And they featured role models like Abraham Lincoln, who was praised for being modest and unassuming. Ralph Waldo Emerson called him "A man who does not offend by superiority." But then we hit the 20th century, and we entered a new culture
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 「性格，世界上最偉大的東西。」他們以亞伯拉罕·林肯(Abraham Lincoln)為榜樣，他因謙虛、謙遜而受到稱讚。拉爾夫·沃爾多·愛默生稱他為“一個不會因優越感而冒犯的人”。但後來我們進入了 20 世紀，我們進入了新的文化

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: that historians call the culture of personality. What happened is we had evolved an agricultural economy to a world of big business. And so suddenly people are moving from small towns to the cities. And instead of working alongside people they've known all their lives,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 歷史學家稱之為人格文化。所發生的事情是，我們已經從農業經濟發展到了大商業世界。突然之間，人們從小城鎮搬到了城市。與其和他們認識了一輩子的人一起工作，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: now they are having to prove themselves in a crowd of strangers. So, quite understandably, qualities like magnetism and charisma suddenly come to seem really important. And sure enough, the self-help books change to meet these new needs and they start to have names
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 現在他們必須在一群陌生人中證明自己。因此，可以理解的是，像吸引力和魅力這樣的品質突然變得非常重要。果然，自助書籍發生了變化，以滿足這些新的需求，並且它們開始有了名字

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And they feature as their role models really great salesmen. So that's the world we're living in today. That's our cultural inheritance. Now none of this is to say that social skills are unimportant, and I'm also not calling for the abolishing of teamwork at all.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 他們以真正優秀的推銷員為榜樣。這就是我們今天所生活的世界。這就是我們的文化傳承。這並不是說社交技能不重要，我也不是在呼籲廢除團隊合作。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: The same religions who send their sages off to lonely mountain tops also teach us love and trust. And the problems that we are facing today in fields like science and in economics are so vast and so complex that we are going to need armies of people coming together to solve them working together.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 將聖人送到孤獨山頂的宗教也教導我們愛與信。我們今天在科學和經濟學等領域面臨的問題是如此巨大和複雜，以至於我們需要大批人齊心協力來解決這些問題。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: But I am saying that the more freedom that we give introverts to be themselves, the more likely that they are to come up with their own unique solutions to these problems. So now I'd like to share with you what's in my suitcase today. I have a suitcase full of books.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 但我想說的是，我們給予內向的人越多的自由，就越有可能針對這些問題提出自己獨特的解決方案。所以現在我想跟大家分享一下我今天行李箱裡有什麼。我有一個裝滿書的手提箱。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Here's Margaret Atwood, "Cat's Eye." Here's a novel by Milan Kundera. And here's "The Guide for the Perplexed" by Maimonides. But these are not exactly my books. I brought these books with me because they were written by my grandfather's favorite authors. My grandfather was a rabbi
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 這是瑪格麗特·阿特伍德的《貓眼》。這是米蘭昆德拉的小說。這是邁蒙尼德的《困惑者指南》。但這些不完全是我的書。我帶著這些書是因為它們是我祖父最喜歡的作家寫的。我的祖父是一位拉比

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: and he was a widower who lived alone in a small apartment in Brooklyn that was my favorite place in the world when I was growing up, partly because it was filled with his very gentle, very courtly presence and partly because it was filled with books.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 他是個鰥夫，獨自住在布魯克林的一間小公寓裡，那是我成長過程中最喜歡的地方，部分原因是那裡充滿了他非常溫柔、非常有禮貌的存在，部分原因是那裡充滿了書籍。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: I mean literally every table, every chair in this apartment had yielded its original function to now serve as a surface for swaying stacks of books. Just like the rest of my family, my grandfather's favorite thing to do in the whole world was to read. But he also loved his congregation,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我的意思是，這間公寓裡的每張桌子、每張椅子都已經放棄了原來的功能，現在充當了搖曳的書堆的表面。就像我的家人一樣，我的祖父在全世界最喜歡做的事就是閱讀。但他也愛他的會眾，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: and you could feel this love in the sermons that he gave every week for the 62 years that he was a rabbi. He would takes the fruits of each week's reading and he would weave these intricate tapestries of ancient and humanist thought. And people would come from all over to hear him speak.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 在他擔任拉比的 62 年裡，你可以從他每週的講道中感受到這種愛。他會汲取每週閱讀的成果，並編織這些古老的人文主義思想的複雜掛毯。人們會從四面八方趕來聽他演講。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: But here's the thing about my grandfather. Underneath this ceremonial role, he was really modest and really introverted -- so much so that when he delivered these sermons, he had trouble making eye contact with the very same congregation that he had been speaking to for 62 years.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 但這是關於我祖父的事情。在這個儀式性的角色之下，他非常謙虛、非常內向——以至於當他發表這些講道時，他很難與他 62 年來一直在演講的同一群會眾進行眼神交流。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And even away from the podium, when you called him to say hello, he would often end the conversation prematurely for fear that he was taking up too much of your time. But when he died at the age of 94, the police had to close down the streets of his neighborhood
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 即使不在講台上，當你打電話給他打招呼時，他也常常會提前結束談話，生怕佔據了你太多的時間。但當他94歲去世時，警方不得不封鎖了他家附近的街道

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: to accommodate the crowd of people who came out to mourn him. And so these days I try to learn from my grandfather's example So I just published a book about introversion, and it took me about seven years to write. And for me, that seven years was like total bliss,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 以容納前來悼念他的人群。所以這些天我試著學習我祖父的例子所以我剛剛出版了一本關於內向的書，我花了大約七年的時間來寫。對我來說，那七年就像是完全的幸福，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: because I was reading, I was writing, I was thinking, I was researching. It was my version of my grandfather's hours of the day alone in his library. But now all of a sudden my job is very different, and my job is to be out here talking about it, talking about introversion.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 因為我在閱讀，我在寫作，我在思考，我在研究。這是我祖父一天中獨自待在圖書館的時光的版本。但現在突然間我的工作變得非常不同，我的工作就是在這裡談論它，談論內向。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And that's a lot harder for me, because as honored as I am to be here with all of you right now, this is not my natural milieu. So I prepared for moments like these as best I could. I spent the last year practicing public speaking every chance I could get.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 這對我來說要困難得多，因為儘管我很榮幸現在能和大家在一起，但這不是我自然的環境。所以我盡我所能為這樣的時刻做好準備。去年我一有機會就練習公開演講。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: And I call this my "year of speaking dangerously." And that actually helped a lot. But I'll tell you, what helps even more is my sense, my belief, my hope that when it comes to our attitudes to introversion and to quiet and to solitude, we truly are poised on the brink on dramatic change.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我把今年稱為「危險言論年」。這實際上有很大幫助。但我會告訴你，更有幫助的是我的感覺、我的信念和我的希望，當談到我們對內向、安靜和孤獨的態度時，我們確實處於戲劇性變化的邊緣。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: I mean, we are. And so I am going to leave you now with three calls for action for those who share this vision. Stop the madness for constant group work. And I want to be clear about what I'm saying, because I deeply believe our offices
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我的意思是，我們是。因此，我現在向你們發出三項呼籲，呼籲那些擁有這個願景的人們採取行動。停止對持續的小組工作的瘋狂。我想澄清我所說的，因為我深信我們的辦公室

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: should be encouraging casual, chatty cafe-style types of interactions -- you know, the kind where people come together and serendipitously have an exchange of ideas. It's great for introverts and it's great for extroverts. But we need much more privacy and much more freedom
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 應該鼓勵休閒、閒聊的咖啡館式互動──你知道，就是人們聚在一起偶然交換想法的那種互動。這對於內向的人來說很棒，對於外向的人來說也很棒。但我們需要更多的隱私和更多的自由

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: and much more autonomy at work. School, same thing. We need to be teaching kids to work together, for sure, but we also need to be teaching them how to work on their own. This is especially important for extroverted children too. They need to work on their own
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 以及工作上更多的自主權。學校，同樣的事情。當然，我們需要教導孩子們一起工作，但我們也需要教他們如何獨立工作。這對外向的孩子來說也尤其重要。他們需要自己工作

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: because that is where deep thought comes from in part. Okay, number two: Go to the wilderness. Be like Buddha, have your own revelations. that we all have to now go off and build our own cabins in the woods and never talk to each other again, but I am saying that we could all stand to unplug
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 因為這就是深度思考的部分來源。好吧，第二點：去荒野。像佛一樣，有自己的啟示。我們現在都必須離開並在樹林裡建造我們自己的小屋，並且不再互相交談，但我想說的是，我們都可以忍受拔掉插頭

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: and get inside our own heads a little more often. Take a good look at what's inside your own suitcase and why you put it there. maybe your suitcases are also full of books. Or maybe they're full of champagne glasses or skydiving equipment.
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 並更頻繁地進入我們自己的頭腦。仔細看看你自己的手提箱裡有什麼，以及為什麼要把它放在那裡。也許你的手提箱裡也裝滿了書。或者他們可能裝滿了香檳杯或跳傘設備。

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: Whatever it is, I hope you take these things out every chance you get and grace us with your energy and your joy. But introverts, you being you, you probably have the impulse to guard very carefully what's inside your own suitcase. And that's okay. But occasionally, just occasionally,
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 不管是什麼，我希望你一有機會就把這些東西拿出來，用你的能量和快樂來帶給我們歡樂。但對於內向的人來說，你可能會有一種衝動，要非常小心地保護自己行李箱裡的東西。沒關係。但偶爾，只是偶爾，

james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.en: I hope you will open up your suitcases for other people to see, because the world needs you and it needs the things you carry. So I wish you the best of all possible journeys and the courage to speak softly. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. Notifications alt+T
james_veitch_this_is_what_happens_when_y.zh: 我希望你打開你的行李箱讓別人看到，因為世界需要你，需要你攜帶的東西。因此，我祝福您旅途順利，並有勇氣輕聲說話。非常感謝。謝謝。謝謝。通知 alt+T

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: A few years ago, I got one of those spam emails. And it managed to get through my spam filter. I'm not quite sure how, but it turned up in my inbox, and it was from a guy called Solomon Odonkoh. It went like this: it said, "Hello James Veitch,
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 幾年前，我收到了一封垃圾郵件。它成功地通過了我的垃圾郵件過濾器。我不太清楚是怎麼回事，但它出現在我的收件匣中，而且是來自一個叫所羅門·奧登科的人。它是這樣的：上面寫著：「你好，詹姆斯·維奇，

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I have an interesting business proposal I want to share with you, Solomon." Now, my hand was kind of hovering on the delete button, right? I was looking at my phone. I thought, I could just delete this. Or I could do what I think we've all always wanted to do.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我有一個有趣的商業提案想與你分享，所羅門。 「現在，我的手懸停在刪除按鈕上，對嗎？我看著我的手機。我想，我可以刪除這個。或者我可以做我認為我們一直想做的事情。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: And I said, "Solomon, Your email intrigues me." And the game was afoot. He said, "Dear James Veitch, We shall be shipping Gold to you."
