"You will earn 10% of any gold you distributes." So I knew I was dealing with a professional. I said, "How much is it worth?"
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我說：「所羅門，你的電子郵件引起了我的興趣。」比賽正在進行中。他說：「親愛的詹姆斯·維奇，我們將把黃金運送給您。」 「你將獲得你所分配的黃金的 10%。」所以我知道我正在和專業人士打交道。我說：“值多少錢？”

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: He said, "We will start with smaller quantity," -- I was like, aww -- and then he said, "of 25 kgs. The worth should be about $2.5 million." I said, "Solomon, if we're going to do it, let's go big. I can handle it. How much gold do you have?" He said, "It is not a matter of how much gold I have,
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 他說，「我們將從較小的數量開始，」——我當時想，哇——然後他說，「25 公斤。價值應該約為 250 萬美元。」我說：「所羅門，如果我們要這麼做，那就做大一點。我能應付。你有多少黃金？」他說：「這不是我有多少黃金的問題，那就做大一點。我能應付。你有多少黃金？」他說：「這不是我有多少黃金的問題，那就做大一點。我能應付。你有多少黃金？」他說：「這不是我有多少黃金的問題，那就做大一點。我能應付。你有多少黃金？」他說：「這不是我有多少黃金的問題，那麼

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: what matters is your capability of handling. We can start with 50 kgs as trial shipment." I said, "50 kgs? There's no point doing this at all unless you're shipping at least a metric ton." He said, "What do you do for a living?" I said, "I'm a hedge fund executive bank manager."
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 重要的是你的處理能力。我們可以從50公斤開始試運。 」我說：「50公斤？除非你要運送至少一噸，否則這樣做根本沒有意義。他說，「你以什麼為生？ 」我說，「我是一名對沖基金執行銀行經理。 」

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: This isn't the first time I've shipped bullion, my friend, Then I started to panic. I was like, "Where are you based?" I don't know about you, but I think if we're going via the postal service, it ought to be signed for. That's a lot of gold." He said, "It will not be easy to convince my company
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 這不是我第一次運送金條，我的朋友，然後我開始恐慌。我當時想，「你在哪裡？」我不了解你的情況，但我認為如果我們透過郵政服務，就應該簽收。這是很多黃金。他說，「說服我的公司並不容易

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: to do larger quantity shipment." I said, "Solomon, I'm completely with you on this one. I'm putting together a visual for you to take into the board meeting. This is what I sent Solomon. I don't know if we have any statisticians in the house, but there's definitely something going on.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 進行更大數量的運輸。 」我說，「所羅門，在這一點上我完全同意你的觀點。我正在整理一個視覺效果供您在董事會會議上使用。這是我寄給所羅門的。我不知道我們家裡是否有統計學家，但肯定有事情發生。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I said, "Solomon, attached to this email you'll find a helpful chart. I've had one of my assistants run the numbers. We're ready for shipping as much gold as possible." There's always a moment where they try to tug your heartstrings, and this was it for Solomon.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我說：「所羅門，在這封​​電子郵件的附件中，您會找到一張有用的圖表。我已經讓我的一位助手計算了這些數字。我們已經準備好運送盡可能多的黃金。」總有那麼一個時刻，他們試圖觸動你的心弦，而這就是所羅門的時刻。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: He said, "I will be so much happy if the deal goes well, because I'm going to get a very good commission as well." And I said, "That's amazing, What are you going to spend your cut on?" And he said, "On RealEstate, what about you?" I thought about it for a long time. And I said, "One word;
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 他說：「如果交易進展順利，我會非常高興，因為我也將獲得非常好的佣金。」我說：「太棒了，你打算把這筆錢花在什麼地方？」他說：「在房地產方面，你呢？」我想了很久。我說：「一句話；

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: "It's going places. I was in Sainsbury's the other day and there were like 30 different varieties. Also you can cut up carrots, and you can dip them. Have you ever done that, Solomon?" He said, "I have to go bed now." Have sweet dream." I didn't know what to say!
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 「它正在流行。前幾天我在塞恩斯伯里，那裡有大約 30 種不同的品種。你還可以切胡蘿蔔，然後將它們浸入水中。你曾經這樣做過嗎，所羅門？」他說：「我現在得去睡覺了。」祝你好夢。 「我不知道該說什麼！

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I said, "Bonsoir my golden nugget, bonsoir." Guys, you have to understand, this had been going for, like, weeks, albeit hitherto the greatest weeks of my life, but I had to knock it on the head. It was getting a bit out of hand. Friends were saying, "James, do you want to come for a drink?"
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我說：「晚安，我的金塊，晚安。」夥計們，你們必須明白，這種情況已經持續了大約幾週，儘管這是我一生中迄今為止最偉大的幾週，但我不得不把它敲在頭上。事情有點失控了。朋友們說：“詹姆斯，你想來喝一杯嗎？”

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I was like, "I can't, I'm expecting an email about some gold." So I figured I had to knock it on the head. I had to take it to a ridiculous conclusion. So I concocted a plan. I said, "Solomon, I'm concerned about security. When we email each other, we need to use a code."
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我當時想，「我不能，我在等一封關於黃金的電子郵件。」所以我想我必須敲它的頭。我不得不得出一個荒謬的結論。所以我制定了一個計劃。我說：“所羅門，我擔心安全問題。當我們互相發送電子郵件時，我們需要使用代碼。”

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I said, "Solomon, I spent all night coming up with this code we need to use in all further correspondence: Lawyer: Gummy Bear. Bank: Cream Egg. Legal: Fizzy Cola Bottle. Claim: Peanut M&Ms. Documents: Jelly Beans. Western Union: A Giant Gummy Lizard." I knew these were all words they use, right?
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我說：「所羅門，我花了一整夜的時間想出這個代碼，我們需要在所有進一步的通信中使用：律師：小熊軟糖。銀行：奶油蛋。法律：碳酸可樂瓶。索賠：花生巧克力豆。文件：果凍豆。西聯匯款：巨型軟糖蜥蜴。」我知道這些都是他們用的詞，對嗎？

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I said, "Please call me Kitkat in all further correspondence." I didn't hear back. I thought, I've gone too far. I've gone too far. So I had to backpedal a little. I said, "Solomon, Is the deal still on? Because you have to be consistent. Then I did get an email back from him.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我說：「在以後的通信中請叫我 Kitkat。」我沒有聽到回應。我想，我已經走得太遠了。我已經走得太遠了。所以我只好稍微退一步。我說，「所羅門，交易還在進行嗎？因為你必須保持一致。然後我確實收到了他的電子郵件。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: He said, "The Business is on and I am trying to blah blah blah ..." I said, "Dude, you have to use the code!" What followed is the greatest email I've ever received. I'm not joking, this is what turned up in my inbox. This was a good day. "The business is on.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 他說，「生意開始了，我正在努力等等……」我說，「夥計，你必須使用代碼！」接下來是我收到過的最棒的電子郵件。我不是開玩笑，這就是我收件匣中出現的內容。這是美好的一天。 「生意還在進行中。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: I am trying to raise the balance for the Gummy Bear -- so he can submit all the needed Fizzy Cola Bottle Jelly Beans to the Creme Egg, for the Peanut M&Ms process to start. Send 1,500 pounds via a Giant Gummy Lizard." And that was so much fun, right, that it got me thinking:
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 我正在努力提高小熊軟糖的餘額，這樣他就可以將所有需要的碳酸可樂瓶果凍豆提交給奶油蛋，以便啟動花生巧克力豆加工過程。透過巨型軟糖蜥蜴發送 1,500 磅。 」這太有趣了，對吧，這讓我想到：

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: replying to as many scam emails as I could? And that's what I've been doing for three years on your behalf. Crazy stuff happens when you start replying to scam emails. It's really difficult, and I highly recommend we do it. I don't think what I'm doing is mean.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 盡可能回覆詐騙電子郵件？這就是我三年來為你們所做的事情。當您開始回覆詐騙電子郵件時，瘋狂的事情就會發生。這確實很難，我強烈建議我們這樣做。我不認為我所做的事是卑鄙的。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: There are a lot of people who do mean things to scammers. All I'm doing is wasting their time. And I think any time they're spending with me is time they're not spending scamming vulnerable adults out of their savings, right? And if you're going to do this -- and I highly recommend you do --
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 有很多人對詐騙者做出卑鄙的事情。我所做的一切都是在浪費他們的時間。我認為他們和我一起度過的任何時間都是他們不會花在騙取弱勢成年人的積蓄上的時間，對嗎？如果你打算這樣做——我強烈建議你這樣做——

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: get yourself a pseudonymous email address. Don't use your own email address. That's what I was doing at the start and it was a nightmare. I'd wake up in the morning and have a thousand emails about penis enlargements, only one of which was a legitimate response -- to a medical question I had.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 為自己取得一個匿名電子郵件地址。不要使用您自己的電子郵件地址。這就是我一開始所做的，那是一場惡夢。我早上醒來時會收到一千封有關陰莖增大的電子郵件，其中只有一封是對我提出的醫療問題的合理答复。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: But I'll tell you what, though, guys, I'll tell you what: any day is a good day, any day is a good day if you receive an email that begins like this: "I AM WINNIE MANDELA, THE SECOND WIFE OF NELSON MANDELA THE FORMER SOUTH AFRICAN PRESIDENT." I was like, oh! -- that Winnie Mandela. I know so many.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 但我會告訴你們，但是，夥計們，我會告訴你們：任何一天都是美好的一天，如果你們收到一封這樣開頭的電子郵件，任何一天都是美好的一天：「我是溫妮·曼德拉，南非前總統納爾遜·曼德拉的第二任妻子。」我當時想，哦！ ——那个温妮·曼德拉。我認識這麼多。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: "I NEED TO TRANSFER 45 MILLION DOLLARS OUT OF THE COUNTRY BECAUSE OF MY HUSBAND NELSON MANDELA'S HEALTH CONDITION." Let that sink in. She sent me this, which is hysterical. And this looks fairly legitimate, this is a letter of authorization.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 「由於我丈夫納爾遜曼德拉的健康狀況，我需要將 4500 萬美元轉出國外。」讓我們理解這一點。她給我發了這個，這太歇斯底里了。這看起來相當合法，這是一封授權書。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: But to be honest, if there's nothing written on it, it's just a shape! I said, "Winnie, I'm really sorry to hear of this. Given that Nelson died three months ago, I'd describe his health condition as fairly serious." That's the worst health condition you can have, not being alive.
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 但說實話，如果上面什麼都沒有寫的話，那就只是一個形狀而已！我說：「溫妮，聽到這個消息我真的很難過。考慮到尼爾森三個月前去世了，我認為他的健康狀況相當嚴重。」這是你可能遇到的最糟糕的健康狀況，而不是活著。

julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.en: She said, "KINDLY COMPLY WITH MY BANKERS INSTRUCTIONS. I said, "Of course. NO WOMAN, NO CRY." She said, "MY BANKER WILL NEED TRANSFER OF 3000 DOLLARS. ONE LOVE." I said, "no problemo. I SHOT THE SHERIFF." [ (BUT I DID NOT SHOOT THE DEPUTY) ] Notifications alt+T
julian_treasure_how_to_speak_so_that_peo.zh: 她說：「請遵守我的銀行家指示。我說：」當然。不，女人，不哭。 」她說，「我的銀行家需要轉帳 3000 美元。一個愛。 」我說，「沒問題。我射殺了警長。 」 [（但我沒有射殺副警長）] 通知 alt+T

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: The human voice: It's the instrument we all play. It's the most powerful sound in the world, probably. It's the only one that can start a war or say "I love you." And yet many people have the experience that when they speak, people don't listen to them. And why is that?
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 人聲：這是我們大家演奏的樂器。這可能是世界上最有力的聲音。它是唯一一個可以發動戰爭或說「我愛你」的人。然而，許多人都有這樣的經驗：當他們說話時，人們不聽他們的話。這是為什麼呢？

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: How can we speak powerfully to make change in the world? What I'd like to suggest, there are a number of habits that we need to move away from. I've assembled for your pleasure here seven deadly sins of speaking. I'm not pretending this is an exhaustive list,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我們怎樣才能發出有力的聲音來改變世界？我想建議的是，我們需要改掉很多習慣。為了讓您高興，我在這裡整理了七種言語的致命罪。我並不是假裝這是一份詳盡的清單，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: but these seven, I think, are pretty large habits that we can all fall into. Speaking ill of somebody who's not present. Not a nice habit, and we know perfectly well the person gossiping, five minutes later, will be gossiping about us. Second, judging.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 但我認為，這七個習慣是我們所有人都可能養成的。說不在場的人的壞話。這不是一個好習慣，我們非常清楚那個八卦的人，五分鐘後，就會八卦我們。第二，判斷。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: We know people who are like this in conversation, and it's very hard to listen to somebody if you know that you're being judged and found wanting at the same time. Third, negativity. You can fall into this. My mother, in the last years of her life, became very negative, and it's hard to listen.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我們知道有人在談話中是這樣的，如果你知道自己在被評判的同時又被認為有缺陷，那麼你就很難傾聽別人的意見。第三，消極情緒。你可能會陷入這樣的境地。我的母親在她生命的最後幾年變得非常消極，很難傾聽。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: I remember one day, I said to her, "It's October 1 today," and she said, "I know, isn't it dreadful?" It's hard to listen when somebody's that negative. And another form of negativity, complaining. Well, this is the national art of the U.K. It's our national sport.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 記得有一天，我對她說：「今天是10月1日。」她說：「我知道，這不是很可怕嗎？」當某人如此消極時，很難傾聽。另一種形式的負面情緒就是抱怨。嗯，這是英國的民族藝術，也是我們的國家運動。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: We complain about the weather, sport, about politics, about everything, but actually, complaining is viral misery. It's not spreading sunshine and lightness in the world. We've all met this guy. Maybe we've all been this guy. Some people have a blamethrower. They just pass it on to everybody else
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我們抱怨天氣、體育、政治、一切，但實際上，抱怨是一種病毒式的痛苦。它並沒有在世界上傳播陽光和光明。我們都見過這個人。也許我們都曾經是這樣的人。有些人有一個指責者。他們只是把它傳遞給其他人

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: and don't take responsibility for their actions, and again, hard to listen to somebody who is being like that. Penultimate, the sixth of the seven, embroidery, exaggeration. It demeans our language, actually, sometimes. For example, if I see something that really is awesome, what do I call it?
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 並且不對他們的行為負責，再說一次，很難傾聽這樣的人的聲音。倒數第二個，七個中的第六個，刺繡，誇張。事實上，有時它貶低了我們的語言。例如，如果我看到一些非常棒的東西，我該怎麼稱呼它？

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: And then, of course, this exaggeration becomes lying, and we don't want to listen to people we know are lying to us. And finally, dogmatism. The confusion of facts with opinions. When those two things get conflated, you're listening into the wind.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 然後，當然，這種誇張就變成了謊言，我們不想聽那些我們知道對我們撒謊的人。最後，教條主義。事實與觀點的混淆。當這兩件事混為一談時，你就會聽到風聲。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: You know, somebody is bombarding you with their opinions as if they were true. It's difficult to listen to that. So here they are, seven deadly sins of speaking. These are things I think we need to avoid. But is there a positive way to think about this?
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 你知道，有人用他們的觀點轟炸你，就好像它們是真的一樣。聽這個很難。這就是言語的七大罪。我認為這些是我們需要避免的事情。但有沒有一種積極的方式來思考這個問題呢？

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: I'd like to suggest that there are four really powerful cornerstones, foundations, that we can stand on if we want our speech to be powerful and to make change in the world. Fortunately, these things spell a word. The word is "hail," and it has a great definition as well.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我想建議的是，如果我們希望我們的演講具有力量並改變世界，我們可以依靠四個真正強大的基石和基礎。幸運的是，這些東西拼成一個字。這個詞是“冰雹”，它也有一個很好的定義。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: I'm not talking about the stuff that falls from the sky and hits you on the head. I'm talking about this definition, to greet or acclaim enthusiastically, which is how I think our words will be received if we stand on these four things. So what do they stand for? See if you can guess.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我說的不是那些從天上掉下來砸到你頭上的東西。我說的這個定義是熱情地打招呼或喝采，我想如果我們站在這四件事上，我們的話就會被接受。那麼它們代表什麼？看看你能不能猜到。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: The H, honesty, of course, being true in what you say, being straight and clear. The A is authenticity, just being yourself. A friend of mine described it as standing in your own truth, which I think is a lovely way to put it. The I is integrity, being your word, actually doing what you say,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: H，當然是誠實，指的是你所說的話是真實的、直截了當、清晰的。 A 是真實性，就是做自己。我的一位朋友將其描述為堅持自己的真理，我認為這是一個可愛的表達方式。我就是正直，言出必行，言行一致，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: and being somebody people can trust. And the L is love. I don't mean romantic love, but I do mean wishing people well, for two reasons. First of all, I think absolute honesty may not be what we want. I mean, my goodness, you look ugly this morning. Perhaps that's not necessary.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 並成為人們可以信任的人。 L是愛。我不是指浪漫的愛情，而是指祝福人們一切順利，原因有兩個。首先，我認為絕對的誠實可能不是我們想要的。我是說，天哪，你今天早上看起來很醜。也許那沒有必要。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Tempered with love, of course, honesty is a great thing. But also, if you're really wishing somebody well, it's very hard to judge them at the same time. I'm not even sure you can do those two things simultaneously. Also, now that's what you say, and it's like the old song, it is what you say,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 當然，用愛來磨練誠實是一件好事。而且，如果你真的希望某人好，就很難同時評判他們。我什至不確定你是否可以同時做這兩件事。而且，現在這就是你所說的，就像那首老歌一樣，這就是你所說的，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: it's also the way that you say it. You have an amazing toolbox. This instrument is incredible, and yet this is a toolbox that very few people have ever opened. I'd like to have a little rummage in there with you now and just pull a few tools out that you might like to take away and play with,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 這也是你說話的方式。你有一個很棒的工具箱。這個工具令人難以置信，但這是一個很少有人打開過的工具箱。我現在想和你一起在那裡翻找一下，然後拿出一些你可能想拿走玩的工具，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: which will increase the power of your speaking. Register, for example. Now, falsetto register may not be very useful most of the time, but there's a register in between. I'm not going to get very technical about this for any of you who are voice coaches. You can locate your voice, however.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 這會增加你說話的力量。例如，註冊。現在，假音音域在大多數情況下可能不是很有用，但在兩者之間有一個音域。對於你們中的任何一位語音教練，我不會對此進行非常技術性的討論。不過，您可以找到您的聲音。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: So if I talk up here in my nose, you can hear the difference. If I go down here in my throat, which is where most of us speak from most of the time. But if you want weight, you need to go down here to the chest. You hear the difference? We vote for politicians with lower voices, it's true,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 所以如果我用鼻子說話，你就能聽出其中的差異。如果我把喉嚨放到喉嚨裡，那是我們大多數人大部分時間說話的地方。但如果你想要重量，你需要從這裡到胸部。你聽出差異了嗎？我們投票給聲音較低的政客，這是事實，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: because we associate depth with power and with authority. That's register. Then we have timbre. It's the way your voice feels. Again, the research shows that we prefer voices which are rich, smooth, warm, Well if that's not you, that's not the end of the world, because you can train.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 因為我們將深度與力量和權威連結在一起。那就是註冊。然後我們就有音色了。這就是你的聲音的感覺。研究再次表明，我們更喜歡豐富、流暢、溫暖的聲音，如果那不是你，那也不是世界末日，因為你可以訓練。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Go and get a voice coach. And there are amazing things you can do with breathing, with posture, and with exercises to improve the timbre of your voice. Then prosody. I love prosody. This is the sing-song, the meta-language that we use in order to impart meaning.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 去找個聲樂教練吧。您可以透過呼吸、姿勢和練習來實現一些令人驚奇的事情來改善您的音質。然後是韻律。我喜歡韻律。這就是歌曲，我們用來傳遞意義的後設語言。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: It's root one for meaning in conversation. People who speak all on one note are really quite hard to listen to if they don't have any prosody at all. That's where the word "monotonic" comes from, or monotonous, monotone. Also, we have repetitive prosody now coming in,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 它是對話中意義的根源之一。那些只用一個音符說話的人，如果沒有任何韻律，真的很難聽。這就是「單調」一詞的由來，或是單調、單調。另外，我們現在有重複的韻律，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: where every sentence ends as if it were a question when it's actually not a question, it's a statement? And if you repeat that one, it's actually restricting your ability to communicate through prosody, which I think is a shame, so let's try and break that habit.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 每個句子的結尾都好像是一個問題，但實際上它不是一個問題，而是一個陳述？如果你重複這個，它實際上限制了你透過韻律溝通的能力，我認為這是一種恥辱，所以讓我們試著打破這個習慣。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: I can get very excited by saying something really quickly, or I can slow right down to emphasize, and at the end of that, of course, is our old friend silence. There's nothing wrong with a bit of silence in a talk, is there? We don't have to fill it with ums and ahs. It can be very powerful.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我可以透過非常快地說一些事情而變得非常興奮，或者我可以放慢速度來強調，最後，當然，是我們的老朋友沉默。談話中保持一點沉默並沒有什麼問題，不是嗎？我們不必用“嗯”和“啊”來填充它。它可以非常強大。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Of course, pitch often goes along with pace to indicate arousal, but you can do it just with pitch. Where did you leave my keys? (Higher pitch) Where did you leave my keys? So, slightly different meaning in those two deliveries. And finally, volume. (Loud) I can get really excited by using volume.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 當然，音調通常與節奏一起表示喚醒，但您可以僅使用音調來做到這一點。你把我的鑰匙留在哪裡了？ （更高的音調）你把我的鑰匙放在哪裡了？因此，這兩次交付的含義略有不同。最後，音量。 （大聲）使用音量我會感到非常興奮。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Sorry about that, if I startled anybody. Or, I can have you really pay attention by getting very quiet. Some people broadcast the whole time. Try not to do that. That's called sodcasting, Imposing your sound on people around you carelessly and inconsiderately.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 抱歉，如果我嚇到了任何人。或者，我可以透過保持安靜來讓你真正集中註意力。有些人一直在廣播。盡量不要這樣做。這就是所謂的 sodcasting，不小心、不顧一切地將你的聲音強加給你周圍的人。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Of course, where this all comes into play most of all is when you've got something really important to do. It might be standing on a stage like this and giving a talk to people. It might be proposing marriage, asking for a raise, a wedding speech. Whatever it is, if it's really important,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 當然，這一切最重要的是當你有一些非常重要的事情要做時。它可能會站在這樣的舞台上向人們演講。可能是求婚、要求加薪、婚禮致詞。不管是什麼，只要它真的很重要，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: you owe it to yourself to look at this toolbox and the engine that it's going to work on, and no engine works well without being warmed up. Warm up your voice. Actually, let me show you how to do that. Would you all like to stand up for a moment? I'm going to show you
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 你應該檢查一下這個工具箱和它將要工作的引擎，沒有一個引擎在沒有預熱的情況下可以很好地工作。溫暖你的聲音。實際上，讓我向您展示如何做到這一點。大家願意站起來一會兒嗎？我要給你看

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: the six vocal warm-up exercises that I do before every talk I ever do. Any time you're going to talk to anybody important, do these. First, arms up, deep breath in, and sigh out, ahhhhh, like that. Ahhhh, very good. Now we're going to warm up our lips, and we're going to go Ba, Ba, Ba, Ba,
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我在每次演講之前都會做六次聲音熱身練習。每當你要與重要的人交談時，請執行以下操作。首先，雙臂舉起，深吸一口氣，然後呼出，啊啊啊，就這樣。啊啊啊，非常好。現在我們要預熱我們的嘴唇，然後我們要Ba，Ba，Ba，Ba，

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Ba, Ba, Ba, Ba. Very good. And now, brrrrrrrrrr, just like when you were a kid. Brrrr. Now your lips should be coming alive. We're going to do the tongue next with exaggerated la, la, la, la, la, la, la, la, la. Beautiful. You're getting really good at this. And then, roll an R. Rrrrrrr.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 巴，巴，巴，巴。非常好。現在，brrrrrrrrrrr，就像你小時候一樣。嗚嗚嗚。現在你的嘴唇應該活躍起來了。接下來我們要用誇張的「啦，啦，啦，啦，啦，啦，啦，啦，啦」來練習舌頭。美麗的。你在這方面已經做得非常好了。然後，擲 R。 Rrrrrrr。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: That's like champagne for the tongue. Finally, and if I can only do one, the pros call this the siren. It's really good. It starts with "we" and goes to "aw." The "we" is high, the "aw" is low. So you go, weeeaawww, weeeaawww. Fantastic. Give yourselves a round of applause. Take a seat, thank you.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 這就像舌頭上的香檳。最後，如果我只能做一件事，專業人士稱之為警報。真的很好。它以“我們”開頭，然後到“哦”。 「我們」高，「aw」低。所以你走吧，weeeaawww，weeeaawww。極好的。給自己熱烈的掌聲。請坐，謝謝。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: Next time you speak, do those in advance. Now let me just put this in context to close. This is a serious point here. This is where we are now, right? We speak not very well to people who simply aren't listening in an environment that's all about noise and bad acoustics.
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 下次講話時，請提前做好這些工作。現在讓我把這個放在上下文中結束。這是一個嚴肅的問題。這就是我們現在所處的位置，對吧？我們對那些在充滿噪音和糟糕音響效果的環境中根本不聽話的人說話不太好。

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: I have talked about that on this stage in different phases. What would the world be like if we were speaking powerfully to people who were listening consciously in environments which were actually fit for purpose? Or to make that a bit larger, what would the world be like
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 我在這個舞台上分不同階段講過這個問題。如果我們對那些在真正適合目的的環境中有意識地傾聽的人進行強有力的講話，世界會是什麼樣子？或者把它放大一點，世界會是什麼樣子

dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.en: if we were creating sound consciously and consuming sound consciously and designing all our environments consciously for sound? That would be a world that does sound beautiful, and one where understanding would be the norm, and that is an idea worth spreading. Notifications alt+T
dan_pink_the_puzzle_of_motivation.zh: 如果我們有意識地創造聲音、有意識地消費聲音、有意識地為聲音設計我們所有的環境呢？那將是一個聽起來確實很美麗的世界，一個理解將成為常態的世界，這是一個值得傳播的想法。通知 alt+T

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: I need to make a confession at the outset here. A little over 20 years ago, I did something that I regret, something that I'm not particularly proud of. Something that, in many ways, I wish no one would ever know, but here I feel kind of obliged to reveal. In the late 1980s,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 我首先需要在這裡坦白。二十多年前，我做了一些讓我後悔的事情，一些我並不特別自豪的事。從很多方面來說，我希望沒有人知道這一點，但在這裡我覺得有必要透露。在 20 世紀 80 年代末，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: in a moment of youthful indiscretion, I went to law school. In America, law is a professional degree: after your university degree, you go on to law school. When I got to law school, I didn't do very well. To put it mildly, I didn't do very well.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 在年輕時的一次不檢點的時刻，我進入了法學院。在美國，法律是一個專業學位：獲得大學學位後，您可以繼續進入法學院。當我進入法學院時，我的成績並不好。溫和地說，我做得不太好。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: I, in fact, graduated in the part of my law school class that made the top 90% possible. I never practiced law a day in my life; I pretty much wasn't allowed to. But today, against my better judgment, against the advice of my own wife, I want to try to dust off some of those legal skills --
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 事實上，我在法學院課程中畢業的那部分課程使前 90% 成為可能。我一生中從未從事過法律工作。我幾乎不被允許這樣做。但今天，根據我更好的判斷，根據我自己妻子的建議，我想嘗試重新掌握一些法律技能——

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: what's left of those legal skills. I don't want to tell you a story. I want to make a case. I want to make a hard-headed, evidence-based, dare I say lawyerly case, for rethinking how we run our businesses. So, ladies and gentlemen of the jury, take a look at this. This is called the candle problem.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 這些法律技能還剩下什麼。我不想跟你講故事。我想做一個案例。我想提出一個冷靜的、基於證據的、我敢說是律師的案例，以重新思考我們如何經營我們的業務。那麼，陪審團的女士們先生們，請看一下這個。這就是所謂的蠟燭問題。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Some of you might know it. It's created in 1945 by a psychologist named Karl Duncker. He created this experiment that is used in many other experiments in behavioral science. And here's how it works. Suppose I'm the experimenter. I bring you into a room.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 你們有些人可能知道。它是由一位名叫卡爾鄧克 (Karl Duncker) 的心理學家於 1945 年創建的。他創建的這個實驗被用於行為科學的許多其他實驗。這是它的工作原理。假設我是實驗者。我帶你進一個房間。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: I give you a candle, some thumbtacks and some matches. And I say to you, "Your job is to attach the candle to the wall so the wax doesn't drip onto the table." Now what would you do? Many people begin trying to thumbtack the candle to the wall. I saw somebody kind of make the motion over here --
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 我給你一支蠟燭、一些圖釘和一些火柴。我對你說，「你的工作就是把蠟燭固定在牆上，這樣蠟就不會滴到桌上。」現在你會怎麼做？許多人開始嘗試將蠟燭用圖釘釘在牆上。我看到有人在這裡提出動議——

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: some people have a great idea where they light the match, melt the side of the candle, try to adhere it to the wall. It's an awesome idea. Doesn't work. And eventually, after five or ten minutes, most people figure out the solution, which you can see here.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 有些人有一個好主意，他們點燃火柴，融化蠟燭的一側，然後嘗試將其粘附在牆上。這是一個很棒的主意。不起作用。最終，五到十分鐘後，大多數人都會找到解決方案，您可以在此處查看。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: The key is to overcome what's called functional fixedness. You look at that box and you see it only as a receptacle for the tacks. But it can also have this other function, as a platform for the candle. The candle problem. I want to tell you about an experiment using the candle problem,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 關鍵是要克服所謂的功能固定性。你看那個盒子，你只會看到它是大頭釘的容器。但它還可以具有其他功能，作為蠟燭的平台。蠟燭問題。我想告訴你一個使用蠟燭問題的實驗，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: done by a scientist named Sam Glucksberg, who is now at Princeton University, US, This shows the power of incentives. He gathered his participants and said: "I'm going to time you, how quickly you can solve this problem." To one group he said, "I'm going to time you to establish norms,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 由美國普林斯頓大學科學家薩姆·格魯克斯伯格（Sam Glucksberg）完成，這顯示了激勵的力量。他召集了參與​​者並說：「我要給你們計時，看看你們能多快解決這個問題。」他對一群人說：「我會安排你們建立規範，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: averages for how long it typically takes someone to solve this sort of problem." To the second group he offered rewards. He said, "If you're in the top 25% of the fastest times, you get five dollars. If you're the fastest of everyone we're testing here today, you get 20 dollars."
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 他向第二組提供了獎勵。他說，“如果你在最快時間中名列前 25%，你將獲得 5 美元。如果你是我們今天測試的所有人中最快的，你將獲得 20 美元。”

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Now this is several years ago, adjusted for inflation, it's a decent sum of money for a few minutes of work. It's a nice motivator. How much faster did this group solve the problem? It took them, on average, three and a half minutes longer. 3.5 min longer. This makes no sense, right?
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 現在這是幾年前的事了，根據通貨膨脹進行調整，對於幾分鐘的工作來說這是一筆不錯的錢。這是一個很好的激勵因素。該小組解決問題的速度有多快？他們平均多花了三分半鐘。多了 3.5 分鐘。這沒有意義，對吧？

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: I mean, I'm an American. I believe in free markets. That's not how it's supposed to work, right? If you want people to perform better, you reward them. Right? Bonuses, commissions, their own reality show. Incentivize them. That's how business works. But that's not happening here.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 我的意思是，我是美國人。我相信自由市場。這不是它應該如何工作的，對吧？如果你希望人們表現得更好，你就獎勵他們。正確的？獎金、佣金、他們自己的真人秀。激勵他們。商業就是這樣運作的。但這裡並沒有發生這種情況。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: You've got an incentive designed to sharpen thinking and accelerate creativity, and it does just the opposite. It dulls thinking and blocks creativity. What's interesting about this experiment is that it's not an aberration. This has been replicated over and over again for nearly 40 years.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 你有一個旨在提高思維和加速創造力的激勵措施，但它的作用恰恰相反。它使思維遲鈍並阻礙創造力。這個實驗的有趣之處在於它不是一個畸變。近40年來，這一點被一再複製。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: These contingent motivators -- if you do this, then you get that -- work in some circumstances. But for a lot of tasks, they actually either don't work or, often, they do harm. This is one of the most robust findings in social science, and also one of the most ignored.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 這些偶然的激勵因素——如果你這樣做，那麼你就會得到那個——在某些情況下會起作用。但對於許多任務來說，它們實際上要不是不起作用，就是常常有害。這是社會科學中最有力的發現之一，也是最容易被忽略的發現之一。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: I spent the last couple of years looking at the science of human motivation, particularly the dynamics of extrinsic motivators and intrinsic motivators. And I'm telling you, it's not even close. If you look at the science, there is a mismatch between what science knows and what business does.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 過去幾年我一直在研究人類動機的科學，特別是外在動機和內在動機的動態。我告訴你，這還差得很遠。如果你檢視科學，你會發現科學知識與商業行為之間有不匹配。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: What's alarming here is that our business operating system -- think of the set of assumptions and protocols beneath our businesses, how we motivate people, how we apply our human resources-- it's built entirely around these extrinsic motivators, around carrots and sticks.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 這裡令人擔憂的是，我們的業務作業系統——想想我們業務背後的一系列假設和協議，我們如何激勵人們，我們如何應用我們的人力資源——它完全是圍繞這些外在激勵因素、胡蘿蔔加大棒建立的。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: That's actually fine for many kinds of 20th century tasks. But for 21st century tasks, that mechanistic, reward-and-punishment approach often doesn't work, and often does harm. Let me show you. Glucksberg did another similar experiment, he presented the problem in a slightly different way,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 對於 20 世紀的許多任務來說，這實際上已經足夠了。但對於 21 世紀的任務，這種機械式的獎懲方法往往不起作用，而且常常有害。讓我告訴你。 Glucksberg 做了另一個類似的實驗，他以稍微不同的方式提出了這個問題，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Attach the candle to the wall so the wax doesn't drip onto the table. Same deal. You: we're timing for norms. You: we're incentivizing. What happened this time? This time, the incentivized group kicked the other group's butt. Because when the tacks are out of the box, it's pretty easy isn't it?
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 將蠟燭固定在牆上，這樣蠟就不會滴到桌子上。同樣的交易。你：我們正在製定規範。你：我們正在激勵。這次發生了什麼事？這一次，受到激勵的小組打敗了另一組。因為當圖釘開箱即用時，這很容易，不是嗎？

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: If-then rewards work really well for those sorts of tasks, where there is a simple set of rules and a clear destination to go to. Rewards, by their very nature, narrow our focus, concentrate the mind; that's why they work in so many cases. So, for tasks like this,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 如果-那麼獎勵對於這類任務非常有效，因為這些任務有一套簡單的規則和明確的目的地。獎勵，就其本質而言，可以縮小我們的注意力，集中註意力；這就是為什麼它們在許多情況下都有效。所以，對於這樣的任務，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: a narrow focus, where you just see the goal right there, zoom straight ahead to it, they work really well. But for the real candle problem, you don't want to be looking like this. The solution is on the periphery. You want to be looking around. That reward actually narrows our focus
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 一個狹窄的焦點，你只能看到目標就在那裡，直接向目標前進，它們的效果非常好。但對於真正的蠟燭問題，你不希望看起來像這樣。解決方案就在外圍。你想要四處看看。這種獎勵實際上縮小了我們的注意力

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: and restricts our possibility. Let me tell you why this is so important. In western Europe, in many parts of Asia, in North America, in Australia, white-collar workers are doing less of this kind of work, and more of this kind of work. That routine, rule-based, left-brain work --
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 並限制了我們的可能性。讓我告訴你為什麼這麼重要。在西歐、亞洲很多地方、北美、澳大利亞，白領做這種工作越來越少，越來越多。這種例行公事、基於規則的左腦工作——

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: certain kinds of accounting, financial analysis, computer programming -- has become fairly easy to outsource, fairly easy to automate. Software can do it faster. Low-cost providers can do it cheaper. So what really matters are the more right-brained creative, conceptual kinds of abilities.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 某些類型的會計、財務分析、電腦程式設計——已經變得相當容易外包，相當容易實現自動化。軟體可以更快地做到這一點。低成本提供者可以更便宜地做到這一點。因此，真正重要的是右腦創造性、概念性的能力。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Think about your own work. Think about your own work. Are the problems that you face, or even the problems we've been talking about here, do they have a clear set of rules, and a single solution? No. The rules are mystifying. The solution, if it exists at all, is surprising and not obvious.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 想想你自己的工作。想想你自己的工作。您面臨的問題，甚至我們在這裡討論的問題，是否有一套明確的規則和單一的解決方案？不，規則很神秘。如果這個解決方案確實存在的話，那也是令人驚訝的，而且並不明顯。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Everybody in this room is dealing with their own version of the candle problem. And for candle problems of any kind, those if-then rewards, the things around which we've built so many of our businesses, It makes me crazy. And here's the thing. This is not a feeling.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 這個房間裡的每個人都在處理他們自己版本的蠟燭問題。對於任何類型的蠟燭問題，那些如果-那麼獎勵，我們圍繞這些事情建立瞭如此多的業務，這讓我發瘋。事情是這樣的。這不是一種感覺。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Okay? I'm a lawyer; I don't believe in feelings. This is not a philosophy. I'm an American; I don't believe in philosophy. This is a fact -- or, as we say in my hometown of Washington, D.C., Let me give you an example. Let me marshal the evidence here. I'm not telling a story, I'm making a case.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 好的？我是律師；我不相信感情。這不是一種哲學。我是美國人；我不相信哲學。這是事實——或者，正如我們在我的家鄉華盛頓特區所說的那樣，讓我給你舉個例子。讓我整理一下這裡的證據。我不是說故事，我是在講一個案例。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, some evidence: Dan Ariely, one of the great economists of our time, he and three colleagues did a study of some MIT students. They gave these MIT students a bunch of games, games that involved creativity, and motor skills, and concentration.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 陪審團的女士們、先生們，請提供一些證據：丹·艾瑞利 (Dan Ariely)，我們這個時代最偉大的經濟學家之一，他和三位同事對麻省理工學院的一些學生進行了一項研究。他們給這些麻省理工學院的學生提供了一堆遊戲，這些遊戲涉及創造力、運動技能和注意力。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: And the offered them, for performance, three levels of rewards: small reward, medium reward, large reward. If you do really well you get the large reward, on down. As long as the task involved only mechanical skill bonuses worked as they would be expected:
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 根據績效，他們提供了三個等級的獎勵：小獎勵、中獎勵、大獎勵。如果你做得很好，你會得到豐厚的獎勵。只要任務只涉及機械技能獎勵，就會如預期發揮作用：

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: the higher the pay, the better the performance. But once the task called for even rudimentary cognitive skill, a larger reward led to poorer performance. Then they said, "Let's see if there's any cultural bias here. Let's go to Madurai, India and test it." Standard of living is lower.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 薪資越高，績效越好。但一旦任務需要基本的認知技能，更大的獎勵就會導致表現更差。然後他們說：「讓我們看看這裡是否存在文化偏見。我們去印度馬杜賴測試一下。」生活水平較低。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: In Madurai, a reward that is modest in North American standards, is more meaningful there. Same deal. A bunch of games, three levels of rewards. People offered the medium level of rewards did no better than people offered the small rewards. But this time, people offered the highest rewards,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 在馬杜賴，以北美標準來說適度的獎勵在那裡更有意義。同樣的交易。一堆遊戲，三個等級的獎勵。提供中等獎勵的人並不比提供小獎勵的人做得更好。但這一次，人們給了最高的獎勵，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: they did the worst of all. In eight of the nine tasks we examined across three experiments, higher incentives led to worse performance. Is this some kind of touchy-feely socialist conspiracy going on here? No, these are economists from MIT, from Carnegie Mellon, from the University of Chicago.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 他們做了最糟糕的事。在我們透過三個實驗檢查的九項任務中的八項中，更高的激勵導致了更差的表現。這是某種敏感的社會主義陰謀嗎？不，這些是來自麻省理工學院、卡內基美隆大學、芝加哥大學的經濟學家。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Do you know who sponsored this research? The Federal Reserve Bank of the United States. That's the American experience. Let's go across the pond to the London School of Economics, LSE, London School of Economics, alma mater of eleven Nobel Laureates in economics.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 你知道誰贊助了這項研究嗎？美國聯邦儲備銀行。這就是美國的經驗。讓我們跨越大洋，來到倫敦政經學院（LSE），倫敦政經學院，十一位諾貝爾經濟學獎得主的母校。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Training ground for great economic thinkers and Mick Jagger. just last month, economists at LSE looked at 51 studies of pay-for-performance plans, inside of companies. Here's what they said: "We find that financial incentives can result in a negative impact on overall performance."
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 偉大的經濟思想家和米克·賈格爾的訓練場。就在上個月，倫敦經濟學院的經濟學家研究了 51 項有關公司內部績效薪酬計畫的研究。他們是這樣說的：“我們發現財務激勵可能會對整體績效產生負面影響。”

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: There is a mismatch between what science knows and what business does. And what worries me, as we stand here in the rubble of the economic collapse, is that too many organizations are making their decisions, their policies about talent and people, based on assumptions that are outdated,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 科學知識與商業行為之間存在不匹配。當我們站在經濟崩潰的廢墟中時，令我擔心的是，太多的組織正在根據過時的假設做出有關人才和人員的決策、政策，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: and rooted more in folklore than in science. And if we really want to get out of this economic mess, if we really want high performance on those definitional tasks of the 21st century, the solution is not to do more of the wrong things, to entice people with a sweeter carrot,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 並且更多地植根於民間傳說而不是科學。如果我們真的想擺脫這場經濟混亂，如果我們真的想在 21 世紀的那些定義性任務上取得優異的成績，解決方案不是做更多錯誤的事情，而是用更甜的胡蘿蔔來吸引人們，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: or threaten them with a sharper stick. We need a whole new approach. The good news is that the scientists who've been studying motivation have given us this new approach. It's built much more around intrinsic motivation. Around the desire to do things because they matter,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 或用更鋒利的棍子威脅他們。我們需要一種全新的方法。好消息是，一直在研究動機的科學家為我們提供了這種新方法。它更多地圍繞著內在動機而建立。圍繞著因為重要而做事的願望，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: because we like it, they're interesting, or part of something important. And to my mind, that new operating system for our businesses revolves around three elements: autonomy, mastery and purpose. Autonomy: the urge to direct our own lives.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 因為我們喜歡它，它們很有趣，或是重要事物的一部分。在我看來，我們企業的新作業系統圍繞著三個要素：自主、掌握、目標。自主：指導我們自己人生的衝動。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Mastery: the desire to get better and better at something that matters. Purpose: the yearning to do what we do in the service of something larger than ourselves. These are the building blocks of an entirely new operating system for our businesses. I want to talk today only about autonomy.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 精通：渴望在重要的事情上變得越來越好。目的：渴望為比我們自己更偉大的事物服務。這些是我們企業全新作業系統的構建塊。我今天只想談自治。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: In the 20th century, we came up with this idea of management. Management did not emanate from nature. Management is not a tree, it's a television set. Somebody invented it. It doesn't mean it's going to work forever. Management is great. Traditional notions of management are great
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 在20世紀，我們提出了這個管理理念。管理並非源自於自然。管理不是一棵樹，而是一台電視。有人發明了它。這並不意味著它會永遠有效。管理很棒。傳統的管理理念是偉大的

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: if you want compliance. But if you want engagement, self-direction works better. Some examples of some kind of radical notions of self-direction. You don't see a lot of it, but you see the first stirrings of something really interesting going on,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 如果你想要合規。但如果你想要參與，自我指導效果會更好。一些自我指導的激進概念的例子。你看不到很多，但你會看到一些真正有趣的事情正在發生，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: what it means is paying people adequately and fairly, absolutely -- getting the issue of money off the table, and then giving people lots of autonomy. How many of you have heard of the company Atlassian? It looks like less than half. Atlassian is an Australian software company.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 這意味著給人們足夠的、公平的、絕對的報酬──把金錢問題排除在外，然後給予人們很大的自主權。你們當中有多少人聽過 Atlassian 公司？看起來還不到一半。 Atlassian 是一家澳洲軟體公司。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: And they do something incredibly cool. A few times a year they tell their engineers, "Go for the next 24 hours and work on anything you want, as long as it's not part of your regular job. Work on anything you want." Engineers use this time to come up with a cool patch for code,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 他們做了一些非常酷的事情。他們每年都會告訴工程師幾次，「在接下來的 24 小時裡，做任何你想做的事情，只要它不是你常規工作的一部分。做任何你想做的事情。」工程師利用這段時間為程式碼提供了一個很酷的補丁，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: come up with an elegant hack. Then they present all of the stuff that they've developed to their teammates, to the rest of the company, in this wild and woolly all-hands meeting at the end of the day. Being Australians, everybody has a beer. They call them FedEx Days.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 想出一個優雅的技巧。然後，他們在一天結束時舉行的瘋狂全體員工會議上，向隊友和公司其他成員展示他們自己開發的所有產品。作為澳洲人，每個人都喝啤酒。他們稱之為聯邦快遞日。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Because you have to deliver something overnight. It's pretty; not bad. It's a huge trademark violation, but it's pretty clever. That one day of intense autonomy has produced a whole array of software fixes that might never have existed.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 因為你必須連夜交付一些東西。很漂亮；不錯。這是一個巨大的商標侵權行為，但它非常聰明。高度自治的那一天產生了一系列可能從未存在過的軟體修復。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: It's worked so well that Atlassian has taken it to the next level with 20% time -- done, famously, at Google -- where engineers can spend 20% of their time working on anything they want. They have autonomy over their time, their task, their team, their technique. Radical amounts of autonomy.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 它的效果非常好，以至於 Atlassian 以 20% 的時間將其提升到了一個新的水平——眾所周知，谷歌就是這樣做的——工程師可以用 20% 的時間做他們想做的任何事情。他們對自己的時間、任務、團隊和技術擁有自主權。極大的自主權。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: And at Google, as many of you know, about half of the new products in a typical year are birthed during that 20% time: things like Gmail, Orkut, Google News. Let me give you an even more radical example of it: something called the Results Only Work Environment (the ROWE),
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 正如許多人所知，在 Google，每年大約有一半的新產品是在這 20% 的時間內誕生的：例如 Gmail、Orkut、Google News。讓我給你一個更激進的例子：稱為「只注重成果的工作環境」（ROWE），

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: created by two American consultants, in place at a dozen companies around North America. In a ROWE people don't have schedules. They show up when they want. They don't have to be in the office at a certain time, or any time. They just have to get their work done.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 由兩名美國顧問創建，在北美各地的十幾家公司都有分佈。在 ROWE 中，人們沒有日程安排。他們會在需要的時候出現。他們不必在特定時間或任何時間出現在辦公室。他們只需要完成他們的工作。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: How they do it, when they do it, where they do it, is totally up to them. Meetings in these kinds of environments are optional. Almost across the board, productivity goes up, worker engagement goes up, worker satisfaction goes up, turnover goes down. Autonomy, mastery and purpose,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 他們如何做、何時做、在哪裡做，完全取決於他們。在此類環境中召開會議是可選的。幾乎所有方面，生產力都提高了，員工敬業度提高了，員工滿意度提高了，人員流動率下降了。自主、掌握和目標，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: the building blocks of a new way of doing things. Some of you might look at this and say, "Hmm, that sounds nice, but it's Utopian." And I say, "Nope. The mid-1990s, Microsoft started an encyclopedia called Encarta. They had deployed all the right incentives,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 新的做事方式的基石。你們中的一些人可能會看到這個並說：「嗯，這聽起來不錯，但這是烏托邦。」我說：「不。20 世紀 90 年代中期，微軟推出了一本名為 Encarta 的百科全書。他們採取了所有正確的激勵措施，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: They paid professionals to write and edit thousands of articles. Well-compensated managers oversaw the whole thing to make sure it came in on budget and on time. A few years later, another encyclopedia got started. Different model, right? No one gets paid a cent, or a euro or a yen.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 他們聘請專業人員撰寫和編輯數千篇文章。薪酬豐厚的經理負責監督整個過程，以確保專案按時按預算完成。幾年後，另一部百科全書開始出版。不同型號吧？沒有人得到一分錢、一歐元或一日元的報酬。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Do it because you like to do it. Just 10 years ago, if you had gone to an economist, anywhere, "Hey, I've got these two different models for creating an encyclopedia. If they went head to head, who would win?" 10 years ago you could not have found a single sober economist anywhere on planet Earth
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 做因為你喜歡做。就在十年前，如果你去找任何地方的經濟學家，「嘿，我有這兩種不同的模型來創建百科全書。如果它們正面交鋒，誰會贏？」十年前，你在地球上找不到任何一個清醒的經濟學家

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: who would have predicted the Wikipedia model. This is the titanic battle between these two approaches. This is the Ali-Frazier of motivation, right? This is the Thrilla in Manila. Intrinsic motivators versus extrinsic motivators. Autonomy, mastery and purpose, versus carrot and sticks, and who wins?
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 誰會預測維基百科模型。這是這兩種方法之間的激烈戰鬥。這就是阿里-弗雷澤的動機，對嗎？這就是馬尼拉的驚險。內在激勵因素與外在激勵因素。自主、掌握和目標，與胡蘿蔔加大棒相比，誰會獲勝？

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: Intrinsic motivation, autonomy, mastery and purpose, in a knockout. Let me wrap up. There is a mismatch between what science knows and what business does. Here is what science knows. One: Those 20th century rewards, those motivators we think are a natural part of business,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 淘汰賽中的內在動機、自主性、掌控力和目標。讓我總結一下。科學知識與商業行為之間存在不匹配。這是科學所知道的。一：那些 20 世紀的獎勵，那些我們認為是商業自然組成部分的激勵因素，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: do work, but only in a surprisingly narrow band of circumstances. Two: Those if-then rewards often destroy creativity. Three: The secret to high performance isn't rewards and punishments, but that unseen intrinsic drive-- the drive to do things for their own sake.
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 確實有效，但只在極少數情況下有效。第二：那些「如果-那麼」獎勵往往會破壞創造力。三：高績效的秘訣不是獎勵和懲罰，而是看不見的內在動力──為了自己的利益而做事的動力。

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: The drive to do things cause they matter. And here's the best part. We already know this. The science confirms what we know in our hearts. So, if we repair this mismatch between science and business, if we bring our motivation, notions of motivation into the 21st century,
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 做事的動力，因為它們很重要。這是最好的部分。我們已經知道這一點了。科學證實了我們心中所知道的事。因此，如果我們修復科學與商業之間的這種不匹配，如果我們將我們的動機、動機的概念帶入 21 世紀，

shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.en: if we get past this lazy, dangerous, ideology of carrots and sticks, we can strengthen our businesses, we can solve a lot of those candle problems, and maybe, maybe -- we can change the world. I rest my case. Notifications alt+T
shawn_achor_the_happy_secret_to_better_w.zh: 如果我們克服這種懶惰、危險、胡蘿蔔加大棒的意識形態，我們就可以加強我們的業務，我們就可以解決許多蠟燭問題，也許，也許——我們可以改變世界。我休息一下。通知 alt+T

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: When I was seven years old and my sister was just five years old, we were playing on top of a bunk bed. I was two years older than my sister at the time -- I mean, I'm two years older than her now -- but at the time it meant she had to do everything that I wanted to do, and I wanted to play war.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當我七歲和我姐姐五歲的時候，我們在雙層床上玩耍。當時我比我姐姐大兩歲——我的意思是，我現在比她大兩歲——但當時這意味著她必須做我想做的一切，而我想玩戰爭。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: So we were up on top of our bunk beds. And on one side of the bunk bed, I had put out all of my G.I. Joe soldiers and weaponry. And on the other side were all my sister's My Little Ponies There are differing accounts of what actually happened that afternoon,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 所以我們躺在雙層床上。在雙層床的一側，我把我所有的退伍軍人物品都拿出來了。喬士兵和武器裝備。另一邊都是我姐姐的小馬駒 對於那天下午實際發生的事情有不同的說法，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: but since my sister is not here with us today, let me tell you the true story -- which is my sister's a little on the clumsy side. Somehow, without any help or push from her older brother at all, Amy disappeared off of the top of the bunk bed and landed with this crash on the floor.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但由於今天我姐姐不在場，所以讓我告訴你真實的故事——這是我姐姐有點笨拙的一面。不知何故，在沒有哥哥的任何幫助或推動的情況下，艾米從雙層床的頂部消失了，然後摔在地板上。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I nervously peered over the side of the bed to see what had befallen my fallen sister and saw that she had landed painfully on her hands and knees on all fours on the ground. I was nervous because my parents had charged me with making sure that my sister and I
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我緊張地從床邊往外看，想看看摔倒的妹妹發生了什麼事，發現她痛苦地四肢著地，雙手雙膝著地。我很緊張，因為我的父母責成我確保我和姐姐

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And seeing as how I had accidentally broken Amy's arm just one week before -- (Laughter ends) heroically pushing her out of the way of an oncoming imaginary sniper bullet, for which I have yet to be thanked, I was trying as hard as I could -- she didn't even see it coming --
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 就在一週前，我不小心折斷了艾米的手臂——（笑聲結束）英勇地把她推開了迎面而來的想像中的狙擊子彈，我還沒有為此感謝我，我已經盡了最大的努力——她甚至沒有預見到它的到來——

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I was trying hard to be on my best behavior. And I saw my sister's face, this wail of pain and suffering and surprise threatening to erupt from her mouth and wake my parents from the long winter's nap for which they had settled. So I did the only thing
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我努力表現出最好的行為。我看到了姐姐的臉，這痛苦、痛苦和驚訝的哀嚎威脅著要從她嘴裡爆發出來，把我父母從他們已經習慣的漫長冬日小睡中吵醒。所以我做了唯一的一件事

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: my frantic seven year-old brain could think to do to avert this tragedy. And if you have children, you've seen this hundreds of times. I said, "Amy, wait. Don't cry. Did you see how you landed? No human lands on all fours like that. Amy, I think this means you're a unicorn." Now, that was cheating,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我七歲的大腦瘋狂地想如何避免這場悲劇。如果你有孩子，你已經見過數百次這種情況了。我說：「艾米，等等。別哭。你看到你是怎麼著陸的了嗎？沒有人像這樣用四肢著地。艾米，我想這意味著你是一隻獨角獸。」現在看來，那是作弊，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: because there was nothing she would want more than not to be Amy the hurt five year-old little sister, but Amy the special unicorn. Of course, this option was open to her brain at no point in the past. And you could see how my poor, manipulated sister faced conflict,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為她最想要的不是艾米那個受傷的五歲小妹妹，而是艾米那隻特殊的獨角獸。當然，她的大腦在過去從未有過這種選擇。你可以看到我可憐的、被操縱的妹妹如何面對衝突，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: as her little brain attempted to devote resources to feeling the pain and suffering and surprise she just experienced, or contemplating her new-found identity as a unicorn. And the latter won. Instead of crying or ceasing our play, instead of waking my parents,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當她的小大腦試圖投入資源來感受她剛剛經歷的痛苦和驚喜，或思考她作為獨角獸的新身份。而後者獲勝了。與其哭泣或停止玩耍，與其叫醒我的父母，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: with all the negative consequences for me, a smile spread across her face and she scrambled back up onto the bunk bed with all the grace of a baby unicorn -- with one broken leg. What we stumbled across at this tender age of just five and seven -- we had no idea at the time --
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 儘管這給我帶來了所有的負面後果，但她的臉上露出了微笑，她像一隻小獨角獸一樣優雅地爬回雙層床上——一條腿斷了。我們在五歲和七歲的年紀偶然發現了什麼——當時我們不知道——

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: was was going be at the vanguard of a scientific revolution occurring two decades later in the way that we look at the human brain. We had stumbled across something called positive psychology, which is the reason I'm here today and the reason that I wake up every morning.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 它將成為二十年後以我們看待人類大腦的方式發生的科學革命的先驅。我們偶然發現了一種叫做正向心理學的東西，這就是我今天在這裡的原因，也是我每天早上醒來的原因。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: When I started talking about this research outside of academia, with companies and schools, the first thing they said to never do is to start with a graph. The first thing I want to do is start with a graph. This graph looks boring, but it is the reason I get excited and wake up every morning.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當我開始在學術界之外、公司和學校談論這項研究時，他們說永遠不要做的第一件事就是從圖表開始。我想做的第一件事是從圖表開始。這張圖看起來很無聊，但這就是我每天早上興奮醒來的原因。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And this graph doesn't even mean anything; it's fake data. What we found is -- If I got this data studying you, I would be thrilled, because there's a trend there, and that means that I can get published, which is all that really matters. There is one weird red dot above the curve,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這張圖根本沒有任何意義；這是假數據。我們發現 - 如果我得到這些研究你的數據，我會很興奮，因為那裡有一種趨勢，這意味著我可以發表，這才是真正重要的。曲線上方有一個奇怪的紅點，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: there's one weirdo in the room -- I know who you are, I saw you earlier -- that's no problem. That's no problem, as most of you know, because I can just delete that dot. I can delete that dot because that's clearly a measurement error.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 房間裡有一個怪人——我知道你是誰，我早些時候見過你——那沒問題。正如你們大多數人所知，這沒問題，因為我可以刪除那個點。我可以刪除那個點，因為這顯然是一個測量錯誤。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And we know that's a measurement error because it's messing up my data. So one of the first things we teach people in economics, statistics, business and psychology courses is how, in a statistically valid way, do we eliminate the weirdos.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們知道這是一個測量錯誤，因為它弄亂了我的數據。因此，我們在經濟學、統計學、商業和心理學課程中教給人們的第一件事就是如何以統計上有效的方式消除怪人。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: How do we eliminate the outliers so we can find the line of best fit? Which is fantastic if I'm trying to find out how many Advil the average person should be taking -- two. But if I'm interested in your potential, or for happiness or productivity or energy or creativity,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們如何消除異常值以便找到最佳擬合線？如果我想知道一般人應該服用多少 Advil（兩片），那就太棒了。但如果我對你的潛力感興趣，或對幸福、生產力、精力或創造力感興趣，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: we're creating the cult of the average with science. If I asked a question like, "How fast can a child learn how to read in a classroom?" scientists change the answer to "How fast does the average child learn how to read in that classroom?" and we tailor the class towards the average.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們正在用科學創造普通人的崇拜。如果我問這樣的問題：「孩子在教室裡學會閱讀的速度有多快？」科學家將答案改為「普通孩子在教室裡學習閱讀的速度有多快？」我們會根據平均水平調整班級。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: If you fall below the average, then psychologists get thrilled, because that means you're depressed or have a disorder, or hopefully both. We're hoping for both because our business model is, if you come into a therapy session with one problem, we want to make sure you leave knowing you have ten,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 如果你的成績低於平均水平，那麼心理學家會很興奮，因為這意味著你患有憂鬱症或患有疾病，或者希望兩者兼而有之。我們希望兩者兼而有之，因為我們的商業模式是，如果你帶著一個問題參加治療，我們希望確保你離開時知道你有十個問題，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: so you keep coming back. We'll go back into your childhood if necessary, but eventually we want to make you normal again. But normal is merely average. And positive psychology posits that if we study what is merely average, we will remain merely average.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 所以你會不斷回來。如有必要，我們會回到你的童年，但最終我們想讓你恢復正常。但正常只是平均。正向心理學認為，如果我們只是研究平均水平，我們就只能保持平均水平。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Then instead of deleting those positive outliers, what I intentionally do is come into a population like this one Why are some of you high above the curve in terms of intellectual, athletic, musical ability, creativity, energy levels, resiliency in the face of challenge, sense of humor?
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 然後，我不是刪除那些正面的異常值，而是有意做的是進入像這樣的人群。 為什麼你們中的一些人在智力、運動、音樂能力、創造力、能量水平、面對挑戰的彈性、幽默感方面都高於曲線？

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Whatever it is, instead of deleting you, what I want to do is study you. Because maybe we can glean information, not just how to move people up to the average, but move the entire average up in our companies and schools worldwide. The reason this graph is important to me
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 不管怎樣，我不想刪除你，而是想研究你。因為也許我們可以收集信息，不僅僅是如何將人們提升到平均水平，而是將我們全球公司和學校的整體平均水平提高。這張圖對我來說很重要的原因

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: is, on the news, the majority of the information is not positive. in fact it's negative. Most of it's about murder, corruption, diseases, natural disasters. And very quickly, my brain starts to think that's the accurate ratio of negative to positive in the world.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 就是，新聞上，大部分的資訊都不是正面。事實上它是負面的。其中大部分是關於謀殺、腐敗、疾病、天災。很快，我的大腦開始認為這就是世界上消極與積極的準確比例。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: This creates "the medical school syndrome." During the first year of medical training, as you read through a list of all the symptoms and diseases, suddenly you realize you have all of them. I have a brother in-law named Bobo, which is a whole other story. Bobo married Amy the unicorn.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這就造成了「醫學院症候群」。在醫學訓練的第一年，當您閱讀所有症狀和疾病的清單時，突然您意識到自己患有所有這些症狀和疾病。我有一個姐夫，名叫波波，這完全是另一回事了。波波與獨角獸艾米結婚了。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Bobo called me on the phone -- from Yale Medical School, and Bobo said, "Shawn, I have leprosy." Which, even at Yale, is extraordinarily rare. But I had no idea how to console poor Bobo because he had just gotten over an entire week of menopause.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 波波從耶魯醫學院給我打電話，波波說：「肖恩，我得了痲瘋病。」即使在耶魯大學，這也是極為罕見的。但我不知道該如何安慰可憐的波波，因為他剛度過了整整一週的更年期。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: We're finding it's not necessarily the reality that shapes us, but the lens through which your brain views the world that shapes your reality. And if we can change the lens, not only can we change your happiness, we can change every single educational and business outcome at the same time.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們發現，塑造我們的不一定是現實，而是大腦看待世界的鏡頭塑造了你的現實。如果我們能夠改變視角，我們不僅可以改變您的幸福感，還可以同時改變每項教育和商業成果。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: I applied to Harvard on a dare. I didn't expect to get in, and my family had no money for college. When I got a military scholarship two weeks later, they let me go. Something that wasn't even a possibility became a reality. I assumed everyone there would see it as a privilege as well,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我大膽地申請了哈佛。沒想到能考上，家裡也沒錢上大學。兩週後，當我獲得軍事獎學金時，他們讓我走了。一些原本不可能的事情變成了現實。我以為那裡的每個人都會認為這是一種特權，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: that they'd be excited to be there. Even in a classroom full of people smarter than you, I felt you'd be happy just to be in that classroom. But what I found is, while some people experience that, when I graduated after my four years
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 他們會很高興能在那裡。即使教室裡擠滿了比你聰明的人，我覺得只要在那間教室裡你就會很高興。但我發現，雖然有些人經歷過這種情況，但當我四年後畢業時

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and then spent the next eight years living in the dorms with the students -- Harvard asked me to; I wasn't that guy. I was an officer to counsel students through the difficult four years. And in my research and my teaching, I found that these students, no matter how happy they were
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 然後在接下來的八年和學生們一起住在宿舍裡——哈佛要求我這麼做；我不是那個人。我是一名官員，為學生提供輔導，幫助他們度過困難的四年。在我的研究和教學中，我發現這些學生，無論他們多麼快樂

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: with their original success of getting into the school, two weeks later their brains were focused, not on the privilege of being there, nor on their philosophy or physics, but on the competition, the workload, the hassles, stresses, complaints.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當他們最初成功進入學校時，兩週後，他們的大腦不再關注在那裡的特權，也不是他們的哲學或物理，而是競爭、工作量、麻煩、壓力和抱怨。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: When I first went in there, I walked into the freshmen dining hall, which is where my friends from Waco, Texas, which is where I grew up -- I know some of you know this. When they'd visit, they'd look around, and say, "This dining hall looks like something out of Hogwart's."
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當我第一次進去時，我走進了新生食堂，這是我來自德克薩斯州韋科的朋友們的地方，那裡是我長大的地方——我知道你們中的一些人知道這一點。當他們參觀時，他們會環顧四周，然後說：“這個餐廳看起來就像霍格華茲的餐廳。”

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: It does, because that was Hogwart's and that's Harvard. And when they see this, they say, "Why do you waste your time studying happiness at Harvard? What does a Harvard student possibly have to be unhappy about?" Embedded within that question is the key to understanding the science of happiness.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 確實如此，因為那是霍格華茲的，那是哈佛的。當他們看到這一點時，他們會說：「為什麼要浪費時間在哈佛研究幸福？哈佛學生可能會因為什麼而不幸福？」這個問題中蘊藏著理解幸福科學的關鍵。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Because what that question assumes is that our external world is predictive of our happiness levels, when in reality, if I know everything about your external world, I can only predict 10% of your long-term happiness. 90 percent of your long-term happiness is predicted not by the external world,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為這個問題假設我們的外在世界可以預測我們的幸福水平，而實際上，如果我了解你外在世界的一切，我只能預測你長期幸福的 10%。你90%的長期幸福不是外在世界預測的，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: but by the way your brain processes the world. And if we change it, if we change our formula for happiness and success, we can change the way that we can then affect reality. What we found is that only 25% of job successes are predicted by IQ, 75 percent of job successes
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但順便說一句，你的大腦處理世界的方式。如果我們改變它，如果我們改變幸福和成功的公式，我們就可以改變我們影響現實的方式。我們發現，只有 25% 的工作成功是由智商預測的，75% 的工作成功是由智商預測的

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: are predicted by your optimism levels, your social support and your ability to see stress as a challenge instead of as a threat. I talked to a New England boarding school, probably the most prestigious one, and they said, "We already know that.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 根據您的樂觀程度、您的社會支持以及您將壓力視為挑戰而不是威脅的能力來預測。我和新英格蘭的一所寄宿學校交談過，這可能是最負盛名的一所，他們說：「我們已經知道了。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: So every year, instead of just teaching our students, we have a wellness week. And we're so excited. Monday night we have the world's leading expert will speak about adolescent depression. Tuesday night it's school violence and bullying. Wednesday night is eating disorders.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因此，每年我們不僅會教導學生，還會舉辦健康週。我們非常興奮。週一晚上，我們將邀請世界領先的專家談論青少年憂鬱症。週二晚上是校園暴力和霸凌事件。週三晚上是飲食失調的日子。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Thursday night is illicit drug use. And Friday night we're trying to decide between risky sex or happiness." I said, "That's most people's Friday nights." Which I'm glad you liked, but they did not like that at all. Silence on the phone.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 週四晚上是非法吸毒的日子。週五晚上，我們要在危險的性行為和幸福之間做出選擇。 」我說，「那是大多數人的星期五晚上。 「我很高興你喜歡，但他們根本不喜歡這樣。電話裡一片沉默。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And into the silence, I said, "I'd be happy to speak at your school, but that's not a wellness week, that's a sickness week. You've outlined all the negative things that can happen, but not talked about the positive." The absence of disease is not health. Here's how we get to health:
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 在一片沉默中，我說：「我很樂意在你們學校演講，但這不是健康週，而是疾病週。你們概述了所有可能發生的負面事情，但沒有談論積極的事情。」沒有疾病並不代表健康。以下是我們如何獲得健康：

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: We need to reverse the formula for happiness and success. In the last three years, I've traveled to 45 countries, working with schools and companies in the midst of an economic downturn. And I found that most companies and schools follow a formula for success, which is this:
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們需要扭轉幸福和成功的公式。在過去的三年裡，我去過 45 個國家，在經濟低迷時期與學校和公司合作。我發現大多數公司和學校都遵循一個成功的公式，那就是：

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: If I work harder, I'll be more successful. And if I'm more successful, then I'll be happier. That undergirds most of our parenting and managing styles, the way that we motivate our behavior. And the problem is it's scientifically broken and backwards for two reasons.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 如果我更加努力，我就會更加成功。如果我更成功，我就會更快樂。這支撐了我們大多數的養育和管理風格，以及我們激勵我們行為的方式。問題是它在科學上是被破壞和倒退的，原因有二。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Every time your brain has a success, you just changed the goalpost of what success looked like. You got good grades, now you have to get better grades, you got into a good school and after you get into a better one, you got a good job, now you have to get a better job,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 每當你的大腦取得成功時，你就改變了成功的目標。你取得了好成績，現在你必須取得更好的成績，你進入了一所好學校，進入更好的學校後，你找到了一份好工作，現在你必須找到一份更好的工作，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: you hit your sales target, we're going to change it. And if happiness is on the opposite side of success, your brain never gets there. We've pushed happiness over the cognitive horizon, as a society. And that's because we think we have to be successful, then we'll be happier.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 您達到了銷售目標，我們將對其進行更改。如果幸福與成功相反，你的大腦永遠不會到達那裡。作為一個社會，我們已經將幸福超越了認知範圍。那是因為我們認為我們必須成功，然後我們才會更快樂。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: But our brains work in the opposite order. If you can raise somebody's level of positivity in the present, then their brain experiences what we now call a happiness advantage, which is your brain at positive performs significantly better than at negative, neutral or stressed.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 但我們的大腦以相反的順序運作。如果你能提高某人目前的積極性水平，那麼他們的大腦就會體驗到我們現在所說的幸福優勢，即你的大腦在積極時的表現明顯好於在消極、中性或壓力時的表現。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Your intelligence rises, your creativity rises, your energy levels rise. In fact, we've found that every single business outcome improves. Your brain at positive is 31% more productive than your brain at negative, neutral or stressed. You're 37% better at sales.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 你的智力提高了，你的創造力提高了，你的能量水平提高了。事實上，我們發現每一項業務成果都得到了改善。處於正面狀態的大腦比處於負面、中性或壓力狀態時的大腦生產力高出 31%。您的銷售業績提高了 37%。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: Doctors are 19 percent faster, more accurate at coming up with the correct diagnosis when positive instead of negative, neutral or stressed. Which means we can reverse the formula. If we can find a way of becoming positive in the present, then our brains work even more successfully
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當呈陽性而不是陰性、中性或有壓力時，醫生做出正確診斷的速度要快 19%，準確率要高 19%。這意味著我們可以反轉公式。如果我們能找到一種讓當下變得積極的方法，那麼我們的大腦就會更有效地運作

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: as we're able to work harder, faster and more intelligently. We need to be able to reverse this formula so we can start to see what our brains are actually capable of. Because dopamine, which floods into your system when you're positive, has two functions. Not only does it make you happier,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 因為我們能夠更努力、更快、更聰明地工作。我們需要能夠扭轉這個公式，這樣我們才能開始了解我們的大腦實際上有什麼能力。因為當您呈陽性時，多巴胺會湧入您的系統，它有兩個功能。不僅能讓你心情愉悅，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: it turns on all of the learning centers in your brain allowing you to adapt to the world in a different way. We've found there are ways that you can train your brain to be able to become more positive. In just a two-minute span of time done for 21 days in a row, we can actually rewire your brain,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 它會打開你腦中所有的學習中心，讓你以不同的方式適應世界。我們發現有一些方法可以訓練你的大腦變得更加積極。在連續 21 天的短短兩分鐘時間內，我們實際上可以重新連接你的大腦，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: allowing your brain to actually work more optimistically and more successfully. We've done these things in research now in every company that I've worked with, getting them to write down three new things that they're grateful for for 21 days in a row, three new things each day.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 讓你的大腦實際上更樂觀、更成功地運作。我們現在在我合作過的每家公司都做了這些研究，讓他們連續 21 天寫下三件讓他們感激的新事情，每天三件新事情。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: And at the end of that, their brain starts to retain a pattern of scanning the world not for the negative, but for the positive first. Journaling about one positive experience you've had over the past 24 hours allows your brain to relive it. Exercise teaches your brain that your behavior matters.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 最後，他們的大腦開始保留一種掃描世界的模式，首先不是尋找負面的一面，而是尋找正面積極的一面。記錄過去 24 小時內的一次積極經歷可以讓您的大腦重溫它。運動會讓你的大腦知道你的行為很重要。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: We find that meditation allows your brain to get over the cultural ADHD that we've been creating by trying to do multiple tasks at once and allows our brains to focus on the task at hand. And finally, random acts of kindness are conscious acts of kindness.
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 我們發現冥想可以讓你的大腦克服我們因試圖同時完成多項任務而造成的文化過動症，並讓我們的大腦專注於手頭上的任務。最後，隨機的善意行為是有意識的善意行為。

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: We get people, when they open up their inbox, to write one positive email praising or thanking somebody in their support network. And by doing these activities and by training your brain just like we train our bodies, what we've found is we can reverse the formula for happiness and success,
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 當人們打開收件匣時，我們會讓他們寫一封積極的電子郵件，讚揚或感謝他們支持網路中的某人。透過進行這些活動並像訓練我們的身體一樣訓練您的大腦，我們發現我們可以扭轉幸福和成功的公式，

elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.en: and in doing so, not only create ripples of positivity, but a real revolution. Thank you very much. Notifications alt+T
elizabeth_gilbert_your_elusive_creative_.zh: 這樣做不僅會產生正面的漣漪，而且會引發一場真正的革命。非常感謝。通知 alt+T

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Writing books is my profession but it's more than that, of course. It is also my great lifelong love and fascination. And I don't expect that that's ever going to change. But, that said, something kind of peculiar has happened recently in my life and in my career,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 寫書是我的職業，但當然不只如此。這也是我一生的摯愛與著迷。我預計這種情況不會改變。但是，話雖如此，最近我的生活和職業生涯中發生了一些奇怪的事情，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: which has caused me to have to recalibrate my whole relationship with this work. And the peculiar thing is that I recently wrote this book, this memoir called "Eat, Pray, Love" which, decidedly unlike any of my previous books, went out in the world for some reason, and became this big,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 這導致我不得不重新調整我與這項工作的整個關係。奇怪的是我最近寫了這本書，這本回憶錄叫做《美食，祈禱，愛》，它與我以前的任何一本書截然不同，由於某種原因在世界範圍內出版，並變得如此重要，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: mega-sensation, international bestseller thing. The result of which is that everywhere I go now, people treat me like I'm doomed. Seriously -- doomed, doomed! "Aren't you afraid you're never going to be able to top that? Aren't you afraid you're going to keep writing for your whole life
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 轟動一時的國際暢銷書。結果是，無論我現在走到哪裡，人們都對待我，就像我注定要失敗一樣。說真的－注定了，注定了！ “你不擔心你永遠無法超越嗎？你不擔心你會一生都在寫作嗎？”

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and you're never again going to create a book that anybody in the world cares about at all, So that's reassuring, you know. But it would be worse, except for that I happen to remember that over 20 years ago, when I was a teenager, when I first started telling people that I wanted to be a writer,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而且你再也不會創作一本世界上任何人都關心的書，所以這讓人放心，你知道。但情況會更糟，除了我碰巧記得二十多年前，當我十幾歲的時候，當我第一次開始告訴人們我想成為作家時，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I was met with this same sort of fear-based reaction. And people would say, "Aren't you afraid you're never going to have any success? Aren't you afraid the humiliation of rejection will kill you? Aren't you afraid that you're going to work your whole life at this craft
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我也遇到了同樣的基於恐懼的反應。人們會說，“你不擔心你永遠不會取得任何成功嗎？你不擔心被拒絕的羞辱會殺死你嗎？你不擔心你將在這門手藝上工作一輩子嗎？”

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and nothing's ever going to come of it and you're going to die on a scrap heap of broken dreams with your mouth filled with bitter ash of failure?" The answer -- the short answer to all those questions is, "Yes." Yes, I'm afraid of all those things. And I always have been.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 一切都不會發生，你會死在夢想破碎的廢墟上，嘴裡充滿失敗的苦澀灰燼嗎？ 」答案——對所有這些問題的簡短回答是，「是的。 「是的，我害怕所有這些事情。而且我一直都是。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And I'm afraid of many, many more things besides that people can't even guess at, But, when it comes to writing, the thing that I've been sort of thinking about lately, and wondering about lately, You know, is it rational? Is it logical that anybody should be expected
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 除了人們無法猜測的事情之外，我還害怕很多很多事情，但是，當談到寫作時，我最近一直在思考，最近想知道的事情，你知道，這是理性的嗎？任何人都應該被期待合乎邏輯嗎？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: to be afraid of the work that they feel they were put on this Earth to do. And what is it specifically about creative ventures that seems to make us really nervous about each other's mental health in a way that other careers kind of don't do, you know?
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 害怕他們覺得自己來到這個地球上要做的工作。創意事業中的什麼特別之處似乎讓我們對彼此的心理健康感到非常緊張，而其他職業則不會這樣做，你知道嗎？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and I don't recall once in his 40 years of chemical engineering anybody asking him if he was afraid to be a chemical engineer, you know? "That chemical-engineering block, John, how's it going?" It just didn't come up like that, you know? But to be fair, chemical engineers as a group
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我不記得在他 40 年的化學工程生涯中，沒有人問過他是否害怕成為化學工程師，你知道嗎？ 「那個化學工程模組，約翰，怎麼樣了？」只是事情不是這樣發生的，你知道嗎？但公平地說，化學工程師作為一個群體

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: haven't really earned a reputation over the centuries for being alcoholic manic-depressives. We writers, we kind of do have that reputation, and not just writers, but creative people across all genres, it seems, have this reputation for being enormously mentally unstable.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 幾個世紀以來，我們並沒有真正因酗酒、躁鬱症而聲名大噪。我們作家確實享有這樣的聲譽，而且不僅僅是作家，所有類型的創意人士似乎都享有精神極度不穩定的聲譽。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And all you have to do is look at the very grim death count in the 20th century alone, of really magnificent creative minds who died young and often at their own hands, you know? And even the ones who didn't literally commit suicide seem to be really undone by their gifts, you know.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 你要做的就是看看僅在 20 世紀就非常嚴峻的死亡人數，那些真正偉大的創造性思想家英年早逝，而且常常死於自己之手，你知道嗎？你知道，即使是那些沒有真正自殺的人，似乎也確實被他們的天賦毀掉了。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Norman Mailer, just before he died, last interview, he said, "Every one of my books has killed me a little more." An extraordinary statement to make about your life's work. But we don't even blink when we hear somebody say this, because we've heard that kind of stuff for so long
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 諾曼梅勒在去世前的最後一次採訪中說：「我的每一本書都讓我多受了一點折磨。」關於您一生的工作的非凡聲明。但當我們聽到有人這麼說時，我們甚至不會眨眼，因為我們已經聽到這種話太久了

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and somehow we've completely internalized and accepted collectively this notion that creativity and suffering are somehow inherently linked and that artistry, in the end, will always ultimately lead to anguish. And the question that I want to ask everybody here today
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 不知怎的，我們已經完全內化並集體接受了這樣的觀念：創造力和痛苦在某種程度上有著內在的聯繫，而藝術最終總是會導致痛苦。今天想問大家的問題

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: is are you guys all cool with that idea? Are you comfortable with that? Because you look at it even from an inch away and, you know -- I'm not at all comfortable with that assumption. I think it's odious. And I also think it's dangerous, and I don't want to see it perpetuated into the next century.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 你們都同意這個想法嗎？你對此感到滿意嗎？因為即使從一英寸遠的地方看它，你也知道——我對這個假設一點也不舒服。我認為這很可惡。我也認為這很危險，我不想看到它延續到下個世紀。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: I think it's better if we encourage our great creative minds to live. And I definitely know that, in my case -- in my situation -- it would be very dangerous for me to start sort of leaking down that dark path particularly given the circumstance that I'm in right now in my career.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我認為，如果我們鼓勵偉大的創造性思維去生活，那就更好了。我絕對知道，就我而言——就我的情況而言——如果我開始走上這條黑暗的道路，那將是非常危險的，特別是考慮到我現在的職業生涯。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Which is -- you know, like check it out, I'm pretty young, I'm only about 40 years old. I still have maybe another four decades of work left in me. And it's exceedingly likely that anything I write from this point forward is going to be judged by the world as the work that came after
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 也就是說——你知道，看看吧，我很年輕，我才 40 歲左右。我也許還剩下四十年的工作。從現在起我寫的任何東西都極有可能被世界評價為後來的作品

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: the freakish success of my last book, right? I should just put it bluntly, because we're all sort of friends here now -- it's exceedingly likely that my greatest success is behind me. So Jesus, what a thought! That's the kind of thought that could lead a person
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我的上一本書取得了驚人的成功，對嗎？我應該坦白說，因為我們現在都是朋友了——我最大的成功很可能已經過去了。所以天哪，這是多麼好的一個想法啊！這樣的思想才能引導一個人

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: to start drinking gin at nine o'clock in the morning, and I don't want to go there. I would prefer to keep doing this work that I love.
