And so, the question becomes, how? And so, it seems to me, upon a lot of reflection, that the way that I have to work now, in order to continue writing,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 早上九點開始喝琴酒，我不想去那裡。我比較願意繼續做我喜歡的工作。那麼，問題變成了，如何？因此，經過深思熟慮，在我看來，為了繼續寫作，我現在必須採取的工作方式，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: is that I have to create some sort of protective psychological construct, right? I have to sort of find some way to have a safe distance between me, as I am writing, and my very natural anxiety about what the reaction to that writing is going to be, from now on.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我必須創造某種保護性的心理結構，對嗎？我必須找到某種方法，在我寫作時與我之間保持安全距離，而我自然會擔心從現在開始對寫作的反應將會是什麼。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And, as I've been looking, over the last year, for models for how to do that, I've been sort of looking across time, and I've been trying to find other societies to see if they might have had better and saner ideas than we have about how to help creative people
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而且，正如我在過去一年中一直在尋找如何做到這一點的模型一樣，我一直在尋找跨越時間的方法，並且我一直在努力尋找其他社會，看看他們是否可能有比我們更好、更明智的想法來幫助有創造力的人

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: sort of manage the inherent emotional risks of creativity. And that search has led me to ancient Greece and ancient Rome. So stay with me, because it does circle around and back. But, ancient Greece and ancient Rome -- people did not happen to believe that creativity
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 管理創造力固有的情感風險。這種探索將我帶到了古希臘和古羅馬。所以請跟著我，因為它確實會繞來繞去。但是，古希臘和古羅馬——人們並不碰巧相信創造力

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: came from human beings back then, OK? People believed that creativity was this divine attendant spirit that came to human beings from some distant and unknowable source, for distant and unknowable reasons. The Greeks famously called these divine attendant spirits of creativity "daemons."
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 是從那時人類來的好嗎？人們相信，創造力是一種神聖的伴隨精神，它出於遙遠且不可知的原因，從某個遙遠且不可知的源頭降臨到人類身上。希臘人將這些神聖的創造力伴隨靈魂稱為「惡魔」。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Socrates, famously, believed that he had a daemon who spoke wisdom to him from afar. The Romans had the same idea, but they called that sort of disembodied creative spirit a genius. Which is great, because the Romans did not actually think that a genius was a particularly clever individual.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 眾所周知，蘇格拉底相信他有一個惡魔從遠處向他講授智慧。羅馬人也有同樣的想法，但他們把這種無形的創造精神稱為天才。這很好，因為羅馬人其實不認為天才是個特別聰明的人。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: They believed that a genius was this, sort of magical divine entity, who was believed to literally live in the walls of an artist's studio, kind of like Dobby the house elf, and who would come out and sort of invisibly assist the artist with their work and would shape the outcome of that work.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他們相信天才就是這樣一種神奇的神聖實體，人們相信他實際上生活在藝術家工作室的牆壁裡，有點像家養小精靈多比，他會出來無形地幫助藝術家完成他們的工作，並塑造作品的結果。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: So brilliant -- there it is, right there, that distance that I'm talking about -- that psychological construct to protect you from the results of your work. And everyone knew that this is how it functioned, right? So the ancient artist was protected from certain things,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 太棒了——就在那裡，就在那裡，我所說的距離——保護你免受工作結果影響的心理結構。每個人都知道它是這樣運作的，對吧？所以古代藝術家受到某些事物的保護，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: If your work was brilliant, you couldn't take all the credit for it, everybody knew that you had this disembodied genius who had helped you. If your work bombed, not entirely your fault, you know? Everyone knew your genius was kind of lame. And this is how people thought about creativity in the West
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 如果你的工作很出色，你就不能把所有的功勞都歸功於你，每個人都知道你有一個無形的天才幫助過你。如果你的工作失敗了，這並不完全是你的錯，你知道嗎？每個人都知道你的天才有點蹩腳。這就是西方人對創造力的看法

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: for a really long time. And then the Renaissance came and everything changed, and we had this big idea, and the big idea was, let's put the individual human being at the center of the universe above all gods and mysteries, and there's no more room for mystical creatures
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 很長一段時間。然後文藝復興到來了，一切都改變了，我們有了這個偉大的想法，這個偉大的想法是，讓我們把個體人類置於宇宙的中心，高於所有的神靈和神秘事物，不再有神秘生物的空間

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: who take dictation from the divine. And it's the beginning of rational humanism, and people started to believe that creativity came completely from the self of the individual. And for the first time in history, you start to hear people referring to this or that artist as being a genius,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他們接受神的口授。這是理性人文主義的開始，人們開始相信創造力完全來自個人的自我。歷史上第一次，你開始聽到人們稱這個或那個藝術家為天才，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: rather than having a genius. And I got to tell you, I think that was a huge error. You know, I think that allowing somebody, one mere person to believe that he or she is like, the vessel, you know, like the font and the essence and the source of all divine, creative, unknowable, eternal mystery
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 而不是擁有天才。我必須告訴你，我認為這是一個巨大的錯誤。你知道，我認為讓某人，一個單純的人相信他或她就像一個容器，你知道，就像所有神聖的、創造性的、不可知的、永恆的奧秘的字體、本質和源泉

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: is just a smidge too much responsibility to put on one fragile, human psyche. It's like asking somebody to swallow the sun. It just completely warps and distorts egos, and it creates all these unmanageable expectations about performance. And I think the pressure of that
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 對一個脆弱的人類心靈來說，責任有點太大了。這就像要求某人吞下太陽一樣。它完全扭曲了自我，並且創造了所有這些對績效難以管理的期望。我認為這帶來的壓力

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: has been killing off our artists for the last 500 years. And, if this is true, and I think it is true, the question becomes, what now? Can we do this differently? Maybe go back to some more ancient understanding about the relationship between humans and the creative mystery.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 過去 500 年來一直在殺害我們的藝術家。而且，如果這是真的，而且我認為這是真的，那麼問題就變成了，現在怎麼辦？我們可以採取不同的做法嗎？也許可以回到一些更古老的關於人類與創造性之謎之間的關係的理解。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Maybe we can't just erase 500 years of rational humanistic thought in one 18 minute speech. And there's probably people in this audience who would raise really legitimate scientific suspicions about the notion of, basically, fairies
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 也許我們不能在一次18分鐘的演講中就抹去500年的理性人文思想。聽眾中可能有人會對仙女的概念提出真正合理的科學懷疑

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: who follow people around rubbing fairy juice on their projects and stuff. I'm not, probably, going to bring you all along with me on this. But the question that I kind of want to pose is -- you know, why not? Why not think about it this way?
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他們跟隨人們在他們的項目和東西上塗抹仙女汁。我可能不會在這件事上讓你們都跟我一起。但我想提出的問題是──你知道，為什麼不呢？為什麼不這樣想呢？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Because it makes as much sense as anything else I have ever heard in terms of explaining the utter maddening capriciousness of the creative process. A process which, as anybody who has ever tried to make something -- which is to say basically everyone here --- knows does not always behave rationally.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 因為在解釋創作過程中令人發狂的反覆無常方面，它和我聽過的任何其他東西一樣有意義。正如任何曾經嘗試過製造某種東西的人——也就是說基本上這裡的每個人——都知道，這個過程並不總是理性地運作。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And, in fact, can sometimes feel downright paranormal. I had this encounter recently where I met the extraordinary American poet Ruth Stone, who's now in her 90s, but she's been a poet her entire life and she told me that when she was growing up in rural Virginia,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 事實上，有時會讓人感到徹頭徹尾的超自然現象。我最近遇到了這樣的遭遇，我遇到了傑出的美國詩人露絲·斯通（Ruth Stone），她現在已經 90 多歲了，但她一生都是詩人，她告訴我，當她在弗吉尼亞州的鄉村長大時，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: she would be out working in the fields, and she said she would feel and hear a poem coming at her from over the landscape. And she said it was like a thunderous train of air. And it would come barreling down at her over the landscape.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 她会去田里干活，她说她会感觉到并听到一首诗从大地的上空向她袭来。她說這就像一列雷鳴般的空氣。它會從風景上空向她襲來。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And she felt it coming, because it would shake the earth under her feet. She knew that she had only one thing to do at that point, and that was to, in her words, "run like hell." And she would run like hell to the house and she would be getting chased by this poem,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 她感覺到它的到來，因為它會震動她腳下的大地。她知道此時她只有一件事要做，用她的話來說，那就是「拼命奔跑」。她會拼命跑回家，然後被這首詩追趕，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and the whole deal was that she had to get to a piece of paper and a pencil fast enough so that when it thundered through her, she could collect it and grab it on the page. And other times she wouldn't be fast enough, so she'd be running and running, and she wouldn't get to the house
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 整個過程是，她必須足夠快地拿到一張紙和一支鉛筆，以便當它轟鳴著穿過她時，她可以收集它並抓住它在紙上。有時她跑得不夠快，所以她會跑啊跑，但她無法到達房子

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and the poem would barrel through her and she would miss it and she said it would continue on across the landscape, looking, as she put it "for another poet." And then there were these times -- this is the piece I never forgot -- she said that there were moments where she would almost miss it, right?
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 這首詩會穿過她的身體，她會想念它，她說它會繼續穿過風景，尋找，正如她所說的「另一位詩人」。然後還有一些時候——這是我永遠不會忘記的作品——她說有些時刻她幾乎會懷念它，對嗎？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: So, she's running to the house and she's looking for the paper and the poem passes through her, and she grabs a pencil just as it's going through her, and then she said, it was like she would reach out with her other hand and she would catch it. She would catch the poem by its tail,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 所以，她跑到房子裡，尋找那張紙，這首詩穿過她，當它穿過她時，她抓住了一支鉛筆，然後她說，就像她伸出另一隻手，她會抓住它。她會抓住這首詩的尾巴，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and she would pull it backwards into her body as she was transcribing on the page. And in these instances, the poem would come up on the page perfect and intact but backwards, from the last word to the first. So when I heard that I was like -- that's uncanny,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 當她在紙上抄寫時，她會把它拉回體內。在這些情況下，這首詩會完美無缺地出現在頁面上，但會倒退，從最後一個字到第一個字。所以當我聽到我的感覺時——這太不可思議了，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that's exactly what my creative process is like. That's not at all what my creative process is -- I'm not the pipeline! I'm a mule, and the way that I have to work is I have to get up at the same time every day, and sweat and labor and barrel through it really awkwardly. But even I, in my mulishness,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 這正是我的創作過程。這根本不是我的創作過程──我不是管道！我是一頭騾子，我必須工作的方式就是每天在同一時間起床，流汗、勞動，非常尷尬地度過。但即使是我，在我的固執中，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: even I have brushed up against that thing, at times. And I would imagine that a lot of you have too. You know, even I have had work or ideas come through me from a source that I honestly cannot identify. And what is that thing?
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 就連我自己也常常遇到這種事。我想你們很多人也有過這樣的經驗。你知道，即使是我，也有一些工作或想法是從我無法確定的來源中獲得的。那東西是什麼？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And how are we to relate to it in a way that will not make us lose our minds, but, in fact, might actually keep us sane? And for me, the best contemporary example that I have of how to do that is the musician Tom Waits, who I got to interview several years ago on a magazine assignment.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我們如何以一種既不會讓我們失去理智，又能讓我們保持理智的方式來對待它？對我來說，當代最好的例子就是音樂家湯姆·威茨（Tom Waits），幾年前我在雜誌任務中採訪了他。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: And we were talking about this, and you know, Tom, for most of his life, he was pretty much the embodiment of the tormented contemporary modern artist, trying to control and manage and dominate these sort of uncontrollable creative impulses that were totally internalized.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我們正在談論這個，你知道，湯姆，在他一生的大部分時間裡，他幾乎是飽受折磨的當代現代藝術家的化身，試圖控制、管理和支配這些完全內化的無法控制的創作衝動。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: But then he got older, he got calmer, and one day he was driving down the freeway in Los Angeles, and this is when it all changed for him. And he's speeding along, and all of a sudden he hears this little fragment of melody,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但隨著年齡的增長，他變得更加平靜，有一天，他開車沿著洛杉磯的高速公路行駛，從那時起，一切都改變了。他正在加速前進，突然他聽到了這個小片段的旋律，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that comes into his head as inspiration often comes, elusive and tantalizing, and he wants it, it's gorgeous, and he longs for it, but he has no way to get it. He doesn't have a piece of paper, or a pencil, or a tape recorder. So he starts to feel all of that old anxiety start to rise in him
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 當靈感經常出現時，它就會出現在他的腦海中，難以捉摸又誘人，他想要它，它很華麗，他渴望它，但他沒有辦法得到它。他沒有一張紙，一支鉛筆，或是一台錄音機。所以他開始感覺到所有舊有的焦慮開始在他體內升起

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and I'll be be haunted by this song forever. I'm not good enough, and I can't do it." And instead of panicking, he just stopped. He just stopped that whole mental process and he did something completely novel. He just looked up at the sky, and he said, "Excuse me, can you not see that I'm driving?"
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我將永遠被這首歌所困擾。我不夠好，我做不到。 」他沒有驚慌，而是停了下來。他停止了整個心理過程，做了一些完全新穎的事情。他只是抬頭看著天空，說：「對不起，你沒看到我在開車嗎？ 」

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: "Do I look like I can write down a song right now? If you really want to exist, come back at a more opportune moment when I can take care of you. Otherwise, go bother somebody else today. Go bother Leonard Cohen." And his whole work process changed after that.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 「我現在看起來像能寫歌嗎？如果你真想存在，就找個更合適的時機回來，等我能照顧你的時候再回來。不然的話，今天就去麻煩別人吧，去麻煩萊昂納德·科恩。」此後他的整個工作流程發生了變化。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Not the work, the work was still oftentimes as dark as ever. But the process, and the heavy anxiety around it was released when he took the genie, the genius out of him where it was causing nothing but trouble, and released it back where it came from,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 不是工作，而是工作常常仍然像以前一樣黑暗。 But the process, and the heavy anxiety around it was released when he took the genie, the genius out of him where it was causing nothing but trouble, and released it back where it came from,

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and realized that this didn't have to be this internalized, tormented thing. It could be this peculiar, wondrous, bizarre collaboration, kind of conversation between Tom and the strange, external thing that was not quite Tom. When I heard that story, it started to shift a little bit
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 並意識到這不一定是這種內化的、痛苦的事。這可能是湯姆和那個不完全是湯姆的奇怪的、外在的東西之間的這種奇特、奇妙、奇異的合作，一種對話。當我聽到這個故事時，情況開始發生了一些變化

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: the way that I worked too, and this idea already saved me once. It saved me when I was in the middle of writing "Eat, Pray, Love," and I fell into one of those sort of pits of despair that we all fall into when we're working on something and it's not coming
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我的工作方式也是如此，這個想法已經救了我一次。當我正在寫《美食、祈禱、愛》的時候，它救了我，我陷入了那種絕望的深淵，當我們正在做某事而它沒有到來時，我們都會陷入這種絕望的深淵。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and you start to think this is going to be a disaster, the worst book ever written. Not just bad, but the worst book ever written. And I started to think I should just dump this project. But then I remembered Tom talking to the open air and I tried it. So I just lifted my face up from the manuscript
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 你開始認為這將是一場災難，是有史以來最糟糕的書。不僅糟糕，而且是有史以來最糟糕的書。我開始想我應該放棄這個計畫。但後來我想起​​湯姆在露天說話，我就嘗試了。於是我就把臉從手稿上抬起來

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and I directed my comments to an empty corner of the room. And I said aloud, "Listen you, thing, you and I both know that if this book isn't brilliant that is not entirely my fault, right? Because you can see that I am putting everything I have into this, I don't have any more than this.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 我把我的評論指向了房間的一個空角落。我大聲說：“聽著，你和我都知道，如果這本書不精彩，那也不完全是我的錯，對吧？因為你可以看到，我正在把我所擁有的一切都投入到這本書中，除了這本書之外，我什麼也沒有了。”

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: If you want it to be better, you've got to show up and do your part of the deal. But if you don't do that, you know what, the hell with it. I'm going to keep writing anyway because that's my job. And I would please like the record to reflect today that I showed up for my part of the job."
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 如果你想讓事情變得更好，你就必須出現並盡自己的一份力量。但如果你不這樣做，你知道嗎，那就見鬼去吧。無論如何我都會繼續寫作，因為那是我的工作。我希望今天的記錄能反映出我履行了自己的職責。 」

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: Because in the end it's like this, OK -- centuries ago in the deserts of North Africa, people used to gather for these moonlight dances of sacred dance and music that would go on for hours and hours, until dawn. They were always magnificent, because the dancers were professionals
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 因為最終是這樣的，好吧——幾個世紀前，在北非的沙漠裡，人們常常聚集在月光下跳舞，跳神聖的舞蹈和音樂，持續幾個小時，直到黎明。他們總是很華麗，因為舞者都是專業人士

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: and they were terrific, right? But every once in a while, very rarely, something would happen, and one of these performers would actually become transcendent. And I know you know what I'm talking about, because I know you've all seen, at some point in your life, a performance like this.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他們很棒，對吧？但每隔一段時間，在極少數情況下，就會發生一些事情，其中​​一位表演者實際上會變得超凡脫俗。我知道你們知道我在說什麼，因為我知道你們都在生命中的某個時刻看過這樣的表演。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: It was like time would stop, and the dancer would sort of step through some kind of portal and he wasn't doing anything different than he had ever done, 1,000 nights before, but everything would align. And all of a sudden, he would no longer appear to be merely human.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 就好像時間會停止，舞者會跨過某種傳送門，他並沒有做任何與一千個夜晚之前不同的事情，但一切都會一致。突然之間，他就不再只是一個人類了。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: He would be lit from within, and lit from below and all lit up on fire with divinity. And when this happened, back then, people knew it for what it was, you know, they called it by its name. They would put their hands together and they would start to chant, "Allah, Allah, Allah, God, God, God."
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 他會從內部被點燃，從下麵點燃，一切都被神性點燃。當這件事發生時，當時，人們知道它是什麼，你知道，他們用它的名字來稱呼它。他們會雙手合十，開始唱誦：“安拉，安拉，安拉，上帝，上帝，上帝。”

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: That's God, you know. Curious historical footnote: when the Moors invaded southern Spain, they took this custom with them and the pronunciation changed over the centuries from "Allah, Allah, Allah," to "Olé, olé, olé," which you still hear in bullfights and in flamenco dances.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 那是上帝，你知道。有趣的歷史腳註：當摩爾人入侵西班牙南部時，他們繼承了這種習俗，幾個世紀以來，發音從“阿拉，阿拉，阿拉”變成了“Olé，olé，olé”，你仍然可以在鬥牛和弗拉門戈舞蹈中聽到這種發音。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: In Spain, when a performer has done something impossible and magic, "Allah, olé, olé, Allah, magnificent, bravo," incomprehensible, there it is -- a glimpse of God. Which is great, because we need that. But, the tricky bit comes the next morning, for the dancer himself, when he wakes up and discovers
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 在西班牙，當表演者完成了一些不可能的、神奇的事情時，「阿拉，olé，olé，阿拉，偉大的，好極了，」難以理解，那就是——上帝的一瞥。這很棒，因為我們需要它。但是，對於舞者本人來說，第二天早上棘手的事情就來了，當他醒來並發現

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: that it's Tuesday at 11 a.m., and he's no longer a glimpse of God. He's just an aging mortal with really bad knees, and maybe he's never going to ascend to that height again. And maybe nobody will ever chant God's name again as he spins, and what is he then to do with the rest of his life?
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 今天是星期二上午 11 點，他再也看不見上帝了。他只是一個膝蓋很不好的老凡人，也許他永遠不會再爬到那個高度了。也許沒有人會在他旋轉時再次吟誦上帝的名字，那麼他的餘生該怎麼辦呢？

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: This is one of the most painful reconciliations to make in a creative life. But maybe it doesn't have to be quite so full of anguish if you never happened to believe, in the first place, that the most extraordinary aspects of your being came from you.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 這是創造性生活中最痛苦的和解之一。但如果你從一開始就沒有碰巧相信你存在中最非凡的面向來自於你，也許事情就不必那麼充滿痛苦。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: But maybe if you just believed that they were on loan to you from some unimaginable source for some exquisite portion of your life to be passed along when you're finished, with somebody else. And, you know, if we think about it this way, it starts to change everything.
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 但也許如果你只是相信它們是從某個難以想像的來源借給你的，以便在你結束時與其他人一起傳遞你生命中的一些美好部分。而且，你知道，如果我們這樣思考，一切就會開始改變。

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: This is how I've started to think, and this is certainly how I've been thinking in the last few months as I've been working on the book that will soon be published, as the dangerously, frighteningly over-anticipated follow up to my freakish success. And what I have to sort of keep telling myself
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 這就是我開始思考的方式，這當然也是我在過去幾個月裡一直在思考的方式，因為我一直在寫這本書，這本書即將出版，這是我驚人的成功的危險、令人恐懼的過度預期的後續行動。我必须不断告诉自己什么

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: when I get really psyched out about that is don't be afraid. Don't be daunted. Just do your job. Continue to show up for your piece of it, whatever that might be. If your job is to dance, do your dance. If the divine, cockeyed genius assigned to your case
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 當我對此感到非常興奮時，不要害怕。不要被嚇倒。做好你的工作吧。繼續展示你的作品，無論是什麼。如果你的工作是跳舞，那就跳舞吧。如果神聖的、偏斜的天才指派給你的案子

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: decides to let some sort of wonderment be glimpsed, for just one moment through your efforts, then "Olé!" And if not, do your dance anyhow. And "Olé!" to you, nonetheless. I believe this and I feel that we must teach it. "Olé!" to you, nonetheless,
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 決定透過你的努力讓某種奇蹟被瞥見，哪怕只是一瞬間，然後「Olé！」如果沒有，無論如何也要跳舞。還有「奧萊！」儘管如此，對你來說。我相信這一點，我覺得我們必須教導它。 「奧萊！」儘管如此，對你來說，

chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.en: just for having the sheer human love and stubbornness to keep showing up. June Cohen: Olé! Notifications alt+T
chimamanda_ngozi_adichie_the_danger_of_a.zh: 只是為了擁有純粹的人類之愛和固執地不斷出現。瓊‧科恩：奧萊！通知 alt+T

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I'm a storyteller. And I would like to tell you a few personal stories about what I like to call "the danger of the single story." I grew up on a university campus in eastern Nigeria. My mother says that I started reading at the age of two, although I think four is probably close to the truth.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我是一個說故事的人。我想告訴你們一些我稱之為「單一故事的危險」的個人故事。我在尼日利亞東部的一所大學校園長大。我母親說我兩歲就開始讀書，儘管我認為四歲可能更接近事實。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: So I was an early reader, and what I read were British and American children's books. I was also an early writer, and when I began to write, at about the age of seven, stories in pencil with crayon illustrations that my poor mother was obligated to read,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 所以我是一個很早的讀者，讀的都是英國和美國的兒童讀物。我也是一位早期作家，當我大約七歲的時候，我開始用鉛筆寫故事，配上蠟筆插圖，我可憐的母親不得不讀這些故事，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I wrote exactly the kinds of stories I was reading: All my characters were white and blue-eyed, they played in the snow, they ate apples, and they talked a lot about the weather, how lovely it was that the sun had come out. Now, this despite the fact that I lived in Nigeria.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我寫的故事和我正在讀的故事一模一樣：我所有的角色都是白色和藍眼睛的，他們在雪地裡玩耍，他們吃蘋果，他們談論了很多關於天氣的事情，太陽出來了是多麼可愛。現在，儘管我住在尼日利亞。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I had never been outside Nigeria. We didn't have snow, we ate mangoes, and we never talked about the weather, because there was no need to. My characters also drank a lot of ginger beer, because the characters in the British books I read drank ginger beer.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我從未出過尼日利亞。我們沒有下雪，我們吃芒果，我們從不談論天氣，因為沒有必要。我的角色也喝了很多薑汁啤酒，因為我讀過的英國書中的人物都喝薑汁啤酒。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Never mind that I had no idea what ginger beer was. And for many years afterwards, I would have a desperate desire to taste ginger beer. But that is another story. What this demonstrates, I think, is how impressionable and vulnerable we are in the face of a story, particularly as children.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 別介意我不知道薑汁啤酒是什麼。此後的許多年裡，我都非常渴望品嚐薑汁啤酒。但那是另一個故事了。我認為，這表明我們在面對故事時是多麼容易受影響和脆弱，尤其是作為孩子。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Because all I had read were books in which characters were foreign, I had become convinced that books by their very nature had to have foreigners in them and had to be about things with which I could not personally identify. Now, things changed when I discovered African books.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 因為我讀過的都是外來人物的書，所以我確信書籍本質上一定有外來人，而且一定是關於我個人無法認同的事物。現在，當我發現非洲書籍時，事情發生了變化。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: There weren't many of them available, and they weren't quite as easy to find as the foreign books. But because of writers like Chinua Achebe and Camara Laye, I went through a mental shift in my perception of literature. I realized that people like me, girls with skin the color of chocolate,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 可用的書並不多，而且不像外國書那麼容易找到。但由於奇努阿·阿契貝和卡馬拉·萊耶這樣的作家，我對文學的看法發生了精神轉變。我意識到像我這樣的人，有著巧克力色皮膚的女孩，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: whose kinky hair could not form ponytails, could also exist in literature. I started to write about things I recognized. Now, I loved those American and British books I read. They stirred my imagination. They opened up new worlds for me. But the unintended consequence
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 捲曲的頭髮無法紮成馬尾辮，也可能存在於文學中。我開始寫我認識的事。現在，我喜歡讀過的美國和英國書籍。他們激發了我的想像。他們為我打開了新世界。但意想不到的後果

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: was that I did not know that people like me could exist in literature. So what the discovery of African writers did for me was this: It saved me from having a single story of what books are. I come from a conventional, middle-class Nigerian family. My father was a professor.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 是我不知道像我這樣的人可以存在於文學中。所以，非洲作家的發現對我來說是這樣的：它讓我不再有關於書籍的單一故事。我來自一個傳統的奈及利亞中產階級家庭。我的父親是一位教授。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: My mother was an administrator. And so we had, as was the norm, live-in domestic help, who would often come from nearby rural villages. So, the year I turned eight, we got a new house boy. His name was Fide. The only thing my mother told us about him was that his family was very poor.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我的母親是一名行政人員。因此，按照慣例，我們有住家女傭，他們通常來自附近的鄉村。所以，我八歲那年，我們有了一個新的男僕。他的名字叫菲德。關於他，我母親唯一告訴我們的是，他的家庭非常貧窮。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: My mother sent yams and rice, and our old clothes, to his family. And when I didn't finish my dinner, my mother would say, "Finish your food! Don't you know? People like Fide's family have nothing." So I felt enormous pity for Fide's family. Then one Saturday, we went to his village to visit,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我媽媽送了他家山藥、米，還有我們的舊衣服。當我沒吃完晚餐時，媽媽就會說：「把飯吃完吧！你不知道嗎？像菲德一家這樣的人甚麼都沒有。」所以我對菲德的家人感到無比的遺憾。然後一個星期六，我們去他的村子參觀，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and his mother showed us a beautifully patterned basket made of dyed raffia that his brother had made. I was startled. It had not occurred to me that anybody in his family could actually make something. All I had heard about them was how poor they were,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他的母親向我們展示了他哥哥用染色拉菲草製成的圖案精美的籃子。我嚇了一跳。我沒想到他家裡的任何人都能真正做出一些東西。我只聽說過他們有多窮

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: so that it had become impossible for me to see them as anything else but poor. Their poverty was my single story of them. Years later, I thought about this when I left Nigeria to go to university in the United States. My American roommate was shocked by me.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 所以我只能把他們看作窮人。他們的貧窮是我關於他們的唯一故事。多年後，當我離開尼日利亞去美國上大學時，我想到了這個問題。我的美國室友被我震驚了。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: She asked where I had learned to speak English so well, and was confused when I said that Nigeria happened to have English as its official language. She asked if she could listen to what she called my "tribal music," and was consequently very disappointed when I produced my tape of Mariah Carey.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 她問我在哪裡學得英語說得這麼好，當我說尼日利亞剛好以英語為官方語言時，她很困惑。她問她是否可以聽聽她所謂的“部落音樂”，因此當我製作瑪麗亞凱莉的磁帶時她感到非常失望。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: She assumed that I did not know how to use a stove. What struck me was this: She had felt sorry for me even before she saw me. Her default position toward me, as an African, was a kind of patronizing, well-meaning pity. My roommate had a single story of Africa: a single story of catastrophe.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 她以為我不知道如何使用爐子。讓我震驚的是：她在見到我之前就已經為我感到難過了。身為一個非洲人，她對我的預設態度是一種居高臨下、善意的憐憫。我的室友有一個關於非洲的故事：一個關於災難的故事。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: In this single story, there was no possibility of Africans being similar to her in any way, no possibility of feelings more complex than pity, no possibility of a connection as human equals. I must say that before I went to the U.S., I didn't consciously identify as African.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 在這個單一的故事中，非洲人不可能在任何方面與她相似，不可能有比憐憫更複雜的感情，也不可能有人類平等的連結。我必須說，在我去美國之前，我並沒有自覺地認定自己是非洲人。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But in the U.S., whenever Africa came up, people turned to me. Never mind that I knew nothing about places like Namibia. But I did come to embrace this new identity, and in many ways I think of myself now as African. Although I still get quite irritable when Africa is referred to as a country,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但在美國，每當提到非洲，人們就會轉向我。別介意我對納米比亞這樣的地方一無所知。但我確實開始接受這個新身份，從很多方面來說，我現在認為自己是非洲人。雖然一提到非洲這個國家我還是很煩躁，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: the most recent example being my otherwise wonderful flight from Lagos two days ago, in which there was an announcement on the Virgin flight about the charity work in "India, Africa and other countries." So, after I had spent some years in the U.S. as an African,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 最近的一個例子是我兩天前從拉各斯起飛的精彩航班，維珍航班上發布了有關「印度、非洲和其他國家」慈善工作的公告。所以，當我作為非洲人在美國生活了幾年後，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I began to understand my roommate's response to me. If I had not grown up in Nigeria, and if all I knew about Africa were from popular images, I too would think that Africa was a place of beautiful landscapes, beautiful animals, and incomprehensible people,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我開始理解室友對我的反應。如果我不是在尼日利亞長大，如果我對非洲的了解全部來自流行的圖像，我也會認為非洲是一個擁有美麗風景、美麗動物和難以理解的人們的地方，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: fighting senseless wars, dying of poverty and AIDS, unable to speak for themselves and waiting to be saved by a kind, white foreigner. I would see Africans in the same way that I, as a child, had seen Fide's family. This single story of Africa ultimately comes, I think, from Western literature.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 進行一場毫無意義的戰爭，死於貧困和愛滋病，無法為自己說話，等待一個善良的白人外國人的拯救。我看待非洲人的方式就像我小時候看待菲德的家人。我認為，這個關於非洲的單一故事最終來自西方文學。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Now, here is a quote from the writing of a London merchant called John Lok, who sailed to west Africa in 1561 and kept a fascinating account of his voyage. After referring to the black Africans as "beasts who have no houses," he writes, "They are also people without heads,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 這裡引用一位名叫約翰·洛克 (John Lok) 的倫敦商人的著作，他於 1561 年航行到西非，並對他的航行進行了精彩的記錄。在將非洲黑人稱為「沒有房子的野獸」之後，他寫道，「他們也是沒有頭的人，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: having their mouth and eyes in their breasts." Now, I've laughed every time I've read this. And one must admire the imagination of John Lok. But what is important about his writing is that it represents the beginning of a tradition of telling African stories in the West:
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 「現在，每次讀到這篇文章我都會笑。人們必須欽佩約翰洛克的想像。但他的寫作的重要之處在於，它代表了在西方講述非洲故事的傳統的開始：

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: A tradition of Sub-Saharan Africa as a place of negatives, of difference, of darkness, of people who, in the words of the wonderful poet Rudyard Kipling, are "half devil, half child." And so, I began to realize that my American roommate must have throughout her life
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 撒哈拉以南非洲的傳統是一個充滿負面、差異、黑暗的地方，用傑出詩人拉迪亞德·吉卜林的話說，這裡的人們「一半是魔鬼，一半是孩子」。於是，我開始意識到，我的美國室友一定一輩子都在

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: seen and heard different versions of this single story, as had a professor, who once told me that my novel was not "authentically African." Now, I was quite willing to contend that there were a number of things wrong with the novel, that it had failed in a number of places,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我看到和聽過這個單一故事的不同版本，就像一位教授一樣，他曾經告訴我，我的小說不是「真正的非洲」。現在，我很願意承認這本小說有很多問題，它在很多地方都失敗了，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: but I had not quite imagined that it had failed at achieving something called African authenticity. In fact, I did not know what African authenticity was. The professor told me that my characters were too much like him, an educated and middle-class man. My characters drove cars.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但我沒想到它未能實現所謂的非洲真實性。事實上，我不知道什麼是非洲的真實性。教授告訴我，我的角色太像他了，一個受過教育的中產階級男人。我的角色開車。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: They were not starving. Therefore they were not authentically African. But I must quickly add that I too am just as guilty in the question of the single story. A few years ago, I visited Mexico from the U.S. The political climate in the U.S. at the time was tense,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們並沒有挨餓。因此他們不是真正的非洲人。但我必須很快補充一點，我對單一故事的問題也同樣感到內疚。幾年前，我從美國訪問墨西哥，當時美國政治氣氛緊張，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and there were debates going on about immigration. And, as often happens in America, immigration became synonymous with Mexicans. There were endless stories of Mexicans as people who were fleecing the healthcare system, sneaking across the border, being arrested at the border, that sort of thing.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 關於移民的爭論正在進行中。而且，正如在美國經常發生的那樣，移民成為墨西哥人的代名詞。關於墨西哥人欺騙醫療保健系統、偷偷越過邊境、在邊境被捕之類的故事層出不窮。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I remember walking around on my first day in Guadalajara, watching the people going to work, rolling up tortillas in the marketplace, smoking, laughing. I remember first feeling slight surprise. And then, I was overwhelmed with shame.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我記得在瓜達拉哈拉的第一天四處走動，看著人們去上班，在市場上捲玉米餅，抽煙，大笑。我記得第一個感覺有點驚訝。然後我就被羞恥感淹沒了。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I realized that I had been so immersed in the media coverage of Mexicans that they had become one thing in my mind, the abject immigrant. I had bought into the single story of Mexicans and I could not have been more ashamed of myself. So that is how to create a single story,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我意識到，我如此沉浸在媒體對墨西哥人的報導中，以至於他們在我的腦海中已經成為一件事：卑鄙的移民。我相信墨西哥人的單一故事，我為自己感到羞愧。這就是如何創建一個故事，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: show a people as one thing, as only one thing, over and over again, and that is what they become. It is impossible to talk about the single story without talking about power. There is a word, an Igbo word, that I think about whenever I think about the power structures of the world, and it is "nkali."
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 一遍又一遍地將一個民族展示為一件事，僅僅一件事，然後他們就變成了這樣。談論單一故事就不可能不談權力。每當我想到世界的權力結構時，我就會想到一個詞，一個伊博語詞，它就是「nkali」。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: It's a noun that loosely translates to "to be greater than another." stories too are defined by the principle of nkali: How they are told, who tells them, when they're told, how many stories are told, are really dependent on power. Power is the ability not just to tell the story of another person,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 這是一個名詞，可以大致翻譯為「比另一個更偉大」。故事也是由 nkali 原則定義的：如何講述、由誰講述、何時講述、講述多少故事，實際上都取決於權力。權力不僅僅是講述另一個人的故事的能力，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: but to make it the definitive story of that person. The Palestinian poet Mourid Barghouti writes that if you want to dispossess a people, the simplest way to do it is to tell their story and to start with, "secondly." Start the story with the arrows of the Native Americans,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 而是讓它成為那個人的最終故事。巴勒斯坦詩人穆里德·巴爾古提（Mourid Barghouti）寫道，如果你想剝奪一個民族的權利，最簡單的方法就是講述他們的故事，然後從「其次」開始。故事從美洲原住民的箭開始，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and not with the arrival of the British, and you have an entirely different story. Start the story with the failure of the African state, and not with the colonial creation of the African state, and you have an entirely different story. I recently spoke at a university
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 而隨著英國人的到來，情況就完全不同了。從非洲國家的失敗開始故事，而不是從非洲國家的殖民創建開始，你會得到一個完全不同的故事。我最近在一所大學演講

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: where a student told me that it was such a shame that Nigerian men were physical abusers I told him that I had just read a novel called "American Psycho" -- -- and that it was such a shame that young Americans were serial murderers. Now, obviously I said this in a fit of mild irritation.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 一名學生告訴我，尼日利亞男人是身體虐待者，這真是太恥辱了，我告訴他我剛剛讀了一本叫《美國精神病人》的小說——而且美國年輕人是連環殺人犯，這真是太恥辱了。現在，顯然我是帶著輕微的刺激才這麼說的。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But it would never have occurred to me to think that just because I had read a novel in which a character was a serial killer that he was somehow representative of all Americans. This is not because I am a better person than that student, but because of America's cultural and economic power,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但我永遠不會想到，僅僅因為我讀過一本小說，其中一個角色是連環殺手，他在某種程度上代表了所有美國人。這不是因為我比那位學生好，而是因為美國的文化和經濟實力，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I had many stories of America. I had read Tyler and Updike and Steinbeck and Gaitskill. I did not have a single story of America. When I learned, some years ago, that writers were expected to have had really unhappy childhoods to be successful,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我有很多關於美國的故事。我讀過泰勒、厄普代克、史坦貝克和蓋茲基爾的書。我沒有一個關於美國的故事。幾年前，當我得知作家必須擁有不快樂的童年才能獲得成功時，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I began to think about how I could invent horrible things my parents had done to me. But the truth is that I had a very happy childhood, full of laughter and love, in a very close-knit family. But I also had grandfathers who died in refugee camps.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我開始思考如何編造出我父母對我做過的可怕的事。但事實是，我在一個非常親密的家庭中度過了一個非常幸福的童年，充滿歡笑和愛。但我也有死於難民營的祖父。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: My cousin Polle died because he could not get adequate healthcare. One of my closest friends, Okoloma, died in a plane crash because our fire trucks did not have water. I grew up under repressive military governments that devalued education, so that sometimes, my parents were not paid their salaries.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我的表弟波勒因無法獲得足夠的醫療保健而去世。我最親密的朋友之一奧科洛馬在一次飛機失事中喪生，因為我們的消防車沒有水。我是在軍政府的壓制下長大的，軍政府貶低了教育的價值，所以有時我的父母拿不到薪水。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: And so, as a child, I saw jam disappear from the breakfast table, then margarine disappeared, then bread became too expensive, then milk became rationed. And most of all, a kind of normalized political fear invaded our lives. All of these stories make me who I am.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 因此，當我還是個孩子的時候，我看到果醬從早餐桌上消失了，然後人造奶油消失了，然後麵包變得太貴了，然後牛奶開始定量供應。最重要的是，一種常態化的政治恐懼侵入了我們的生活。所有這些故事造就了現在的我。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: But to insist on only these negative stories is to flatten my experience and to overlook the many other stories that formed me. The single story creates stereotypes, and the problem with stereotypes is not that they are untrue, but that they are incomplete. They make one story become the only story.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但堅持只講這些負面故事，就是抹平我的經歷，而忽略了塑造我的許多其他故事。單一的故事會產生刻板印象，而刻板印象的問題不是它們不真實，而是它們不完整。他們讓一個故事成為唯一的故事。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Of course, Africa is a continent full of catastrophes: There are immense ones, such as the horrific rapes in Congo and depressing ones, such as the fact that 5,000 people apply for one job vacancy in Nigeria. But there are other stories that are not about catastrophe,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 當然，非洲是一個充滿災難的大陸：有巨大的災難，例如剛果的可怕強姦案，也有令人沮喪的災難，例如尼日利亞一個職位空缺就有5000人申請。但還有其他一些與災難無關的故事，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and it is very important, it is just as important, to talk about them. I've always felt that it is impossible to engage properly with a place or a person without engaging with all of the stories of that place and that person. The consequence of the single story is this: It robs people of dignity.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 談論它們非常重要，同樣重要。我一直覺得，如果不了解那個地方和那個人的所有故事，就不可能正確地與一個地方或一個人交往。單一故事的後果是：它剝奪了人們的尊嚴。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: It makes our recognition of our equal humanity difficult. It emphasizes how we are different rather than how we are similar. So what if before my Mexican trip, I had followed the immigration debate from both sides, the U.S. and the Mexican? What if my mother had told us that Fide's family was poor
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 它使我們難以承認我們平等的人性。它強調我們的不同之處，而不是我們的相似之處。那麼，如果在我的墨西哥之行之前，我關注了美國和墨西哥雙方的移民辯論呢？如果我媽告訴我們菲德家很窮怎麼辦？

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and hardworking? What if we had an African television network that broadcast diverse African stories all over the world? What the Nigerian writer Chinua Achebe calls "a balance of stories." What if my roommate knew about my Nigerian publisher, a remarkable man who left his job in a bank
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 和勤奮？如果我們有一個非洲電視網絡在世界各地播放不同的非洲故事會怎麼樣？尼日利亞作家奇努阿·阿契貝（Chinua Achebe）稱之為「故事的平衡」。如果我的室友知道我的奈及利亞出版商，一個辭去了銀行工作的傑出人士，該怎麼辦

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: to follow his dream and start a publishing house? Now, the conventional wisdom was that Nigerians don't read literature. He felt that people who could read, would read, if you made literature affordable and available to them. Shortly after he published my first novel,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 追隨他的夢想並創辦一家出版社？現在，傳統觀點是尼日利亞人不讀文學。他認為，如果你能讓文學作品負擔得起並可供他們使用，那麼那些能夠閱讀的人就會閱讀。在他出版我的第一本小說後不久，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I went to a TV station in Lagos to do an interview, and a woman who worked there as a messenger came up to me and said, "I really liked your novel. I didn't like the ending. Now, you must write a sequel, and this is what will happen ..." And she went on to tell me what to write in the sequel.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我去拉各斯的一家電視台接受採訪，一位在那裡擔任信差的女士走過來對我說：「我真的很喜歡你的小說。我不喜歡結局。現在，你必須寫續集，這就是將會發生的事情……」她接著告訴我續集中要寫什麼。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I was not only charmed, I was very moved. Here was a woman, part of the ordinary masses of Nigerians, who were not supposed to be readers. She had not only read the book, but she had taken ownership of it and felt justified in telling me what to write in the sequel.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 我不僅被迷住了，而且非常感動。這是一位女性，屬於一般尼日利亞人，不應該成為讀者。她不僅讀過這本書，而且已經擁有了這本書，並覺得有理由告訴我在續集中要寫什麼。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Now, what if my roommate knew about my friend Funmi Iyanda, a fearless woman who hosts a TV show in Lagos, and is determined to tell the stories that we prefer to forget? What if my roommate knew about the heart procedure that was performed in the Lagos hospital last week?
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 現在，如果我的室友知道我的朋友 Funmi Iyanda（一位在拉各斯主持電視節目的無所畏懼的女性）並決心講述我們寧願忘記的故事，該怎麼辦？如果我的室友知道上週在拉各斯醫院進行的心臟手術怎麼辦？

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: What if my roommate knew about contemporary Nigerian music, talented people singing in English and Pidgin, and Igbo and Yoruba and Ijo, mixing influences from Jay-Z to Fela to Bob Marley to their grandfathers. What if my roommate knew about the female lawyer
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 如果我的室友了解當代尼日利亞音樂，了解用英語和洋涇浜語演唱的才華橫溢的人，以及伊博語、約魯巴語和伊喬語，融合了從 Jay-Z 到費拉、鮑勃·馬利再到他們的祖父的影響，那會怎麼樣呢？如果我的室友知道女律師的事怎麼辦

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: who recently went to court in Nigeria to challenge a ridiculous law that required women to get their husband's consent before renewing their passports? What if my roommate knew about Nollywood, full of innovative people making films despite great technical odds, films so popular
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 誰最近在尼日利亞向法庭提出質疑一項荒謬的法律，該法律要求婦女在更新護照之前必須徵得丈夫的同意？如果我的室友了解諾萊塢，那裡充滿了創新人才，儘管技術難度很大，電影如此受歡迎，但他們仍然在製作電影，那會怎麼樣？

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: that they really are the best example of Nigerians consuming what they produce? What if my roommate knew about my wonderfully ambitious hair braider, who has just started her own business selling hair extensions? Or about the millions of other Nigerians who start businesses and sometimes fail,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 他們真的是尼日利亞人消費他們生產的產品的最佳例子嗎？如果我的室友知道我雄心勃勃的編髮師剛開始自己的接發生意怎麼辦？或者關於數以百萬計的其他尼日利亞人創業但有時會失敗，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: but continue to nurse ambition? Every time I am home I am confronted with the usual sources of irritation for most Nigerians: our failed infrastructure, our failed government, but also by the incredible resilience of people who thrive despite the government, rather than because of it.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 但繼續培育野心？每次我回到家，我都會遇到大多數尼日利亞人常見的煩惱：我們失敗的基礎設施，我們失敗的政府，而且還有人們令人難以置信的韌性，他們在政府的幫助下茁壯成長，而不是因為政府。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: I teach writing workshops in Lagos every summer, and it is amazing to me how many people apply, how many people are eager to write, to tell stories. My Nigerian publisher and I have just started a non-profit called Farafina Trust, and we have big dreams of building libraries
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 每年夏天我都會在拉各斯教授寫作研討會，令我驚訝的是有多少人申請，有多少人渴望寫作、講故事。我和我的奈及利亞出版商剛創辦了一個名為 Farafina Trust 的非營利組織，我們有建立圖書館的遠大夢想

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: and refurbishing libraries that already exist and providing books for state schools that don't have anything in their libraries, and also of organizing lots and lots of workshops, in reading and writing, for all the people who are eager to tell our many stories. Stories matter. Many stories matter.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 翻新現有的圖書館，為圖書館裡沒有任何書籍的公立學校提供書籍，並為所有渴望講述我們的故事的人們組織大量閱讀和寫作研討會。故事很重要。許多故事都很重要。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: Stories have been used to dispossess and to malign, but stories can also be used to empower and to humanize. Stories can break the dignity of a people, but stories can also repair that broken dignity. The American writer Alice Walker wrote this about her Southern relatives who had moved to the North.
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 故事被用來剝奪和誹謗，但故事也可以用來賦予權力和人性化。故事可以打破一個民族的尊嚴，但故事也可以修復受損的尊嚴。美國作家愛麗絲沃克（Alice Walker）寫了這篇關於她移居北方的南方親戚的故事。

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: She introduced them to a book about the Southern life that they had left behind. "They sat around, reading the book themselves, listening to me read the book, and a kind of paradise was regained." I would like to end with this thought: That when we reject the single story,
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 她向他們介紹了一本關於他們留下的南方生活的書。 「他們圍坐在一起，自己看書，聽我讀書，重新找回了一種天堂。」我想以這樣的想法結束：當我們拒絕單一故事時，

adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.en: when we realize that there is never a single story about any place, we regain a kind of paradise. Notifications alt+T
adam_grant_the_surprising_habits_of_orig.zh: 當我們意識到任何地方都不存在單一的故事時，我們就重新獲得了一種天堂。通知 alt+T

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Seven years ago, a student came to me and asked me to invest in his company. He said, "I'm working with three friends, and we're going to try to disrupt an industry by selling stuff online." And I said, "OK, you guys spent the whole summer on this, right?"
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 七年前，有個學生來找我，要我投資他的公司。他說：“我正在與三個朋友合作，我們將嘗試通過在線銷售產品來顛覆一個行業。”我說：“好吧，你們整個夏天都在做這個，對吧？”

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: "No, we all took internships just in case it doesn't work out." "All right, but you're going to go in full time once you graduate." "Not exactly. We've all lined up backup jobs." Six months go by, it's the day before the company launches, and there is still not a functioning website.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 「不，我們都去實習，以防萬一不行。」 「好吧，不過畢業後你就得全職工作了。」 「不完全是。我們都已經安排好了備份工作。」六個月過去了，公司成立的前一天，網站仍然沒有正常運作。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: "You guys realize, the entire company is a website. That's literally all it is." So I obviously declined to invest. And they ended up naming the company Warby Parker. They sell glasses online. They were recently recognized as the world's most innovative company and valued at over a billion dollars.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 「你們知道，整個公司就是一個網站。這就是它的全部。」所以我顯然拒絕了投資。他們最終將公司命名為 Warby Parker。他們在線銷售眼鏡。他們最近被公認為世界上最具創新力的公司，估值超過十億美元。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And now? My wife handles our investments. Why was I so wrong? To find out, I've been studying people that I come to call "originals." Originals are nonconformists, people who not only have new ideas but take action to champion them. They are people who stand out and speak up.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 現在呢？我的妻子負責我們的投資。為什麼我錯得這麼離譜？為了找到答案，我一直在研究那些我稱之為「原創者」的人。原創者是不墨守成規的人，他們不僅有新想法，而且採取行動來捍衛它們。他們是站出來說話的人。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Originals drive creativity and change in the world. They're the people you want to bet on. And they look nothing like I expected. I want to show you today three things I've learned about recognizing originals and becoming a little bit more like them. So the first reason that I passed on Warby Parker
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 原創推動世界的創意與改變。他們是你想賭的人。他們看起來一點也不像我想像的那樣。今天我想向你們展示我在識別原創作品並變得更像他們方面學到的三件事。所以我放棄 Warby Parker 的第一個原因

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: was they were really slow getting off the ground. Now, you are all intimately familiar with the mind of a procrastinator. Well, I have a confession for you. I'm the opposite. I'm a precrastinator. Yes, that's an actual term. You know that panic you feel a few hours before a big deadline
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 他們的起步速度真的很慢。現在，你們都非常了解拖延者的想法。好吧，我有件事要向你坦白。我恰恰相反。我是一個提前計劃者。是的，這是一個實際的術語。你知道在截止日期前幾個小時你感受到的恐慌

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: when you haven't done anything yet. I just feel that a few months ahead of time. So this started early: when I was a kid, I took Nintendo games very seriously. I would wake up at 5am, start playing and not stop until I had mastered them. Eventually it got so out of hand that a local newspaper came
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 當你還沒做任何事的時候。我只是覺得提前了幾個月。所以這很早就開始了：當我還是個孩子的時候，我非常認真地對待任天堂遊戲。我會早上 5 點起床，開始演奏，直到我掌握它們為止。最後事情變得一發不可收拾，以至於當地報紙來了

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and did a story on the dark side of Nintendo, starring me. Since then, I have traded hair for teeth. But this served me well in college, because I finished my senior thesis four months before the deadline. And I was proud of that, until a few years ago.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 並製作了一個由我主演的關於任天堂陰暗面的故事。從那時起，我就用頭髮換了牙齒。但這在大學裡對我很有幫助，因為我在截止日期前四個月完成了我的畢業論文。直到幾年前，我還為此感到自豪。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: I had a student named Jihae, who came to me and said, "I have my most creative ideas when I'm procrastinating." And I was like, "That's cute, where are the four papers you owe me?" No, she was one of our most creative students,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我有一個名叫 Jihae 的學生，他來找我說：「當我拖延的時候，我會產生最有創意的想法。」我當時想，「太可愛了，你欠我的四篇論文在哪裡？」不，她是我們最有創意的學生之一，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and as an organizational psychologist, this is the kind of idea that I test. So I challenged her to get some data. She goes into a bunch of companies. She has people fill out surveys about how often they procrastinate. Then she gets their bosses to rate how creative and innovative they are.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 作為一名組織心理學家，我正在測試這種想法。所以我要求她取得一些數據。她進過很多公司。她讓人們填寫有關他們拖延的頻率的調查。然後她讓他們的老闆評價他們的創造力和創新能力。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And sure enough, the precrastinators like me, who rush in and do everything early are rated as less creative than people who procrastinate moderately. So I want to know what happens to the chronic procrastinators. She was like, "I don't know. They didn't fill out my survey." No, here are our results.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 果然，像我這樣提前行動的人，那些急於完成所有事情的人，被認為比那些適度拖延的人缺乏創造力。所以我想知道長期拖延者會發生什麼事。她說，「我不知道。他們沒有填寫我的調查。」不，這是我們的結果。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: You actually do see that the people who wait until the last minute are so busy goofing off that they don't have any new ideas. And on the flip side, the people who race in are in such a frenzy of anxiety that they don't have original thoughts either. There's a sweet spot where originals seem to live.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 事實上，你確實看到那些等到最後一刻的人都忙著偷懶，沒有任何新的想法。另一方面，參加比賽的人也處於瘋狂的焦慮之中，他們也沒有原創的想法。原創者似乎生活在一個甜蜜的地方。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Maybe original people just have bad work habits. Maybe procrastinating does not cause creativity. To find out, we designed some experiments. We asked people to generate new business ideas, and then we get independent readers to evaluate how creative and useful they are.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 也許原始人只是工作習慣不好。也許拖延不會帶來創造力。為了找到答案，我們設計了一些實驗。我們要求人們提出新的商業想法，然後我們讓獨立的讀者來評估他們的創意和有用性。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And some of them are asked to do the task right away. Others we randomly assign to procrastinate by dangling Minesweeper in front of them for either five or 10 minutes. And sure enough, the moderate procrastinators are 16 percent more creative than the other two groups.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 其中一些人被要求立即完成任務。我們隨機分配其他人拖延，讓掃雷器在他們面前晃來晃去五分鐘或十分鐘。果然，適度拖延者的創造力比其他兩組人高出 16%。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Now, Minesweeper is awesome, but it's not the driver of the effect, because if you play the game first before you learn about the task, there's no creativity boost. It's only when you're told that you're going to be working on this problem, and then you start procrastinating,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 現在，《掃雷》很棒，但它並不是效果的驅動因素，因為如果你在了解任務之前先玩遊戲，就不會提升創造力。只有當你被告知你要解決這個問題時，你才開始拖延，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: but the task is still active in the back of your mind, that you start to incubate. Procrastination gives you time to consider divergent ideas, to think in nonlinear ways, to make unexpected leaps. So just as we were finishing these experiments, I was starting to write a book about originals,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但任務仍然活躍在你的腦海中，你開始醞釀。拖延讓你有時間考慮不同的想法，以非線性的方式思考，做出意想不到的飛躍。因此，就在我們完成這些實驗時，我開始寫一本關於原創的書，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and I thought, "This is the perfect time to teach myself to procrastinate, while writing a chapter on procrastination." So I metaprocrastinated, and like any self-respecting precrastinator, I woke up early the next morning and I made a to-do list with steps on how to procrastinate.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 我想，「這是自學拖延的最佳時機，同時寫一章關於拖延的內容。」所以我開始拖延，就像任何有自尊的拖延者一樣，我第二天早上很早就醒來，並列出了一份待辦事項清單，其中包含如何拖延的步驟。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And then I worked diligently toward my goal of not making progress toward my goal. I started writing the procrastination chapter, and one day -- I was halfway through -- I literally put it away in mid-sentence But when I came back to it, I had all sorts of new ideas. As Aaron Sorkin put it,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 然後我就朝著自己的目標努力奮鬥，沒有任何進展。我開始寫拖延症一章，有一天——我已經寫到一半了——我真的把它寫到一半了，但當我回到它時，我有了各種各樣的新想法。正如亞倫·索金所說，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: "You call it procrastinating. I call it thinking." And along the way I discovered that a lot of great originals in history were procrastinators. Take Leonardo da Vinci. He toiled on and off for 16 years on the Mona Lisa. He felt like a failure. He wrote as much in his journal.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 「你稱之為拖延。我稱之為思考。」一路上我發現歷史上許多偉大的原創者都是拖延者。以達文西為例。他為《蒙娜麗莎》斷斷續續工作了16年。他感覺自己是個失敗者。他在日記中寫下了同樣的內容。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: But some of the diversions he took in optics transformed the way that he modeled light and made him into a much better painter. What about Martin Luther King, Jr.? The night before the biggest speech of his life, the March on Washington, he was up past 3am, rewriting it.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但他在光學方面的一些轉變改變了他塑造光的方式，並使他成為更好的畫家。馬丁路德金呢？在他一生中最重要的演講「向華盛頓進軍」的前一天晚上，他凌晨 3 點多就起來重寫了演講。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: He's sitting in the audience waiting for his turn to go onstage, and he is still scribbling notes and crossing out lines. When he gets onstage, 11 minutes in, he leaves his prepared remarks to utter four words that changed the course of history: "I have a dream." That was not in the script.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 他坐在觀眾席上，等待輪到他上台，他仍在寫筆記和劃掉台詞。 11 分鐘後，當他走上舞台時，他留下了準備好的講話，說出了四個改變歷史進程的詞：「我有一個夢想。」那是劇本裡沒有的。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: By delaying the task of finalizing the speech until the very last minute, he left himself open to the widest range of possible ideas. And because the text wasn't set in stone, he had freedom to improvise. Procrastinating is a vice when it comes to productivity, but it can be a virtue for creativity.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 透過將完成演講的任務推遲到最後一刻，他讓自己對最廣泛的可能想法保持開放的態度。因為文本並不是一成不變的，所以他可以自由地即興創作。拖延對生產力來說是一種惡習，但對創造力來說卻是一種美德。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: What you see with a lot of great originals is that they are quick to start but they're slow to finish. And this is what I missed with Warby Parker. When they were dragging their heels for six months, I looked at them and said, "You know, a lot of other companies are starting to sell glasses online."
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你會看到很多偉大的原創作品開始得很快，但完成得很慢。這就是我對 Warby Parker 的懷念。當他們拖延六個月時，我看著他們說：“你知道，很多其他公司都開始在網上銷售眼鏡。”

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: They missed the first-mover advantage. But what I didn't realize was they were spending all that time trying to figure out how to get people to be comfortable ordering glasses online. And it turns out the first-mover advantage is mostly a myth. Look at a classic study of over 50 product categories,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 他們錯過了先發優勢。但我沒有意識到他們花了所有時間試圖弄清楚如何讓人們放心地在網路上訂購眼鏡。事實證明，先發優勢大多是個神話。看看 50 多個產品類別的經典研究，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: comparing the first movers who created the market with the improvers who introduced something different and better. What you see is that the first movers had a failure rate of 47 percent, compared with only 8 percent for the improvers. Look at Facebook, waiting to build a social network
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 將創造市場的先行者與引入不同且更好的東西的改進者進行比較。您可以看到，先行者的失敗率為 47%，而改進者的失敗率為 8%。看看Facebook，等待建立一個社群網絡

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: until after Myspace and Friendster. Look at Google, waiting for years after Altavista and Yahoo. It's much easier to improve on somebody else's idea than it is to create something new from scratch. So the lesson I learned is that to be original you don't have to be first.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 直到 Myspace 和 Friendster 之後。看看谷歌，它在 Altavista 和雅虎之後等待了數年。改進別人的想法比從頭開始創造新的東西要容易得多。所以我學到的教訓是，要原創，你不必成為第一名。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: You just have to be different and better. But that wasn't the only reason I passed on Warby Parker. They were also full of doubts. They had backup plans lined up, and that made me doubt that they had the courage to be original, because I expected that originals would look something like this.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你只需要變得與眾不同並且變得更好。但這並不是讓我放棄沃比·帕克的唯一原因。他們心中也充滿了疑惑。他們制定了備用計劃，這讓我懷疑他們是否有勇氣原創，因為我預計原創會是這樣的。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Now, on the surface, a lot of original people look confident, but behind the scenes, they feel the same fear and doubt that the rest of us do. They just manage it differently. Let me show you: this is a depiction of how the creative process works for most of us.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 現在，許多原創者表面上看起來很自信，但在幕後，他們和我們其他人一樣感到恐懼和懷疑。他們只是以不同的方式管理它。讓我告訴你：這是我們大多數人的創作過程的描述。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Now, in my research, I discovered there are two different kinds of doubt. There's self-doubt and idea doubt. Self-doubt is paralyzing. It leads you to freeze. But idea doubt is energizing. It motivates you to test, to experiment, to refine, just like MLK did. And so the key to being original
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 現在，在我的研究中，我發現有兩種不同的懷疑。有自我懷疑和想法懷疑。自我懷疑會讓人癱瘓。它會讓你凍結。但對想法的懷疑卻充滿活力。它激勵你去測試、去實驗、去改進，就像 MLK 所做的那樣。所以原創的關鍵

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: is just a simple thing of avoiding the leap from step three to step four. Instead of saying, "I'm crap," you say, "The first few drafts are always crap, and I'm just not there yet." So how do you get there? Well, there's a clue, it turns out, in the Internet browser that you use.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 只是避免從第三步跳到第四步的簡單事情。你不要說“我很糟糕”，而應該說“最初的幾稿總是很糟糕，我只是還沒有達到那個水平”。那麼要如何到達那裡呢？事實證明，您使用的網路瀏覽器中存在一條線索。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: We can predict your job performance and your commitment just by knowing what web browser you use. Now, some of you are not going to like the results of this study -- But there is good evidence that Firefox and Chrome users significantly outperform Internet Explorer and Safari users.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 只需了解您使用的網頁瀏覽器，我們就可以預測您的工作表現和承諾。現在，有些人可能不會喜歡這項研究的結果——但有充分的證據表明，Firefox 和 Chrome 用戶的表現明顯優於 Internet Explorer 和 Safari 用戶。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: They also stay in their jobs 15 percent longer, by the way. Why? It's not a technical advantage. The four browser groups on average have similar typing speed and they also have similar levels of computer knowledge. It's about how you got the browser. Because if you use Internet Explorer or Safari,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 順便說一句，他們的工作時間也延長了 15%。為什麼？這不是技術優勢。這四個瀏覽器組的平均打字速度相似，而且他們的電腦知識程度也相似。這是關於你如何獲得瀏覽器的。因為如果您使用 Internet Explorer 或 Safari，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: those came preinstalled on your computer, and you accepted the default option that was handed to you. If you wanted Firefox or Chrome, you had to doubt the default and ask, is there a different option out there, and then be a little resourceful and download a new browser.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 這些已預先安裝在您的電腦上，並且您接受了提供給您的預設選項。如果你想要 Firefox 或 Chrome，你必須懷疑預設設定並詢問是否有不同的選擇，然後發揮一點足智多謀並下載一個新的瀏覽器。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: So people hear about this study and they're like, "Great, if I want to get better at my job, I just need to upgrade my browser?" No, it's about being the kind of person who takes the initiative to doubt the default and look for a better option. And if you do that well,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 所以人們聽到這項研究後會想，「太好了，如果我想在工作中做得更好，我只需要升級我的瀏覽器就可以了？」不，這是關於成為那種主動懷疑默認並尋找更好選擇的人。如果你做得很好，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: you will open yourself up to the opposite of déjà vu. There's a name for it. It's called vuja de. Vuja de is when you look at something you've seen many times before and all of a sudden see it with fresh eyes. It's a screenwriter who looks at a movie script
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你會向似曾相識的反面敞開心扉。它有一個名字。它被稱為“vuja de”。 Vuja de 是指當你看著以前看過很多次的東西時，突然用新的眼光看待它。這是一個看電影劇本的編劇

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: that can't get the green light for more than half a century. In every past version, the main character has been an evil queen. But Jennifer Lee starts to question whether that makes sense. She rewrites the first act, reinvents the villain as a tortured hero
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 半個多世紀以來都無法獲得批准。在過去的每個版本中，主角都是邪惡的女王。但詹妮弗·李開始質疑這是否有意義。她重寫了第一幕，將反派重塑為飽受折磨的英雄

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and Frozen becomes the most successful animated movie ever. So there's a simple message from this story. When you feel doubt, don't let it go. What about fear? Originals feel fear, too. They're afraid of failing, but what sets them apart from the rest of us
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 《冰雪奇緣》成為有史以來最成功的動畫電影。这个故事传达了一个简单的信息。当你感到怀疑时，不要放开它。恐懼呢？原著者也感到恐惧。他們害怕失敗，但什麼讓他們與我們其他人不同

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: is that they're even more afraid of failing to try. They know you can fail by starting a business that goes bankrupt or by failing to start a business at all. They know that in the long run, our biggest regrets are not our actions but our inactions.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 是他們更害怕失敗。他們知道你可能會因為創辦一家破產的企業或根本無法創辦企業而失敗。他們知道，從長遠來看，我們最大的遺憾不是我們的行動，而是我們的不作為。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: The things we wish we could redo, if you look at the science, are the chances not taken. Elon Musk told me recently, he didn't expect Tesla to succeed. He was sure the first few SpaceX launches would fail to make it to orbit, let alone get back, but it was too important not to try.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 如果你看看科學，我們希望能夠重做的事情就是我們沒有抓住的機會。伊隆馬斯克最近告訴我，他沒想到特斯拉會成功。他確信 SpaceX 的前幾次發射都無法進入軌道，更不用說返回軌道了，但這太重要了，不能不嘗試。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: And for so many of us, when we have an important idea, we don't bother to try. But I have some good news for you. You are not going to get judged on your bad ideas. A lot of people think they will. If you look across industries and ask people about their biggest idea, their most important suggestion,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 對我們很多人來說，當我們有一個重要的想法時，我們不會費心去嘗試。但我有一些好消息要告訴你。你不會因為你的壞想法而被評判。很多人認為他們會的。如果你縱觀各個行業並詢問人們他們最大的想法、最重要的建議，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: 85 percent of them stayed silent instead of speaking up. They were afraid of embarrassing themselves, of looking stupid. But guess what? Originals have lots and lots of bad ideas, tons of them, in fact. Take the guy who invented this. Do you care that he came up with a talking doll so creepy
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 85%的人保持沉默，沒有發聲。他們害怕讓自己難堪，害怕看起來很愚蠢。但你猜怎麼著？事實上，原創作品有很多很多糟糕的想法。以發明這個的人為例。你介意他想出了一個如此令人毛骨悚然的會說話的娃娃嗎

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: that it scared not only kids but adults, too? No. You celebrate Thomas Edison for pioneering the light bulb. If you look across fields, the greatest originals are the ones who fail the most, because they're the ones who try the most. Take classical composers, the best of the best.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 它不僅嚇壞了孩子，還嚇壞了大人？不。您慶祝托馬斯愛迪生發明了燈泡。如果你縱觀各個領域，最偉大的原創者就是那些失敗最多的人，因為他們是嘗試最多的人。以古典作曲家為例，他們是最優秀的作曲家。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Why do some of them get more pages in encyclopedias than others and also have their compositions rerecorded more times? One of the best predictors is the sheer volume of compositions that they generate. The more output you churn out, the more variety you get
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 為什麼他們中的一些人比其他人在百科全書中獲得了更多頁數，並且他們的作品被重新錄製的次數更多？最好的預測因素之一是它們生成的作品的絕對數量。你生產的產品越多，你獲得的多樣性就越多

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: and the better your chances of stumbling on something truly original. Even the three icons of classical music -- Bach, Beethoven, Mozart -- had to generate hundreds and hundreds of compositions to come up with a much smaller number of masterpieces. Now, you may be wondering,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 你就越有機會偶然發現真正原創的東西。即使是古典音樂的三大偶像——巴赫、貝多芬、莫札特——也必須創作成百上千的作品才能創作出數量少得多的傑作。現在，您可能想知道，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: how did this guy become great without doing a whole lot? I don't know how Wagner pulled that off. But for most of us, if we want to be more original, we have to generate more ideas. The Warby Parker founders, when they were trying to name their company,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 這傢伙是如何在沒有做很多事情的情況下變得偉大的？我不知道瓦格納是如何做到這一點的。但對我們大多數人來說，如果我們想要更原創，我們必須產生更多的想法。 Warby Parker 的創辦人在嘗試為他們的公司命名時，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: they needed something sophisticated, unique, with no negative associations to build a retail brand, and they tested over 2,000 possibilities before they finally put together Warby and Parker. So if you put all this together, what you see is that originals are not that different from the rest of us.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 他們需要一些複雜、獨特、沒有負面聯想的東西來打造一個零售品牌，他們測試了 2,000 多種可能性，然後最終將 Warby 和 Parker 組合在一起。因此，如果你把所有這些放在一起，你會發現原創者與我們其他人並沒有太大不同。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: They feel fear and doubt. They procrastinate. They have bad ideas. And sometimes, it's not in spite of those qualities but because of them that they succeed. So when you see those things, don't make the same mistake I did. Don't write them off. And when that's you, don't count yourself out either.
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 他們感到恐懼和懷疑。他們拖延。他們有壞主意。有時，他們的成功並不是因為這些特質，而是因為它們。所以當你看到這些東西時，不要犯我犯過的同樣的錯誤。不要註銷它們。當你就是這樣的時候，也不要認為自己被排除在外。

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: Know that being quick to start but slow to finish can boost your creativity, that you can motivate yourself by doubting your ideas and embracing the fear of failing to try, and that you need a lot of bad ideas in order to get a few good ones. Look, being original is not easy,
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 要知道，快速開始但緩慢完成可以提高你的創造力，你可以透過懷疑自己的想法和擁抱嘗試失敗的恐懼來激勵自己，並且你需要很多糟糕的想法才能得到一些好想法。看來，原創並不容易，

kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.en: but I have no doubt about this: it's the best way to improve the world around us. Notifications alt+T
kelly_mcgonigal_how_to_make_stress_your_.zh: 但我對此毫不懷疑：這是改善周遭世界的最佳方式。通知 alt+T

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: I have a confession to make. But first, I want you to make a little confession to me. In the past year, I want you to just raise your hand if you've experienced relatively little stress. How about a moderate amount of stress? Who has experienced a lot of stress? But that is not my confession.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我有件事要坦白。但首先，我想讓你向我坦白一點。在過去的一年裡，如果你經歷的壓力相對較小，我希望你舉手。適度的壓力怎麼樣？誰經歷過很大的壓力？但這不是我的坦白。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: My confession is this: I am a health psychologist, and my mission is to help people be happier and healthier. But I fear that something I've been teaching for the last 10 years is doing more harm than good, and it has to do with stress. For years I've been telling people, stress makes you sick.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我的坦白是：我是一名健康心理學家，我的使命是幫助人們變得更快樂、更健康。但我擔心過去十年我一直在教授的東西弊大於利，而且它與壓力有關。多年來我一直告訴人們，壓力會讓人生病。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: It increases the risk of everything from the common cold to cardiovascular disease. Basically, I've turned stress into the enemy. But I have changed my mind about stress, and today, I want to change yours. Let me start with the study that made me rethink my whole approach to stress.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 它會增加從普通感冒到心血管疾病等各種疾病的風險。基本上，我已經把壓力變成敵人了。但我改變了對壓力的看法，今天，我想改變你的看法。讓我從一個讓我重新思考我應對壓力的方法的研究開始。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: This study tracked 30,000 adults in the United States for eight years, and they started by asking people, "How much stress have you experienced in the last year?" They also asked, "Do you believe that stress is harmful for your health?" And then they used public death records to find out who died.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 這項研究對美國 30,000 名成年人進行了長達八年的追踪，他們首先詢問人們「去年你經歷了多少壓力？」他們還問：「你認為壓力對你的健康有害嗎？」然後他們利用公開的死亡記錄來找出死亡者。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: Some bad news first. People who experienced a lot of stress in the previous year had a 43 percent increased risk of dying. But that was only true for the people who also believed that stress is harmful for your health. People who experienced a lot of stress but did not view stress as harmful
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 首先是一些壞消息。去年經歷過巨大壓力的人死亡風險增加 43%。但這僅適用於那些也相信壓力有害健康的人。經歷過很大壓力但不認為壓力有​​害的人

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: were no more likely to die. In fact, they had the lowest risk of dying of anyone in the study, including people who had relatively little stress. Now the researchers estimated that over the eight years they were tracking deaths, 182,000 Americans died prematurely, not from stress,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 不再有可能死亡。事實上，在研究中，包括壓力相對較小的人在內，他們的死亡風險最低。現在，研究人員估計，在他們追蹤死亡情況的八年裡，有 182,000 名美國人過早死亡，而不是因為壓力，

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: but from the belief that stress is bad for you. That is over 20,000 deaths a year. Now, if that estimate is correct, that would make believing stress is bad for you the 15th largest cause of death in the United States last year, killing more people than skin cancer, HIV/AIDS and homicide.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 但因為相信壓力對你有害。也就是說，每年有超過 2 萬人死亡。現在，如果這個估計是正確的，那麼相信壓力對你不利將成為去年美國第 15 大死因，比皮膚癌、愛滋病毒/愛滋病和兇殺案造成的死亡人數還要多。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: You can see why this study freaked me out. Here I've been spending so much energy telling people stress is bad for your health. So this study got me wondering: Can changing how you think about stress make you healthier? And here the science says yes. When you change your mind about stress,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 你可以明白為什麼這項研究讓我害怕了。在這裡，我花了很多精力告訴人們壓力對健康有害。所以這項研究讓我想知道：改變你對壓力的看法能讓你更健康嗎？科學說是的。當你改變對壓力的看法時，

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: you can change your body's response to stress. Now to explain how this works, I want you all to pretend that you are participants in a study designed to stress you out. It's called the social stress test. You come into the laboratory, and you're told you have to give
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 您可以改變身體對壓力的反應。現在為了解釋這是如何運作的，我希望大家假裝自己是一項旨在給你壓力的研究的參與者。這就是所謂的社會壓力測試。你進入實驗室，你被告知你必須給予

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: a five-minute impromptu speech on your personal weaknesses to a panel of expert evaluators sitting right in front of you, and to make sure you feel the pressure, there are bright lights and a camera in your face, kind of like this. And the evaluators have been trained
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 對坐在你面前的專家評估小組進行五分鐘的即興演講，介紹你的個人弱點，並確保你感受到壓力，有明亮的燈光和相機在你的臉上，有點像這樣。且評估人員都經過培訓

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: to give you discouraging, non-verbal feedback, Now that you're sufficiently demoralized, time for part two: a math test. And unbeknownst to you, the experimenter has been trained to harass you during it. Now we're going to all do this together. It's going to be fun.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 給你令人沮喪的非語言回饋，現在你已經士氣低落了，是時候進行第二部分了：數學測驗。而你不知道的是，實驗者已經接受過訓練，可以在實驗過程中騷擾你。現在我們要一起做這件事。這會很有趣。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: I want you all to count backwards from 996 in increments of seven. You're going to do this out loud, as fast as you can, starting with 996. (Audience counting) Go faster. Faster please. You're going too slow. (Audience counting) Stop. Stop, stop, stop. That guy made a mistake.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我希望大家從 996 開始倒數，每次遞增 7。你要盡可能快地大聲說出這一點，從 996 開始。 （觀眾數）走得更快。請快點。你走得太慢了。 （數觀眾）停止。停下來，停下來，停下來。那傢伙犯了一個錯誤。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: We are going to have to start all over again. You're not very good at this, are you? Okay, so you get the idea. If you were actually in this study, you'd probably be a little stressed out. Your heart might be pounding, you might be breathing faster, maybe breaking out into a sweat.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 我們將不得不重新開始。你不太擅長這個，是嗎？好的，所以你明白了。如果你真的參與這項研究，你可能會有點壓力。你的心跳可能會加快，呼吸可能會加快，甚至可能會出汗。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: And normally, we interpret these physical changes as anxiety or signs that we aren't coping very well with the pressure. But what if you viewed them instead as signs that your body was energized, was preparing you to meet this challenge? Now that is exactly what participants were told
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 通常，我們將這些身體變化解釋為焦慮或我們不能很好地應對壓力的跡象。但是，如果您將它們視為您的身體充滿活力、正在準備迎接這項挑戰的跡象，該怎麼辦？現在這正是參與者被告知的

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: in a study conducted at Harvard University. Before they went through the social stress test, they were taught to rethink their stress response as helpful. That pounding heart is preparing you for action. If you're breathing faster, it's no problem. It's getting more oxygen to your brain.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 在哈佛大學進行的一項研究中。在他們進行社交壓力測試之前，他們被教導要重新考慮他們的壓力反應是否有幫助。那顆劇烈跳動的心正在為你的行動做好準備。如果你呼吸加快，那就沒問題。它為你的大腦提供更多的氧氣。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: And participants who learned to view the stress response as helpful for their performance, well, they were less stressed out, less anxious, more confident, but the most fascinating finding to me was how their physical stress response changed. Now, in a typical stress response,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 那些學會將壓力反應視為對他們的表現有幫助的參與者，他們的壓力減輕了，焦慮減少了，更加自信了，但對我來說最有趣的發現是他們的身體壓力反應如何改變。現在，在典型的壓力反應中​​，

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: your heart rate goes up, and your blood vessels constrict like this. And this is one of the reasons that chronic stress is sometimes associated with cardiovascular disease. It's not really healthy to be in this state all the time. But in the study,
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 你的心率會上升，你的血管會這樣收縮。這就是為什麼慢性壓力有時與心血管疾病相關的原因之一。一直處於這種狀態並不健康。但在研究中，

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: when participants viewed their stress response as helpful, their blood vessels stayed relaxed like this. Their heart was still pounding, but this is a much healthier cardiovascular profile. It actually looks a lot like what happens in moments of joy and courage.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 當參與者認為他們的壓力反應有幫助時，他們的血管就會像這樣保持放鬆。他們的心臟仍在跳動，但這是一個更健康的心血管狀況。它實際上看起來很像在歡樂和勇氣時刻發生的事情。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: Over a lifetime of stressful experiences, this one biological change could be the difference between a stress-induced heart attack at age 50 and living well into your 90s. And this is really what the new science of stress reveals, that how you think about stress matters.
matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.zh: 在一生的壓力經歷中，這項生物變化可能是 50 歲時因壓力引起的心臟病發作和活到 90 多歲時的區別。這確實是關於壓力的新科學所揭示的，你如何看待壓力很重要。

matt_cutts_try_something_new_for_30_days.en: So my goal as a health psychologist has changed. I no longer want to get rid of your stress. I want to make you better at stress. And we just did a little intervention. If you raised your hand and said you'd had a lot of stress in the last year, we could have saved your life,
